name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads dynamic consulting overview content, highlights AI focus areas, and routes users to key actions like consultations, plans, assessment, training, events, and resources using homepage APIs.

core_features: Load homepage configuration (summary text, focus areas, and primary call-to-actions) via getHomePageContent and expose these to child components.

Display focus areas for AI and business strategy consulting and allow users to select a focus area to refine visible actions or jump to relevant sections, with the selected focus reflected in the URL.
Render a dynamic list of primary calls-to-action based on getHomePageContent.primaryCtas and route each CTA to its corresponding page using the defined navigation rules.

Fetch and display a limited list of featured case studies via getFeaturedCaseStudies with maxItems derived from URL state, and provide navigation to the case studies listing with optional highlighting of a selected case study.

Fetch and display a limited list of recent blog posts via getRecentBlogPosts with maxItems derived from URL state, and provide navigation to the blog listing with optional highlighting of a selected post.
Provide quick navigation entry points to Project Estimator, Resources (Blog and Events), Training, and Consultation flows through dynamically defined CTAs and static header/footer navigation links.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive on Home and understand available services
1) User navigates to index.html. 2) home-page-controller triggers getHomePageContent, getFeaturedCaseStudies, and getRecentBlogPosts using any limits in the URL. 3) hero-summary-and-cta-panel shows summary and primary CTAs from getHomePageContent. 4) focus-areas-list shows focusAreas, allowing the user to click one to set the focus selection and update the URL. 5) User identifies a relevant CTA (e.g., assessment, consultation, training) and clicks it to navigate to the appropriate page.

Workflow: Navigate to a specific focus area from an external link
1) User follows a link to index.html?focusKey={focusKey}. 2) home-page-controller reads focusKey from the URL. 3) After getHomePageContent resolves, focus-areas-list pre-selects the focus area matching focusKey if present. 4) hero-summary-and-cta-panel can optionally emphasize CTAs relevant to the selected focus. 5) User chooses a CTA or scrolls to case studies or blog posts to continue.

Workflow: Explore featured case studies from Home
1) User scrolls to the featured-case-studies-strip. 2) featured-case-studies-strip has already received CaseStudy data from getFeaturedCaseStudies(maxItems) where maxItems is taken from URL parameter featuredCaseStudiesLimit if present. 3) User clicks an individual featured case study item. 4) The component navigates to case_studies.html with URL parameter highlightCaseStudyId set to the clicked CaseStudy.id so the case studies page can highlight or scroll to it. 5) Alternatively, the user clicks a 'view all' action within the strip, which navigates to case_studies.html without extra parameters.

Workflow: Explore recent blog insights from Home
1) User scrolls to the recent-blog-posts-strip. 2) recent-blog-posts-strip receives BlogPost data from getRecentBlogPosts(maxItems) where maxItems is taken from URL parameter recentPostsLimit if present. 3) User clicks a blog post item. 4) The component navigates to blog.html with URL parameter highlightPostId set to the clicked BlogPost.id so the blog page can highlight or pre-select it. 5) Alternatively, user clicks a 'view all resources' or 'view all posts' action that navigates to blog.html without extra parameters.

Workflow: Use Home as a router to key flows (consultation, plans, assessment, training, events, estimator)
1) User lands on index.html, with hero-summary-and-cta-panel showing CTAs loaded from getHomePageContent.primaryCtas. 2) User finds the desired action within the CTA list (e.g., a CTA whose targetPage corresponds to consultation, plans, assessment, training, events, or estimator). 3) User clicks the CTA; hero-summary-and-cta-panel uses the CTA’s key/targetPage to determine the correct URL. 4) The browser navigates to the mapped page (e.g., book_consultation.html, plans.html, assessment.html, training.html, events.html with dateRangeKey=upcoming, blog.html, estimator.html). 5) User continues the more detailed flow on the destination page.

Workflow: Return to Home from other sections and preserve section focus
1) User is on another page and returns to Home via a header or footer 'Home' link, optionally with a query parameter such as section=resources or focusKey={focusKey}. 2) home-page-controller reads section and focusKey from the URL. 3) If section is set (e.g., 'resources', 'case_studies', 'blog'), the page automatically scrolls or focuses the corresponding Home section. 4) If focusKey is set, focus-areas-list activates that focus area. 5) User resumes navigation using CTAs or section links.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load (index.html loaded in browser).
- Behavior: home-page-controller reads URL parameters (focusKey, featuredCaseStudiesLimit, recentPostsLimit, section). It calls getHomePageContent (no parameters), getFeaturedCaseStudies(maxItems) with maxItems derived from featuredCaseStudiesLimit when present, and getRecentBlogPosts(maxItems) with maxItems derived from recentPostsLimit when present. It stores results in page state and passes them as props to child components.

Interaction: Selecting a focus area
- Trigger: User clicks on an item in focus-areas-list representing one focus area from getHomePageContent.focusAreas.
- Behavior: The component sets the selected focus key in local component state and notifies home-page-controller. The controller updates the browser URL to index.html?focusKey={selectedKey}[&otherExistingParams], preserving other parameters. The UI may optionally filter or visually emphasize CTAs whose configuration matches the selected focus.

Interaction: Clicking a primary CTA
- Trigger: User clicks a CTA button or link in hero-summary-and-cta-panel, bound to an item in getHomePageContent.primaryCtas.
- Behavior: The component reads the clicked CTA’s key and targetPage. It uses a routing table in the client logic to map targetPage or key to a concrete URL (e.g., consultation-related key → book_consultation.html). For event/webinar CTAs, the component appends dateRangeKey=upcoming to events.html. The browser navigates to the resulting URL.

Interaction: Navigating to featured case studies
- Trigger: User clicks an individual featured case study item in featured-case-studies-strip.
- Behavior: The component accesses the bound CaseStudy.id from the clicked item and performs navigation to case_studies.html?highlightCaseStudyId={id}. This allows the case studies page to highlight the corresponding case study.
- Alternative Trigger: User clicks a 'view all case studies' action within the same component.
- Alternative Behavior: Navigate to case_studies.html without highlightCaseStudyId.

Interaction: Navigating to recent blog posts
- Trigger: User clicks an individual blog post item in recent-blog-posts-strip.
- Behavior: The component accesses the bound BlogPost.id from the clicked item and performs navigation to blog.html?highlightPostId={id}. This allows the blog page to emphasize the selected post.
- Alternative Trigger: User clicks a 'view all resources' or 'view all posts' action in the same component.
- Alternative Behavior: Navigate to blog.html without additional parameters.

Interaction: Section deep-linking and automatic scrolling
- Trigger: User lands on index.html with URL parameter section set (for example, 'resources', 'case_studies', or 'blog').
- Behavior: After data load, home-page-controller inspects section and instructs the corresponding component (e.g., featured-case-studies-strip or recent-blog-posts-strip) to scroll into view or take focus. Changes to section (e.g., via internal navigation) are synchronized back to the URL.

Interaction: Header and footer navigation
- Trigger: User clicks header or footer links such as 'Home', 'Book Consultation', 'Plans & Packages', 'Training', 'Events & Webinars', 'Resources', 'Project Estimator', 'Saved Items', 'About', or footer 'Contact'.
- Behavior: The click handler performs straightforward navigation to the corresponding HTML file, adding any required query parameters described in the navigation information (e.g., events.html?dateRangeKey=upcoming). No data from page interfaces is mutated by these actions.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not require server-side URL parameters but uses client-side URL query parameters to maintain and restore state. The following parameters are read and written by the front-end:
- focusKey: Optional. Represents the key of a selected focus area (matching one of focusAreas[].key from getHomePageContent). When present on load, focus-areas-list will mark this focus area as active, and hero-summary-and-cta-panel may emphasize relevant CTAs. When the user changes focus via the UI, this parameter is updated in the URL while preserving other existing parameters.
- featuredCaseStudiesLimit: Optional. Numeric. When present, home-page-controller uses its value as maxItems for getFeaturedCaseStudies. If absent, the implementation uses an internal default determined outside this specification. Changing this parameter (e.g., via a control to see more or fewer items) causes the page to update the URL and re-fetch featured case studies.
- recentPostsLimit: Optional. Numeric. When present, home-page-controller uses its value as maxItems for getRecentBlogPosts. If absent, an internal implementation default is used. Updating this parameter (e.g., via a 'load more recent posts' control that adjusts the limit) updates the URL and triggers a re-fetch.
- section: Optional. String. Indicates which logical section should be focused or scrolled into view on page load. Expected values correspond to internal section identifiers such as 'hero', 'focus', 'case_studies', 'blog', or 'resources'. When set, once data is loaded, the controller scrolls the corresponding component into view. Internal navigation within the page (e.g., clicking a 'Jump to resources' link) updates this parameter.
These parameters are manipulated solely through URL updates (history.pushState / history.replaceState or equivalent) and are not stored anywhere else. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used for view state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading using getHomePageContent, getFeaturedCaseStudies, and getRecentBlogPosts; interprets URL parameters for state (focusKey, featuredCaseStudiesLimit, recentPostsLimit, section); distributes loaded data and state to child components; and handles intra-page navigation (e.g., scrolling sections into view).
event_handlers: onLoad: Read URL parameters (focusKey, featuredCaseStudiesLimit, recentPostsLimit, section), call getHomePageContent(), call getFeaturedCaseStudies(maxItems) using featuredCaseStudiesLimit when present, call getRecentBlogPosts(maxItems) using recentPostsLimit when present, then render child components with the resulting data.

onFocusKeyChange: Invoked when focus-areas-list reports a change. Updates the URL query parameter focusKey while preserving other parameters and notifies hero-summary-and-cta-panel of the active focus.

onFeaturedLimitChange: Invoked when featured-case-studies-strip requests a new limit (e.g., user action to see more or fewer items). Updates featuredCaseStudiesLimit in the URL and re-invokes getFeaturedCaseStudies with the new maxItems.
onRecentPostsLimitChange: Invoked when recent-blog-posts-strip requests a new limit. Updates recentPostsLimit in the URL and re-invokes getRecentBlogPosts with the new maxItems.
onSectionChange: Invoked when internal navigation wants to focus a particular section. Updates section in the URL and scrolls the corresponding component into view.

onDataLoadComplete: After all data is loaded, checks the section parameter and scrolls the appropriate section (hero-summary-and-cta-panel, focus-areas-list, featured-case-studies-strip, recent-blog-posts-strip) into view.

components.functionality: Displays the main summary text of the consulting website and renders the primary call-to-action items returned by getHomePageContent.primaryCtas. It routes users to key flows according to CTA configuration and can adjust emphasis based on the selected focus area.
event_handlers: onClickCta: Triggered when the user clicks a CTA. Uses the CTA’s key and/or targetPage from primaryCtas to compute the navigation target. Examples of routing logic include mapping a consultation-related CTA to book_consultation.html, a plans-related CTA to plans.html, an assessment CTA to assessment.html, a training CTA to training.html, an events/webinars CTA to events.html with dateRangeKey=upcoming, a resources CTA to blog.html, and a project estimator CTA to estimator.html. Then performs browser navigation to the computed URL.
onFocusKeyUpdated: Triggered when the active focusKey changes. May adjust ordering or styling of CTAs to prioritize ones relevant to the selected focus area, without changing underlying data.

components.functionality: Presents the list of AI-related focus areas returned by getHomePageContent.focusAreas and allows users to select one. The selected focus area becomes part of the Home page state and is written to the URL.
event_handlers: onClickFocusArea: Triggered when the user selects a focus area item. Reads the associated focusAreas[].key and notifies home-page-controller via onFocusKeyChange so the key is stored in state and written into the URL as focusKey.
onInitialRender: If focusKey from the URL matches one of the focusAreas[].key values, marks that item as selected to reflect the current state.

components.functionality: Shows a horizontal or vertical strip of featured case studies returned by getFeaturedCaseStudies, limited by the maxItems parameter. Provides navigation to the Case Studies listing and highlights specific items via URL state when requested by other sections.
data_binding: section state (to know when to scroll into view)
event_handlers: onClickCaseStudyItem: Triggered when a user clicks an individual case study entry. Reads the bound CaseStudy.id and triggers navigation to case_studies.html?highlightCaseStudyId={id}.

onClickViewAllCaseStudies: Triggered when the user chooses to view all case studies from this strip (e.g., a dedicated 'view all' action). Navigates to case_studies.html without additional parameters.

onChangeVisibleItemCount: Optional handler when a user chooses to display more or fewer featured case studies. Computes a new maxItems value, notifies home-page-controller via onFeaturedLimitChange, which updates featuredCaseStudiesLimit in the URL and re-fetches data.
onSectionFocusRequested: When home-page-controller indicates that section=case_studies (or equivalent) is active, scrolls this component into view and may visually emphasize it.

components.functionality: Displays a list or strip of the most recent blog posts returned by getRecentBlogPosts, respecting a dynamic limit. Enables navigation to the general blog listing and highlights a selected post via URL parameters on the destination page.
data_binding: section state (to know when to scroll into view)
event_handlers: onClickBlogPostItem: Triggered when a user clicks a blog post entry. Reads the bound BlogPost.id and navigates to blog.html?highlightPostId={id}.
onClickViewAllPosts: Triggered when a user selects an action to see the full blog/resources section. Navigates to blog.html.

onChangeVisiblePostCount: Optional handler when the user opts to load more or fewer posts. Computes a new maxItems value, notifies home-page-controller via onRecentPostsLimitChange, which updates recentPostsLimit in the URL and re-fetches data.
onSectionFocusRequested: When home-page-controller indicates that section=blog or section=resources is active, scrolls this component into view and may visually emphasize it.

components.functionality: Represents the header or a dedicated navigation bar on the Home page that provides quick links to other core pages, consistent with the outgoing navigation specification. While it does not use the page’s data interfaces, it is part of the Home page’s functional structure.
data_binding: None (links are based on global site navigation configuration, not on getHomePageContent or other interfaces)
event_handlers: onClickHome: Navigates to index.html without altering existing query parameters unless explicitly required.
onClickBookConsultation: Navigates directly to book_consultation.html.
onClickPlansAndPackages: Navigates to plans.html.
onClickTraining: Navigates to training.html.
onClickEventsAndWebinars: Navigates to events.html with query parameter dateRangeKey=upcoming.
onClickResources: Navigates to blog.html.
onClickProjectEstimator: Navigates to estimator.html.
onClickSavedItems: Navigates to saved_items.html.
onClickAbout: Navigates to about.html.
onClickFooterContact: Navigates to contact.html from footer.

components.functionality: Acts as a compact overview section that ties together the recent-blog-posts-strip and any event-related CTA from getHomePageContent.primaryCtas, positioning the Home page as an entry point to the broader resources ecosystem (blog and events).
data_binding: getHomePageContent.primaryCtas (filtered to any resources- or events-related CTAs)
BlogPost[] from getRecentBlogPosts (for summarizing recent content)
event_handlers: onClickResourcesCta: When a resources-related CTA from primaryCtas is clicked, navigates to blog.html.
onClickEventsCta: When an events/webinars-related CTA from primaryCtas is clicked, navigates to events.html with dateRangeKey=upcoming.

onSectionLinkClick: If the panel exposes an internal 'Jump to recent posts' or similar link, notifies home-page-controller via onSectionChange so section is updated (e.g., to 'blog') in the URL and the recent-blog-posts-strip is scrolled into view.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-down, section-based layout anchored by a dark, two-column hero at the top. At the very top is a horizontal navigation/header band (quick-navigation-links-bar) spanning full width. Immediately beneath it, the hero-summary-and-cta-panel uses a left-heavy text column and a right portrait/visual column. Below the hero, the content flows vertically in clearly separated bands: focus-areas-list (AI focus areas) first, then featured-case-studies-strip, then recent-blog-posts-strip, and finally a strong resources-teaser-panel near the bottom as a closing CTA. Each section has its own internal hierarchy but respects the linear, editorial flow established in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-down, section-based layout anchored by a dark, two-column hero at the top. At the very top is a horizontal navigation/header band (quick-navigation-links-bar) spanning full width. Immediately beneath it, the hero-summary-and-cta-panel uses a left-heavy text column and a right portrait/visual column. Below the hero, the content flows vertically in clearly separated bands: focus-areas-list (AI focus areas) first, then featured-case-studies-strip, then recent-blog-posts-strip, and finally a strong resources-teaser-panel near the bottom as a closing CTA. Each section has its own internal hierarchy but respects the linear, editorial flow established in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The layout uses a centered max-width container (~1120px) on a 12-column grid, with 24–32px side padding inside the viewport. In the hero, about 60–65% of the width is reserved for the left text column and 35–40% for the right portrait/interaction column. The navigation bar is shallow (roughly 64–80px tall) and full-width but visually light compared to the hero. Below the hero, each main section (focus areas, case studies, posts, resources) occupies the full container width but with clear internal margins and gutters. Vertical spacing between major sections is generous (roughly 64–96px), ensuring that no band feels cramped.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low and airy. The hero uses large type with substantial line spacing, a single paragraph of supporting copy, and a small cluster of buttons, all separated by 16–32px vertical gaps. Subsequent sections use compact headings and short descriptions with a small set of items per row (e.g., 3–4 cards), each card having ample padding and whitespace. There are no dense multi-column text blocks; instead, information is broken into concise, scannable segments with clear breathing room around and between components, aligned to an 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the navigation at the top, but the primary attention is pulled quickly to the bold white hero headline on the dark background. The eye then moves slightly up to register the smaller context line, then down through the supporting paragraph and CTA row. From there, the gaze travels horizontally to the right-hand portrait/visual column and any chat or interactive widget anchored in that area. As the user scrolls, the path becomes a vertical rhythm of bands: focus areas (what you do), then proof via case studies, then thought leadership via recent posts, and finally a strong prompt into the resources hub. Section headings and subtle background shifts guide the eye, creating a controlled Z-pattern across the hero and a clean vertical scan down the rest of the page.

layout.overall_description: The homepage presents as a modern, dark-themed hero sitting under a slim, sticky navigation bar, all centered within a wide but controlled content column. At the very top, a horizontal nav band stretches edge-to-edge on the viewport, with the brand at the left, key links across the center-right, and small social icons at the far right. Directly beneath, the hero fills most of the initial viewport height: on the left, stacked text elements (context label, large headline, short paragraph, and a horizontal row of primary CTAs) occupy roughly two-thirds of the width; on the right, a tall portrait of the consultant and any AI-chat prompt fills the remaining third, visually anchoring the hero. As you scroll down, the background lightens slightly and sections are arranged in wide horizontal slices: first a focus-areas section with a heading and a row of neatly spaced cards describing AI and strategy domains; next, a band of featured case studies with a few large, clickable tiles; below that, a streamlined strip of recent blog posts; and finally, a high-impact resources panel that stretches across the width with a contrasting tone and a bold call to explore resources. Generous vertical spacing between these bands and consistent side margins keep the page feeling calm and premium, while the hierarchy naturally leads users from understanding the value proposition to exploring offerings, proof, insights, and deeper resources.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-page-controller acts as the structural frame for the entire viewport. It anchors a full-width background that is darkest at the top hero section and subtly lightens or shifts tone for the content bands below, maintaining contrast between sections. Inside, it centers a max-width content container (~1120px) on large screens, with equal left and right padding (24–32px) and 16px gutters between grid columns. It arranges all other components in a single vertical sequence: at the top sits the quick-navigation-links-bar (header band), followed by the hero-summary-and-cta-panel occupying essentially the first viewport height, then the focus-areas-list, featured-case-studies-strip, recent-blog-posts-strip, and finally the resources-teaser-panel near the bottom. Vertical spacing between each component is controlled by the controller, using roughly 64–96px margins to separate each major section. On scroll, the controller ensures that the navigation bar remains fixed at the top, while the other sections scroll beneath, preserving the dark, editorial feel of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the global grid and vertical stacking; manages full-width background transitions between sections; keeps the navigation/header component anchored at the top while allowing all content sections to scroll naturally beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-navigation-links-bar is positioned at the very top of the page as a full-width horizontal band, approximately 64–80px tall. It sits directly against the top edge of the viewport with no gap above and spans the entire width, but its contents are constrained to the central max-width container. Inside that container, the brand/wordmark is aligned to the far left, occupying roughly the first 3 grid columns. To the right, in the center portion (about 5–6 columns), a horizontal row of primary navigation links is evenly spaced, aligned vertically to the middle of the bar. On the far right (last 3–4 columns), small, minimal social icons and possibly a ‘Contact’ or external link cluster are right-aligned. The bar uses a slightly lighter dark tone than the hero background or a subtle divider line at the bottom to distinguish it, while remaining visually understated so the hero below still dominates. On scroll, this bar remains fixed at the top of the viewport, with a slight drop shadow to visually lift it above the hero and subsequent content bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport once loaded; full-width horizontal band with a fixed height; other page components scroll beneath it. On smaller screens, the central navigation condenses into a hamburger or overflow menu while preserving the left brand and right utility alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The hero-summary-and-cta-panel occupies the first main content section directly beneath the navigation bar and fills almost the entire initial viewport height on desktop. It spans the full width of the central container and is laid out as a two-column grid. The left column takes approximately 60–65% of the width (about 7–8 grid columns) and contains a vertical stack of text elements: at the top, a small all-caps context label is aligned left with generous space above (24–32px from the bottom of the nav bar); below it, a large multi-line H1 headline stretches over two to three lines, taking up roughly one-third of the viewport height. Beneath the headline, a concise supporting paragraph runs across the same column width, set with comfortable line spacing and width for readability. Under the paragraph, a horizontal row of 2–3 CTAs (buttons and possibly a ghost/secondary style) is aligned to the left and spaced with 16–24px gaps between buttons; this CTA row sits about 24–32px beneath the paragraph. The right column occupies the remaining 35–40% of width (about 4–5 grid columns) and is vertically centered relative to the left content. It features a large rectangular or lightly rounded portrait of the consultant that extends from just below the nav bar down towards the lower third of the hero section. At the bottom-right corner of this portrait column, a small floating chat or ‘Ask me about AI’ widget sits slightly inset from the container edges (16–24px from the right and bottom of the hero). The entire hero background is a deep charcoal, with the text in high-contrast white and CTAs using a single, restrained accent color. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to a single column where the text stack appears first and the portrait shifts below, still centered, with the chat widget anchoring to the bottom-right of the visible hero area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies most of the initial viewport height, flexing vertically to keep the portrait and text roughly centered; the right-column chat widget behaves like a floating element within the hero, anchored to the bottom-right of the hero area. Below medium breakpoints, the two columns stack and the hero height becomes content-driven while maintaining generous top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focus-areas-list sits directly beneath the hero, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 80–96px to clearly mark a new section. It spans the full width of the central container but remains horizontally centered with the same left/right padding as the hero. At the top of this section, a left-aligned section label or small eyebrow (e.g., ‘Focus Areas’) appears with moderate spacing below it (16px), followed by a slightly larger H2-style heading clarifying the AI/business strategy focus, occupying about 60% of the container width. Below the heading, an optional one-line description may span up to 50–60% of the width, keeping text compact. The core of the section is a grid of focus area items presented as 3–4 cards in a row on desktop: each card occupies an equal fraction of the width (roughly 3–4 grid columns), with 24–32px horizontal gutters between them. Each card has comfortable internal padding (16–24px), a concise title, a 2–3 line description, and possibly a small icon aligned at the top-left. The cards sit on a slightly lighter dark or mid-tone background than the hero, creating a subtle contrast without breaking the overall dark-mode aesthetic. Vertical spacing between the heading and the grid is about 32px, and between card rows (if multiple rows) about 24px. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to two cards per row or one per row, increasing vertical length but preserving wide margins and card spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard stacked section in the vertical flow, expanding vertically as more focus items are present. The card grid is responsive, adjusting the number of columns based on viewport width while maintaining consistent gutters and card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-case-studies-strip appears below the focus-areas-list with a clear vertical separation of about 64–80px. It spans the central container width and is treated visually as a horizontal strip that emphasizes proof and outcomes. At the top-left of the strip, a section heading (‘Featured Case Studies’ or similar) is aligned with the container edge, with a brief one-line subtitle optionally aligned beneath it. To the right of the heading or aligned to the far right edge, a small text link or button (‘View all case studies’) is pinned, creating a top row that runs across the full width. Beneath this header row, 2–3 prominent case study tiles are arranged horizontally. Each tile occupies about 3–4 grid columns and uses a card layout with a thumbnail or abstract visual at the top, followed by a short title and a 1–2 line result-oriented summary. The strip may use a slightly darker or subtly tinted background band compared to the focus-areas section to visually differentiate it, but the density remains low with 24–32px padding above and below the tiles within the strip. The tiles have more visual weight than the focus area cards, potentially slightly taller with bolder type to emphasize their importance. On smaller screens, the tiles either stack vertically or become horizontally scrollable cards within the same strip, preserving the feeling of a horizontal content band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a horizontally oriented content band, fixed in the vertical flow. The case study tiles may optionally support horizontal scrolling on narrow viewports, but on desktop they are static and aligned in a row within the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-blog-posts-strip is positioned beneath the featured-case-studies-strip, separated by roughly 64–80px of vertical whitespace. It continues the full-width, centered-container pattern but with slightly lighter visual weight than the case studies. At the top, a left-aligned section heading (‘Latest Insights’ or ‘Recent Posts’) sits with modest spacing above and below (16–24px), potentially accompanied by a narrow description line that spans no more than half the container width. Directly beneath, 3 blog post cards are laid out in a horizontal row on desktop, each card occupying about 3–4 grid columns with consistent 24px gutters. Each card includes a smaller thumbnail or category label at the top, a concise title, a short 1–2 line excerpt, and meta information (date, reading time) in a low-emphasis style. The cards are slightly more compact than the case study tiles, with less height and tighter internal spacing, to avoid overwhelming the page. The background of this section may either match the focus-areas section or be a subtle variant, but it remains clean and minimally styled, allowing the hero and resources panel to carry the strongest personalities. On mobile, these blog cards stack vertically with generous vertical spacing (24–32px) between them, maintaining clarity and scan-ability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section in the vertical flow. The card layout is responsive, transitioning from a 3-column row on desktop to a single-column stack on narrow viewports while keeping consistent padding and gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-teaser-panel sits near the bottom of the homepage, following the recent-blog-posts-strip with a pronounced vertical gap of about 80–96px to signal a distinct, high-impact closing section. It spans the full width of the central container but may extend with a slightly different background tone across the full viewport width, visually reading as a band that caps the page. Within the container, the content is arranged in a broadly two-column feel: on the left (roughly 60% width), a bold, left-aligned heading invites users to explore resources (e.g., ‘Deep-dive Resources’), accompanied by a short supporting paragraph that explains what they’ll find (guides, tools, assessments). On the right (about 40% width), a compact cluster of key resource links or a primary button (‘Browse Resources’) with 1–2 secondary actions (e.g., ‘Download a sample’, ‘Watch a training’) is vertically centered relative to the left text. The CTAs are stacked or arranged in a vertical mini-stack on the right, with 12–16px gaps, ensuring clear tap targets and a strong call to action. The background of this panel is slightly higher contrast than the preceding content strips—either deeper dark or an accent tint—to elevate its prominence while remaining harmonious with the overall dark, minimalist aesthetic. Vertical padding inside the panel is generous (48–64px top and bottom), creating a sense of an intentional, standalone CTA band before any footer elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a concluding CTA band in the vertical sequence. On smaller screens, the left and right content stacks into a single column: heading and paragraph appear first, followed by the CTA cluster centered or left-aligned, with increased vertical spacing to maintain emphasis and clarity.

name: Book Consultation
title: Book Consultation
description: Page for configuring and submitting an AI consulting or strategy consultation request, using dynamic form options and contextual prefill from other pages.

core_features: Load dynamic form options (service types, durations, industries, budget ranges) via getConsultationFormOptions and bind them to the booking form inputs.
Apply contextual prefill to the form using getConsultationPrefill when sourceType and sourceReferenceId URL parameters are present, merging these with any explicit URL parameters.

Allow the user to configure a consultation request: select service type and duration, choose industry, pick a date and time, specify participant count and budget range, and enter contact details and notes.
Submit the configured consultation request through submitConsultationBooking and handle the returned ConsultationBooking object to show submission status and confirmation details.
Support navigation back to the home page (index.html) and to the plans and packages page (plans.html) from the post-submission confirmation state.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Direct booking from navigation
1) User opens book_consultation.html via header or footer navigation without additional URL parameters.
2) On page load, getConsultationFormOptions is called to retrieve selectable options for service types, durations, industries, and budget ranges.
3) The page initializes an empty ConsultationBooking draft state using those options (implementation-defined defaults, such as selecting a default service type and duration if desired).
4) User selects a service type and duration from dropdowns bound to the options returned by getConsultationFormOptions.
5) User selects an industry from the dynamically loaded industry options.
6) User picks a desired date and time using a date/time picker component bound to the scheduledStart field.
7) User optionally enters participants count, selects a budget range from dynamic options, and fills contact name, email, company name, and notes.
8) User submits the form. Client-side validation runs (e.g., required fields not empty, email format checks). If validation passes, submitConsultationBooking is called with the current form values and sourceType/sourceReferenceId passed as empty or implementation-defined values.
9) While the request is in progress, the form submit button is disabled and a loading state is shown.
10) On success, the returned ConsultationBooking object is stored in page state, the form is locked or hidden, and a confirmation component is shown with key details. The confirmation provides actions to go back to index.html or navigate to plans.html.
11) On failure, an error message is displayed and the user can correct fields if needed and re-submit.

Workflow 2: Booking from an offering detail page
1) User clicks a call-to-action on offering_detail.html, which navigates to book_consultation.html with URL parameters such as serviceType, durationMinutes, industry, offeringId, sourceType=from_offering, and sourceReferenceId set to the related ConsultingOffering id.
2) On page load, the Book Consultation page parses incoming URL parameters and stores them in initial page state.
3) getConsultationFormOptions is called to load dynamic options.
4) Because sourceType and sourceReferenceId are present, getConsultationPrefill(sourceType, sourceReferenceId) is called in parallel or after options load.
5) When getConsultationPrefill returns, the page merges three layers to compute initial form values: (a) explicit URL parameters, (b) prefill values from getConsultationPrefill, and (c) generic defaults based on loaded options. Explicit URL parameters always override prefill values for the same field.
6) The form is rendered with these merged initial values (e.g., service type, duration, industry, notes, offeringId, and any scheduledStartSuggestion if relevant).
7) User reviews or adjusts the prefilled values as needed (e.g., changes duration or notes) and fills any missing required fields like contact name and email.
8) User submits the form. submitConsultationBooking is called with the finalized values, including offeringId, sourceType, and sourceReferenceId taken from the merged state.
9) On success, the confirmation view shows that the booking is associated with the context provided (e.g., implicitly tied to the offering via offeringId in the ConsultationBooking), and provides navigation back to index.html or onward to plans.html.
10) If an error occurs, an error state is displayed, with an option to retry submission.

Workflow 3: Booking from an assessment or event
1) User completes an assessment on assessment.html or registers/reads about an event on event_detail.html and initiates a consultation booking. Navigation to book_consultation.html includes URL parameters such as sourceType (from_assessment or from_event), sourceReferenceId (assessment session id or event id), and possibly serviceType, industry, and scheduledStartSuggestion.
2) On load, the page parses all URL parameters and stores them into initial state.
3) getConsultationFormOptions is invoked to obtain current selectable options for all relevant fields.
4) getConsultationPrefill(sourceType, sourceReferenceId) is invoked to retrieve context-aware suggestions (such as a recommended serviceType, industry, durationMinutes, notes, offeringId, or a suggested scheduledStart for events).
5) When both options and prefill data are available, form initial values are computed with the same merge strategy: URL parameters highest priority, then prefill values, then generic defaults.
6) If scheduledStartSuggestion exists either from the URL or from getConsultationPrefill, the date/time picker is initialized to that value; user may adjust it.
7) User completes contact information and any missing fields, then submits the form.
8) Page sends submitConsultationBooking with all fields, including sourceType and sourceReferenceId so that backend can link the ConsultationBooking to the assessment or event context.
9) On success, a confirmation view acknowledges the submission; user can navigate back to index.html or to plans.html.
10) If submission fails, the error is displayed and user can retry without losing already entered values.

Workflow 4: Editing values before submission and retrying after error
1) User opens the page through any entry path and begins filling the form.
2) User attempts to submit, but client-side validation fails (e.g., a required field is empty). The form displays inline validation messages while retaining all previously entered values.
3) User corrects the invalid inputs and submits again.
4) If server-side submitConsultationBooking fails (e.g., due to network or backend error), a generic error message is shown in a status panel, and the form remains editable.
5) User may modify fields (for example, choose a different scheduled date/time) and click submit again, which triggers a new submitConsultationBooking call with the updated values.
6) Once submission succeeds, the confirmation view appears, and the form can optionally be reset or kept read-only for reference.
interactions: On page load, the page parses URL parameters (serviceType, durationMinutes, industry, scheduledStartSuggestion, offeringId, sourceType, sourceReferenceId) and initializes an internal state object that represents the pending ConsultationBooking plus context metadata.

On page load, the page triggers getConsultationFormOptions. While options are loading, form inputs that depend on these options (service type, duration, industry, budget range) are disabled or not rendered. When the API call succeeds, these inputs are populated and enabled.

If sourceType and sourceReferenceId URL parameters are present, the page triggers getConsultationPrefill with those parameters. While prefill data is loading, the form can either show a loading state or allow entry with just generic defaults; when prefill data arrives, any still-empty fields are populated with prefill values without overwriting values the user has already modified.

When the user changes the service type field, the form updates the underlying ConsultationBooking draft state for serviceType, and may optionally adjust other dependent fields such as suggested durationMinutes according to logic implemented in the front-end (e.g., if no user-custom duration chosen yet).

When the user changes the duration field, the ConsultationBooking draft state is updated for durationMinutes, and any non-persisted derived values (such as a client-side computed scheduled end time) are recalculated as needed for display only.

When the user changes the industry field, the ConsultationBooking draft state is updated for industry. This selection is always picked from the dynamic industry options returned by getConsultationFormOptions.

When the user interacts with the date/time picker, changes are captured and mapped to the scheduledStart field in the draft ConsultationBooking state, maintaining the full datetime value expected by submitConsultationBooking.

When the user updates participants count, budget range, contact name, contact email, company name, or notes, those values are written to the corresponding fields in the ConsultationBooking draft state in real time.

When the user clicks the Submit button, the form triggers validation. If all required fields (including at least serviceType, durationMinutes, industry, scheduledStart, contactName, and contactEmail) are valid, submitConsultationBooking is called with the current draft state values plus the contextual fields offeringId, sourceType, and sourceReferenceId from page state.

During the submitConsultationBooking call, the form is placed into a submitting state: the submit button is disabled, optional loading indicators are shown, and repeated submissions are prevented until the call resolves.

If submitConsultationBooking returns success=true, the page stores the returned ConsultationBooking object as the submitted booking state and switches from the editing form state to a confirmation state. The confirmation state uses data from booking (such as id and scheduledStart) for display and disables further editing of that booking.

If submitConsultationBooking returns success=false or the call fails, an error state is shown within a status or error component. The user can modify fields or keep them as-is and click a Retry or Submit button, which triggers another submitConsultationBooking call with the current draft state.
In the confirmation state, when the user clicks 'Back to Home', the page navigates to index.html without additional parameters. This navigation does not depend on any interface calls.
In the confirmation state, when the user clicks 'Explore Packages', the page navigates to plans.html without additional parameters, allowing the user to browse consulting offerings after booking.

If the user uses browser navigation (back/forward) causing URL parameters to change, the page re-runs its initialization logic (including parsing URL parameters and possibly re-calling getConsultationFormOptions and getConsultationPrefill) to keep the form state in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters, interface responses, and live user input, with clear priority rules. On initial load, the page reads the URL parameters serviceType, durationMinutes, industry, scheduledStartSuggestion, offeringId, sourceType, and sourceReferenceId. These raw values are stored in a 'urlContext' state object. Next, the page fetches option metadata via getConsultationFormOptions, storing the result in an 'optionsState' object (for serviceTypes, durations, industries, budgetRanges). If both sourceType and sourceReferenceId are present, the page also calls getConsultationPrefill(sourceType, sourceReferenceId) and stores the response in a 'prefillState' object. Once optionsState and (if applicable) prefillState are available, initial form values are derived using the following precedence for each field: 1) If a corresponding URL parameter is provided and valid, use that value. 2) Otherwise, if getConsultationPrefill returned a value for that field, use the prefill value. 3) Otherwise, fall back to an implementation-defined default based on optionsState (e.g., leaving the field empty or selecting a default option where appropriate). This merged result initializes an editable 'draftBookingState' that mirrors the ConsultationBooking fields plus context fields offeringId, sourceType, and sourceReferenceId. User interactions always update draftBookingState directly; once the user edits a field, subsequent arrival of prefill data must not override the user-modified value. The scheduledStartSuggestion from either URL or prefill is treated as an initial suggestion only and maps into the scheduledStart field; any further changes come exclusively from user interaction. When the form is submitted, submitConsultationBooking is called with values from draftBookingState. On success, the returned ConsultationBooking object replaces or augments draftBookingState in a 'submittedBookingState' and a 'statusState' is set to 'submitted' or 'confirmed', which the UI uses to swap from form view to confirmation view. On failure, statusState is set to 'error' while draftBookingState remains intact, enabling retries. The page does not persist any state outside the current page context; it relies solely on URL parameters for cross-page context and the three assigned interfaces for server interaction.

components.functionality: Initializes the Book Consultation page by parsing URL parameters, calling getConsultationFormOptions, conditionally calling getConsultationPrefill, and constructing the initial draft consultation booking state used by the form.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlParamChange
onOptionsLoaded
onPrefillLoaded
onInitError

components.functionality: Displays and manages the consultation booking form, including fields for service type, duration, industry, scheduled date/time, participants count, budget range, contact details, company name, notes, and any hidden context fields (offeringId, sourceType, sourceReferenceId). It binds to the draft ConsultationBooking state and prepares data for submitConsultationBooking.
event_handlers: onFieldChange
onDateTimeChange
onBudgetRangeChange
onParticipantsChange
onValidationError
onSubmit

components.functionality: Handles date and time selection for the scheduledStart field of the consultation booking. It initializes from any scheduledStartSuggestion and writes updates to the draft booking state in a format compatible with submitConsultationBooking.
event_handlers: onDateChange
onTimeChange
onDateTimeClear

components.functionality: Receives form options from getConsultationFormOptions (serviceTypes, durations, industries, budgetRanges) and exposes them to the consultation-form component. Ensures that option-dependent fields are only interactive when options are available and that default selections are applied consistently.
event_handlers: onOptionsUpdated
onOptionsLoadError

components.functionality: Merges URL parameter values, getConsultationPrefill results, and option-based defaults into a single draft ConsultationBooking state using a defined priority order. Prevents prefill data from overwriting any fields that the user has already edited.
event_handlers: onPrefillDataReceived
onUserEdit
onMergeCompleted

components.functionality: Reflects the current submission status of the consultation booking (idle, validating, submitting, success, error). Shows loading indicators during submitConsultationBooking, displays errors if submission fails, and signals the form and confirmation components about the current status.
event_handlers: onSubmitStart
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitError
onRetryClick

components.functionality: Displays a confirmation view once submitConsultationBooking succeeds, using data from the returned ConsultationBooking (such as id, scheduledStart, and serviceType) for reference. Provides navigation actions to go back to the home page (index.html) or to view plans and packages (plans.html).
event_handlers: onBackToHomeClick
onExplorePackagesClick
onNewBookingClick

components.functionality: Optionally shows concise context about how the user arrived at the booking page based on sourceType, sourceReferenceId, serviceType, or offeringId fields present in the draft or submitted ConsultationBooking state, without fetching additional data. This helps users confirm that the booking is linked to the correct assessment, event, or offering.
event_handlers: onContextChange

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a hero-like, two-column layout similar to the mockup. A dark, charcoal background fills the viewport with a centered max-width container (about 1120px). In the left column, a vertical stack runs from top to bottom: 1) a subtle context line (e.g., "BOOK CONSULTATION" style) integrated with or near the context-indicator, 2) a large, bold H1 for the booking page, 3) a short supporting paragraph about what the consultation covers, 4) the main consultation-form with grouped inputs, including the datetime-picker, and 5) the submission-status-panel just under the primary actions. The right column serves as the counterpart to the portrait in the original mockup: a tall, visually strong context-indicator card that summarizes the consultation details and any prefilled information. The booking-confirmation replaces the form area in the left column once submission succeeds, while the right column remains as supporting context or evolves to show a concise summary. Non-visual controllers (data-loader and state-managers) sit logically above or around these main blocks but do not consume visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a hero-like, two-column layout similar to the mockup. A dark, charcoal background fills the viewport with a centered max-width container (about 1120px). In the left column, a vertical stack runs from top to bottom: 1) a subtle context line (e.g., "BOOK CONSULTATION" style) integrated with or near the context-indicator, 2) a large, bold H1 for the booking page, 3) a short supporting paragraph about what the consultation covers, 4) the main consultation-form with grouped inputs, including the datetime-picker, and 5) the submission-status-panel just under the primary actions. The right column serves as the counterpart to the portrait in the original mockup: a tall, visually strong context-indicator card that summarizes the consultation details and any prefilled information. The booking-confirmation replaces the form area in the left column once submission succeeds, while the right column remains as supporting context or evolves to show a concise summary. Non-visual controllers (data-loader and state-managers) sit logically above or around these main blocks but do not consume visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Following the original hero, the central content area is split into roughly a 65 percent left column (for messaging and the form) and a 35 percent right column (for context-indicator and secondary panels) on desktop. The full layout lives within a max-width container centered on the page, with generous horizontal padding (around 32px on large screens, reducing proportionally on smaller devices). Vertically, the booking section nearly fills the first viewport height (minus the global header), with about 64 to 96px padding at the top and bottom of the main container. Within the left column, the H1, body copy, form fields, and CTAs are spaced with 16 to 32px gaps to maintain a spacious editorial feel. The right column context card has generous internal padding (16 to 24px) and consistent outer margin from the container edges to mirror the portrait's visual weight.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density echoes the mockup: low density, high legibility. The form is broken into clear sections (e.g., About your business, Consultation focus, Scheduling) with noticeable vertical gaps between sections. Each input (including the datetime-picker) has ample vertical spacing, avoiding cramped multi-column field arrangements except where the layout is clearly beneficial (such as date and time side-by-side on wide screens). The right column holds a single dominant info panel (context-indicator), not multiple small widgets, ensuring it reads as one coherent visual element rather than a busy sidebar. The submission-status-panel is compact and unobtrusive when idle, surfacing only brief messages; the booking-confirmation is bold but still airy, with clear hierarchy and generous padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the same Z-pattern implied by the mockup. After the global navigation at the very top, the eye lands on the large white H1 in the upper-left of the hero area, just under any small all-caps context text. From there, the user scans slightly up and right to notice the context-indicator in the right column, which visually balances the left headline. The gaze travels down the left column to the explanatory paragraph and then the consultation-form fields in order. Near the bottom-left of the main zone, the primary call-to-action (Submit/Book) anchors attention; just beneath it, the submission-status-panel provides immediate feedback. In parallel, the right column remains a steady reference, reinforcing what is being booked and how the prefilled details are applied. On successful submission, the flow shifts to the booking-confirmation in the left column, which becomes the primary focal point while the right context card reinforces key details, preserving the left-to-right balance established in the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Book Consultation page presents as a dark-mode, hero-style booking canvas that fills the initial viewport. A global header band spans the top with brand and navigation, mirroring the original site. Below it, a wide, centered container sits on a charcoal background, divided into a larger left column and a narrower right column. On the left, a small, restrained context label and a large, high-contrast headline announce that you are booking an AI and strategy consultation, followed by a short, clear description of what to expect. Directly below, the main consultation form cascades down in a single, clean vertical stack: profile and business questions, consultation goals, and finally the scheduling area featuring the datetime-picker. Each section breathes with generous vertical spacing and clear labels, keeping the layout calm despite its functional depth. At the bottom of the left column, a strong primary button row anchors the booking action, with a subtle status strip below it showing submission states. On the right, a tall, elegant context-indicator card acts like the portrait from the mockup: it is the single, dominant visual in that column, summarizing selected services, prefilled details from other pages, and any constraints or expectations. Non-visual controllers quietly power dynamic behavior without adding visual clutter. When the user submits the form successfully, the left column transitions to a bright, concise booking confirmation panel that takes over the form area while the right context card pivots to a more compact summary of what was booked. The overall feel is modern, composed, and highly intentional, with a clear left-to-right narrative and plenty of space around each component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-page-init component operates behind the scenes and does not present any direct visual interface. In the DOM structure, it sits near the top of the main booking section, just inside the hero container but before visible components like the context-indicator and consultation-form. While it has no visible height or width in the final layout, its loading state is reflected indirectly: until initialization is complete, the left-column consultation-form area and the right-column context-indicator area can display skeleton placeholders or a subtle shimmer within their respective spaces, still respecting the same 65/35 horizontal split and full-viewport vertical structure. Once data is loaded and bindings are ready, these visual placeholders give way to the actual form and context content without changing the spatial arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive; occupies no visible space and does not affect layout flow directly. It acts solely as a functional initializer whose state is represented by temporary placeholder states inside the form and info panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultation-form is the primary focal element in the left column of the hero-like booking area. It begins directly below the page’s H1 and supporting paragraph, aligned to the left edge of the main content container and spanning approximately 65 percent of the container width on desktop. The form forms a tall vertical stack that extends downward, with its top aligned roughly one-third of the viewport height from the top of the hero section. Each field group is separated by 24 to 32 pixels of vertical space, giving the form a spacious, sectioned feel rather than a dense list of inputs. Section headers within the form (such as About your organization, Consultation focus, Scheduling) are slightly bolder and spaced further from preceding content, creating distinct clusters. On larger screens, some related fields (like first and last name, or company size and industry) may share a single row using a simple two-column arrangement, but the overall layout maintains generous width and never feels crowded. The bottom of the form leads directly into the main action area: a wide, primary button row aligned with the left edge of the form content, with secondary actions (if any) to its right or below. The submission-status-panel sits immediately beneath this action row, making the form feel anchored at the bottom of the left column while still leaving a comfortable margin to the lower edge of the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally with the document in the left column of a responsive, two-column grid. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, the form stacks above the right-column content, expanding to near full width (about 90 to 100 percent) while maintaining vertical spacing and section structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The datetime-picker lives inside the lower portion of the consultation-form as part of the Scheduling section. On desktop, it appears roughly in the lower third of the left column height, after the business and consultation focus fields but before the final notes/comments area (if present). The scheduling subsection begins with a clear label and brief helper text, followed by the datetime-picker itself. The control spans approximately half of the form’s content width when there is room (for example, date selector on the left and time selector on the right in a single row), keeping the rest of the row clean and uncluttered. On narrower viewports, the datetime-picker collapses into a single stacked block that spans the full form width, with the date input above the time input and 8 to 16 pixels of vertical spacing between them. Horizontally, it aligns with other fields in the form, keeping a consistent left margin and matching field widths. Vertical spacing above and below the datetime-picker is generous (about 24px above and below) so it reads as a distinct and important step in the booking process while remaining visually integrated with the surrounding form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an inline form control within the consultation-form, adapting responsively between a side-by-side layout for date and time on wider screens and a stacked layout on smaller screens. It does not float or stick, instead moving naturally with the form content as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The options-binding-controller is a non-visual state manager that influences which options appear inside the consultation-form (for example, adjusting available time slots in the datetime-picker or tailoring question sets based on prior selections). It has no explicit on-screen representation. Conceptually, it is associated with the form block in the left column and the context-indicator in the right column, but visually those components occupy the full foreground. Any effect of this controller appears as dynamic changes within existing elements, such as dropdowns, toggles, or context summaries updating in place, without introducing additional UI chrome or consuming extra space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layout-transparent; it does not alter the physical positioning or size of components, only their internal state and content. All visible changes occur inside the existing bounds of the form and right-column info panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prefill-merge-controller functions behind the scenes to merge context from other pages (for example, selected service type or organization details) into the consultation-form and context-indicator. It is not drawn as a visible component. From a layout perspective, its influence is seen in pre-populated fields in the left-column form and in richer, more detailed content in the right-column context-indicator card. These values appear seamlessly within the existing layout as soon as the page is initialized, creating the sense that the page already understands the user’s intent without adding any new visual elements or occupying additional space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully non-visual; does not affect spacing or placement of visible components. All spatial behavior is expressed through updated values within the pre-existing form fields and info panel, preserving the minimalist, uncluttered layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-panel is a slim, horizontal feedback strip positioned directly beneath the primary action row at the bottom of the consultation-form in the left column. It aligns with the left edge of the form’s input fields and spans nearly the full width of the form content (around 80 to 90 percent of the left column width), stopping short of the far edge to maintain a sense of breathing room. In its idle state, the panel either remains hidden (taking up no visible height) or shows a very subtle placeholder line height (around 16 to 20 pixels) with neutral text, ensuring the bottom of the form still feels light. During submission or on errors, the panel expands slightly in visual weight, presenting concise messages in high-contrast text, possibly with a small icon, but still keeping to a low height that does not disrupt the page’s balance. It remains tightly integrated with the form, reading as a continuation of the action area rather than a separate block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the form content and anchored to the bottom of the left column’s booking area. It does not float or stick; instead, it scrolls with the form on smaller screens. Its height adjusts dynamically depending on message presence but does not shift major layout sections, only local vertical spacing near the CTAs.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation panel appears in place of the consultation-form in the left column once a submission succeeds. It occupies the same footprint as the form: aligned with the left margin of the main container and spanning approximately 65 percent of the container width on desktop. Visually, it presents as a clean, high-contrast success card integrated into the dark hero background: a bold confirmation title at the top (for example, "Your consultation is booked"), followed by key details such as date, time, and meeting format. These details are arranged in a tidy vertical list with 16 to 24 pixels of spacing between items, maintaining the editorial spacing rhythm of the page. A secondary action row (e.g., Add to calendar, View details, or Return to site) sits at the bottom of the panel, echoing the previous location of the primary CTA but now serving post-booking actions. The right column’s context-indicator continues to occupy its 35 percent width, now acting as a complementary summary or next-steps panel, which visually balances the new confirmation content on the left so the two-column structure is preserved even after the form disappears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the consultation-form within the left column when active, using a smooth transition (such as fade or slide) but retaining the same spatial footprint. It scrolls naturally with the page, and on smaller screens, it stacks above the right-column content, occupying near-full width while keeping generous top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context-indicator is the dominant visual element in the right column and plays the role of the portrait-equivalent from the original hero mockup. It is placed at the top-right of the main booking container, aligned horizontally with the page’s headline and vertically with the top of the consultation-form. The panel is rendered as a tall, card-like block that spans roughly 35 percent of the container width on desktop and extends down to cover about 60 to 75 percent of the hero height, depending on its content. It has generous internal padding (around 24px) and rounded corners, standing out subtly against the charcoal background with either a slightly lighter tone or a thin border. Inside, content is arranged in a clear vertical hierarchy: at the top, a concise label (for example, "Consultation context") followed by a short summary of the selected service, then key prefilled details from previous interactions (such as organization, team size, preferred focus areas) presented as neatly spaced lines or compact chips. The lower portion of the panel can highlight what will be covered or expectations, maintaining clear separation between content blocks with 16 to 24 pixels of spacing. On smaller screens, this panel shifts below the consultation-form or booking-confirmation, still spanning nearly full width but maintaining its card-like appearance and generous breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored in the right column of the two-column grid, effectively static relative to the container. On wide viewports, it visually counterbalances the left-column form or confirmation; on tablet and mobile, it collapses into the main vertical flow beneath the left-column content, expanding to near-full width while preserving its internal structure and spacing.

name: Plans & Packages
title: Plans & Packages
description: Browse and filter standardized AI consulting packages by company size, budget, and offering type, then navigate to detailed pages or request a proposal for a selected package.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options for company size segments, budget ranges, and offering types using getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.
Parse incoming URL parameters (companySizeSegment, maxPrice, offeringType) to pre-populate filters and initial search state.
Search and retrieve matching consulting packages using searchConsultingOfferings with the current filter and sort state.
Display a list of consulting packages (ConsultingOffering entities) with key attributes such as name, price, target company size segment, duration, and workshop inclusion.
Provide interactive filters for company size segment, budget range or numeric max price, offering type, and workshop inclusion, all synchronized with URL parameters.
Provide sorting controls (e.g., by price, relevance, or other backend-supported criteria) that update searchConsultingOfferings via the sortBy parameter.
Show summary information about the current result set (totalCount, selected filters) and allow clearing all filters.
Allow navigation to offering_detail.html with the selected offeringId when a user clicks a package card or a 'View details' action.
Allow navigation to proposal_request.html with offeringId and sourceType=from_plans when a user clicks a 'Request proposal' action on a package.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load with or without preset filters
1. User opens plans.html (directly or via navigation from another page).
2. plans-page-controller reads URL query parameters: companySizeSegment, maxPrice, offeringType (if present).
3. plans-page-controller invokes getConsultingPackageFilterOptions to obtain companySizeSegments, budgetRanges, and offeringTypes.
4. Once filter options are loaded, plans-page-controller derives initial filter state:
   - If companySizeSegment is present, it selects the matching segment option.
   - If maxPrice is present, it sets the numeric maxPrice filter and, if applicable, aligns it with one of the returned budgetRanges.
   - If offeringType is present, it selects the matching offeringTypes option.
5. plans-page-controller triggers searchConsultingOfferings with:
   - targetCompanySizeSegment from the selected companySizeSegment value (if any).
   - minEmployees and maxEmployees derived from the selected company size option (if any).
   - maxPrice from URL or selected budget range (if any), and minPrice from the selected budget range (if any).
   - currency from the selected budget range (if any).
   - offeringType from URL or selected filter (if any).
   - includesWorkshops and minWorkshopsCount unset on initial load unless provided via URL.
   - status set to the value that returns only active offerings (e.g., 'active').
   - sortBy set to a default sort value used consistently by the page.
6. searchConsultingOfferings returns results and totalCount.
7. package-list renders the list of ConsultingOffering items; results-summary shows totalCount and the currently active filters.
8. If searchConsultingOfferings fails or returns no results, loading-error-state displays an error or no-results message with an option to clear filters.

Workflow 2: Adjusting filters and seeing updated packages
1. User interacts with filter-bar (e.g., selects a different company size, changes budget, toggles workshop inclusion, selects an offering type).
2. filter-bar emits an onFilterChange event with the updated filter state.
3. plans-page-controller receives the updated state, updates the URL query string accordingly (adding or updating parameters such as companySizeSegment, maxPrice, offeringType, includesWorkshops, minWorkshopsCount, currency), and keeps them in sync.
4. plans-page-controller invokes searchConsultingOfferings with parameters mapped from the new filter state.
5. While waiting for the response, loading-error-state shows a loading indicator and disables repeated search triggers if necessary.
6. When the response arrives, package-list re-renders with the new results and results-summary updates totalCount and filter summary.
7. If the user clicks 'Clear all filters' in results-summary or filter-bar, plans-page-controller removes filter-related parameters from the URL, resets internal filter state, and runs searchConsultingOfferings without those filters.

Workflow 3: Sorting the package list
1. User opens sort-control and selects a sort option (e.g., by price or by relevance), each mapped to a backend-supported sortBy value.
2. sort-control emits onSortChange with the selected sortBy value.
3. plans-page-controller updates the URL query parameter for sortBy and internal state.
4. plans-page-controller calls searchConsultingOfferings with the existing filters plus the new sortBy value.
5. package-list re-renders in the new order once the results are returned; results-summary keeps the same filter summary but may reflect the current sort mode in its display.

Workflow 4: Viewing package details
1. User scans package-list and clicks anywhere on a package-card (or a dedicated 'View details' action) for a specific ConsultingOffering.
2. package-card emits onViewDetails with the offeringId of the selected ConsultingOffering.
3. plans-page-controller constructs a navigation URL to offering_detail.html including offeringId as a query parameter (e.g., offering_detail.html?offeringId=<id>), leaving other current filters out unless explicitly desired.
4. Browser navigates to offering_detail.html where the offering detail page can use the offeringId to load full package details.

Workflow 5: Requesting a proposal from a package
1. User identifies a suitable package in package-list and clicks a 'Request proposal' action on that package-card.
2. package-card emits onRequestProposal with the offeringId.
3. plans-page-controller navigates to proposal_request.html, appending query parameters offeringId=<id> and sourceType=from_plans.
4. proposal_request.html can then pre-fill its form using the offeringId and sourceType indicating the request originated from Plans & Packages.

Workflow 6: Navigating via back/forward and deep links
1. User modifies filters or sorting several times, causing URL query parameters to change on each update.
2. When the user uses the browser back or forward buttons, the URL changes to a previous or next state.
3. plans-page-controller listens to URL changes (onUrlChange) and re-parses current query parameters into filter and sort state.
4. For each URL change, plans-page-controller calls searchConsultingOfferings with parameters derived from the URL.
5. package-list and results-summary update accordingly, ensuring that the displayed state always matches the URL.

Workflow 7: No results handling and recovery
1. User applies a set of filters that yield no results from searchConsultingOfferings (totalCount is 0).
2. package-list shows an empty state via loading-error-state including an explanation and options such as 'Clear all filters'.
3. If user clicks 'Clear all filters', filter-bar resets, plans-page-controller clears filter-related URL parameters, and searchConsultingOfferings is called with minimal constraints.
4. The user can then gradually reapply filters to narrow down the listings.
interactions:
On page load (onPageLoad in plans-page-controller):
  - Read URL parameters (companySizeSegment, maxPrice, offeringType, plus any internal parameters like includesWorkshops, minWorkshopsCount, currency, sortBy if present).
  - Call getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.
  - Initialize filter-bar with options and preselected values derived from URL.
  - Call searchConsultingOfferings with mapped filter and sort parameters.

On URL change (onUrlChange in plans-page-controller):
  - Re-parse URL query parameters into filter and sort state.
  - Update filter-bar and sort-control selections to match URL.
  - Re-call searchConsultingOfferings using these parameters.

On filter field change (onFilterFieldChange in filter-bar):
  - Triggered when user selects or changes company size segment.
  - filter-bar emits updated filter state; plans-page-controller updates URL (companySizeSegment and minEmployees/maxEmployees derived from the selected segment) and re-runs searchConsultingOfferings.

On budget change (onBudgetChange in filter-bar):
  - Triggered when user selects a budget range from budgetRanges or manually adjusts a max price field.
  - filter-bar emits an updated filter state, including minPrice, maxPrice, and currency where applicable.
  - plans-page-controller updates maxPrice and any other relevant budget-related query parameters and calls searchConsultingOfferings.

On offering type change (onOfferingTypeChange in filter-bar):
  - Triggered when user selects an offering type option.
  - filter-bar emits offeringType; plans-page-controller updates the offeringType parameter in the URL and searchConsultingOfferings.

On workshop inclusion toggle (onWorkshopsToggle in filter-bar):
  - Triggered when user toggles a control to show only packages that include workshops.
  - filter-bar emits includesWorkshops and optional minWorkshopsCount.
  - plans-page-controller updates URL parameters includesWorkshops and minWorkshopsCount, then calls searchConsultingOfferings.

On clear all filters (onClearFilters in filter-bar or results-summary):
  - Clears all user-adjustable filter controls in filter-bar.
