  - plans-page-controller removes filter-related query parameters (including companySizeSegment, maxPrice, offeringType, includesWorkshops, minWorkshopsCount, currency) from the URL.
  - plans-page-controller calls searchConsultingOfferings with only default constraints such as status.
  - package-list re-renders with the broader result set.

On sort option change (onSortChange in sort-control):
  - User selects a different sort option.
  - sort-control emits the chosen sortBy value.
  - plans-page-controller updates the sortBy URL parameter and calls searchConsultingOfferings with existing filters plus the new sortBy.

On package card click for details (onViewDetails in package-card):
  - User clicks a package-card (or a specific 'View details' control on that card).
  - package-card emits offeringId.
  - plans-page-controller navigates the browser to offering_detail.html?offeringId=<offeringId>.

On 'Request proposal' click (onRequestProposal in package-card):
  - User clicks a 'Request proposal' control on a listed package.
  - package-card emits offeringId.
  - plans-page-controller navigates to proposal_request.html?offeringId=<offeringId>&sourceType=from_plans.

On search error (onSearchError in plans-page-controller):
  - If searchConsultingOfferings returns an error, plans-page-controller updates loading-error-state with error information.
  - loading-error-state can display a retry control; on clicking retry (onRetryClick), plans-page-controller re-invokes searchConsultingOfferings with the current URL-derived parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses the URL query string as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. On every load or navigation event, plans-page-controller parses the URL and derives the state passed to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions (only for options) and searchConsultingOfferings (for actual results):

- Incoming parameters recognized from other pages:
  - companySizeSegment (string):
    - If present, sets the selected company size segment filter in filter-bar.
    - Passed directly as targetCompanySizeSegment to searchConsultingOfferings.
    - minEmployees and maxEmployees for searchConsultingOfferings are derived from the selected segment in companySizeSegments returned by getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.
  - maxPrice (number):
    - If present, sets the numeric maxPrice filter in filter-bar (independent of budgetRanges selection logic).
    - Passed to searchConsultingOfferings.maxPrice.
  - offeringType (string):
    - If present, preselects the offering type in filter-bar.
    - Passed as offeringType to searchConsultingOfferings.

- Additional URL parameters managed internally by this page:
  - includesWorkshops (boolean-like string):
    - If present, filter-bar toggles its workshop inclusion control accordingly.
    - Converted to a boolean when mapped to searchConsultingOfferings.includesWorkshops.
  - minWorkshopsCount (number):
    - If present, sets a numeric threshold in filter-bar and is passed directly to searchConsultingOfferings.minWorkshopsCount.
  - currency (string):
    - May be set when a user chooses a budget range with a specific currency.
    - Passed to searchConsultingOfferings.currency.
  - minPrice (number):
    - May be set when a user selects a budget range; derived from that range.
    - Passed to searchConsultingOfferings.minPrice.
  - sortBy (string):
    - Reflects the currently selected sort mode from sort-control.
    - Passed directly to searchConsultingOfferings.sortBy.

- Constant parameter used for all searches:
  - status: plans-page-controller always passes a value that returns only active packages to searchConsultingOfferings.status.

- Synchronization rules:
  - Any change in filter-bar or sort-control triggers a URL update. The controller merges the new key-value pairs into the existing query string while preserving unrelated parameters.
  - After updating the URL, the controller immediately calls searchConsultingOfferings using the new URL-derived state.
  - Browser navigation (back/forward) triggers the same URL parsing logic and re-executes searchConsultingOfferings, so the UI always reflects the URL.

- State precedence:
  - On page load, URL parameters (if present) take precedence over any client-side defaults.
  - After getConsultingPackageFilterOptions returns, the controller validates that URL parameter values match available options (e.g., companySizeSegment and offeringType). If a URL value does not correspond to any option, the controller ignores that specific value and removes it from internal state (and optionally from the URL on the next state update).

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL state parsing, filter/sort application, and calls to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions and searchConsultingOfferings. Acts as the central state manager, ensuring that filter-bar, sort-control, package-list, and results-summary remain synchronized with URL query parameters.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlChange
onFiltersUpdated
onSortUpdated
onSearchSuccess
onSearchError
onViewDetails
onRequestProposal

components.functionality: Displays and manages all user-controlled search filters: company size segment, budget range or numeric max price, offering type, and workshop inclusion. Uses dynamic options from getConsultingPackageFilterOptions and emits normalized filter state to the controller whenever a filter is changed or cleared.
event_handlers: onFilterFieldChange
onBudgetChange
onOfferingTypeChange
onWorkshopsToggle
onClearFilters

components.functionality: Provides the user with sorting options for the package list (for example, by price or other supported criteria). The selected sort option is mapped to a sortBy value and synchronized with the URL and searchConsultingOfferings.
event_handlers: onSortChange

components.functionality: Shows the number of matching packages (totalCount from searchConsultingOfferings) and a concise summary of currently active filters (derived from URL parameters and filter-bar state). Provides an action to clear all filters.
event_handlers: onClearFilters

components.functionality: Renders the list of ConsultingOffering results returned by searchConsultingOfferings, delegating each item to package-card. Handles empty state display when there are no results.
event_handlers: onSelectOffering
onRequestProposal

components.functionality: Represents a single ConsultingOffering within the package-list. Displays dynamic attributes such as name, price, currency, priceType, targetCompanySizeSegment, typicalDurationWeeks, includesWorkshops, and highlightBullets. Provides user actions to view details and request a proposal for the specific offering.
event_handlers: onViewDetails
onRequestProposal

components.functionality: Displays loading indicators while getConsultingPackageFilterOptions or searchConsultingOfferings are in progress, and shows error messages or a no-results message when appropriate. Offers a retry action in case of errors.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the hero pattern from the mockup, but adapted to a listing context. The top of the main content forms a hero-like band where the page-controller text occupies the left column (section label, H1, short description, and any high-level controls), and the results-summary sits as a compact, card-like panel in the right column, visually replacing the portrait. Below this hero band, a single horizontal control strip houses the filter-bar aligned to the left and the sort-control aligned to the right. Under that, the package-list flows vertically down the page in a low-density, editorial-style stack of package-cards, with the loading-error-state occupying the same visual footprint whenever needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the hero pattern from the mockup, but adapted to a listing context. The top of the main content forms a hero-like band where the page-controller text occupies the left column (section label, H1, short description, and any high-level controls), and the results-summary sits as a compact, card-like panel in the right column, visually replacing the portrait. Below this hero band, a single horizontal control strip houses the filter-bar aligned to the left and the sort-control aligned to the right. Under that, the package-list flows vertically down the page in a low-density, editorial-style stack of package-cards, with the loading-error-state occupying the same visual footprint whenever needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content sits in a centered max-width container (≈1100–1200px) on a dark charcoal background, with 24–32px horizontal padding on smaller screens and more generous margins on large screens. The hero band at the top of the main content takes roughly the top 30–40% of the viewport height on desktop: about 60–65% width for the left text (page-controller) and 35–40% width for the right summary panel (results-summary). The filter/sort strip directly beneath spans the full container width but is relatively shallow. The package-list then consumes the remainder of the vertical space, scrolling as needed, with cards taking the full content width on mobile and a comfortable 1–2 column layout on larger screens while preserving wide margins and breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density mirrors the mockup’s spacious, editorial style. Each logical cluster (page controller vs summary panel vs filters vs cards) is clearly separated by 32–64px vertical gaps. Within components, content is arranged in tight, linear vertical stacks but surrounded by ample white (or dark) space. Package-cards use generous padding (24–32px) and clear separation between text groups; filters and sort controls are not crowded, with 8–16px gaps between individual controls. There are no dense tables; instead, information is grouped into short, scannable blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the hero-first emphasis: eyes land on the large ‘Plans & Packages’ headline in the top-left of the hero band, scan the short supporting copy below, then move right to the results-summary card. From there, the gaze naturally drops down to the filter-bar on the left, then across to the sort-control on the right, before continuing downward into the vertical stack of package-cards. The overall flow creates a Z-pattern at the top (header → hero headline left → summary right) and then a vertical reading rhythm down the list, with CTAs (like ‘Request proposal’ on cards) aligned towards the right edges to gently pull attention horizontally again.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark charcoal canvas with a centered, max-width content column. At the very top (outside this spec) is the global header and navigation, thin and unobtrusive, echoing the mockup. Immediately beneath, the main content opens with a dark-mode hero band for the Plans & Packages page: on the left, a tight vertical stack of an all-caps context label, a large white ‘Plans & Packages’ H1, and a short, high-contrast description explaining how to browse and request proposals. On the right, in the same band, a light-surfaced results-summary card floats against the dark background, capturing total packages found and key filter highlights. This two-column hero area fills roughly the upper third of the viewport, with generous padding top and bottom. Below, a horizontal control strip runs across the full width: filter chips and dropdowns stretch from the left, while a compact sort-control sits anchored to the right, all on a slightly elevated or subtly lighter panel to distinguish controls from content. Beneath this, the page opens into a clean, vertical scroll of package-cards, each a full-width card with plenty of breathing room, subtly separated by whitespace rather than heavy borders. Cards present plan details in a left-dominant layout with pricing and primary CTAs aligned to the right, keeping with the page’s left-to-right visual pull. If something goes wrong (errors, empty state), a centered loading-error-state appears exactly where the list would be, using the same generous margins and dark-mode aesthetic, ensuring the page feels cohesive and calm even in edge cases.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans-page-controller forms the left side of the main hero band for this page. It is positioned just below the global header, aligned to the left within the centered max-width container. On desktop, it occupies approximately 60–65% of the container width, beginning about 64px below the header and extending down for roughly 40–50% of the viewport height. Its content is arranged in a clean vertical stack: at the very top, a small, all-caps context label (e.g., ‘PLANS & PACKAGES’) aligned left; beneath it, a large, multi-line H1 headline that can comfortably span 2–3 lines without feeling cramped; below the headline, a short supporting paragraph of 2–4 lines. Any high-level page controls (such as a ‘Compare view’ toggle or breadcrumb) sit either directly beneath the paragraph or right-aligned within this left column, but still visually subordinate to the headline. There is generous whitespace above the context label and below the last line of text (32–48px), keeping the block visually anchored but airy. On tablet and mobile, this component becomes full-width, with its content centered within the container and stacked above the results-summary, maintaining similar vertical spacing but reducing the horizontal span.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element anchored at the top of the main content area, spanning the left portion of a two-column hero on desktop and converting to a full-width hero text block on smaller screens without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar sits directly below the hero band (plans-page-controller + results-summary), forming the left portion of a horizontal control strip. It is aligned to the left edge of the main content container and spans roughly 65–75% of the container width on desktop, leaving room for the sort-control on the right. Vertically, it is relatively shallow, with 16–24px of padding above and below its controls, and at least 24–32px of whitespace separating it from the hero band above and from the package-list below. The individual filters (dropdowns, pill toggles, etc.) are arranged left-to-right with 8–16px gaps, and the entire row is kept uncluttered: if there are many filters, they wrap onto a second line with a consistent 8–12px vertical gap between rows. The background of this strip can be a slightly lighter dark tone or a subtle surface elevation to visually distinguish it from the hero above and content below. On tablet and mobile, the filter-bar becomes full-width, stacked above the sort-control, with filters potentially wrapping into multiple rows while still aligned left with generous side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a horizontal toolbar that can optionally become sticky to the top of the viewport once the hero scrolls out of view, staying pinned just beneath the global header so filters remain accessible while the package-list scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control shares the same horizontal strip as the filter-bar but is anchored to the right edge of the content container. On desktop, it occupies approximately 20–30% of the container width, appearing as a compact control group (e.g., a label plus dropdown or segmented buttons) right-aligned within that strip. It sits on the same baseline as the first row of filters, creating a clean left-to-right tension within the bar: filters on the left, sorting on the right. Horizontal spacing between it and the nearest filter is ample (at least 32–48px) to avoid crowding. On smaller screens, the sort-control drops beneath the filter-bar and becomes left-aligned, with a 16–24px vertical gap separating it from the bottom of the filters, preserving the overall low-density feel. Regardless of screen size, it visually reads as a secondary—but always visible—control, never dominating the hero or list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares the same sticky behavior as the filter-bar if that bar is configured as sticky, moving along with it as a right-aligned element within the same horizontal band while the underlying package-list scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary occupies the right column of the hero band, mirroring the portrait’s role from the original mockup. On desktop, it is positioned to the right of the plans-page-controller, aligned to the top of that hero band and taking approximately 35–40% of the container width. It appears as a visually distinct card or panel with a lighter surface against the dark background, soft corners, and generous inner padding (24–32px). Within the panel, content is arranged vertically: at the top, a small label (e.g., ‘Current matches’), followed by a prominent numeric count (e.g., ‘24 plans found’) set in a bold style, and then 1–3 lines of summary text or key filter chips beneath. The card’s height roughly aligns with the height of the hero text on the left, creating a balanced rectangular block; however, it should be allowed to auto-size based on content, with at least 32px of breathing room between its bottom edge and the bottom of the hero band. On tablet and mobile, the results-summary shifts below the plans-page-controller, becomes full-width, and is centered horizontally, with 24–32px vertical separation from the hero text above, maintaining its card-like appearance and prominence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel within the hero area, anchored to the right side of the container on desktop and flowing into a full-width block beneath the hero text on smaller screens, without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-list is the main scrolling content region beneath the filter/sort strip. It spans the full width of the main content container, aligned centrally with the same left and right margins as the hero band. A vertical gap of 32–48px separates it from the control strip above, reinforcing the sense of distinct sections. On desktop, package-cards are presented as a vertical stack, each card taking most of the container width; depending on available width, a responsive layout can show either a comfortable single column or switch to two columns with a 24–32px horizontal gutter while still maintaining wide outer margins. Vertical spacing between each card is generous, typically 24–32px, so that each package reads as a distinct unit. The list grows downward as more packages are loaded, with the page scrolling beneath the fixed header (and optional sticky filters). On tablet and mobile, the list simplifies to a single column of full-width cards, preserving the same vertical spacing and centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard scrollable content region that expands vertically as needed, positioned below the filter and sort controls, with no fixed or sticky behavior; it is the area that visually scrolls while header and optional sticky controls remain in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package-card appears as a distinct, horizontally oriented card within the package-list. The card stretches across the usable content width for the current layout: on desktop, nearly the full width of a single column (or one of two columns if used), with 24–32px inner padding on all sides and subtly rounded corners. The interior layout follows a two-column structure inspired by the hero: the left roughly 65–70% of the card width is dedicated to textual details (plan name, short description, key inclusions), arranged in a vertical stack; the right 30–35% houses pricing, plan tier labels, and the primary CTA (‘Request proposal’, ‘View details’), vertically centered or aligned to the top with sufficient spacing between elements. The plan title uses a strong typographic style and sits at the top-left, followed by 1–3 lines of supporting description, then a concise list of 3–5 key bullets or tags. The right side may feature the price set in a bold style, with the CTA button aligned towards the bottom-right or centered horizontally within that column, ensuring it stands out without overwhelming the layout. Vertical spacing between text lines and between left and right columns follows the 8–16px grid, keeping the card visually calm and legible. On mobile, the card collapses to a single column: text content first, then pricing and CTA stacked below, all left-aligned with consistent spacing, while preserving generous padding and separation from adjacent cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A repeatable, responsive list item within the package-list that maintains its card-like appearance across breakpoints, reflowing from a two-column internal layout on desktop to a single-column layout on smaller screens without any fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-error-state occupies the same spatial region as the package-list but is displayed when there are no results to show, when filters return nothing, or when an error or loading state is active. It is centered horizontally within the main content container and vertically given generous breathing room, typically starting 64–96px below the filter/sort strip so it feels distinct and noticeable. The panel itself may be a subtle, lightly elevated card with 24–32px padding, containing an icon or simple illustration at the top, a short bold headline (‘No plans found’ or ‘Something went wrong’), and 1–2 lines of explanatory text, followed by an optional CTA (e.g., ‘Clear filters’ or ‘Try again’) centered beneath. The width is constrained to approximately 50–60% of the container on desktop to maintain focus and avoid sprawling across the entire width, while on mobile it becomes nearly full-width with appropriate side padding. Surrounding whitespace is ample, with no other content immediately adjacent, so the message is clear and unambiguous.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the visible package-list: it appears in the same vertical region where the list would normally render. It scrolls with the page as a normal block element, without stickiness, and disappears once valid results are available.

name: Offering Detail
title: Offering Detail
description: Displays full details for a single consulting offering, resolved by URL parameters, and provides primary actions to request a proposal or book a consultation.

core_features: Resolve and load a single ConsultingOffering using getOfferingDetail based on URL parameters (offeringId or slug).
Show key offering information such as name, offeringType, tagline, shortDescription, fullDescription, objectives, deliverables, and highlightBullets.
Display structured details including targetCompanySizeSegment, minEmployees, maxEmployees, includesWorkshops, workshopsCount, and typicalDurationWeeks.
Display pricing information using price, currency, and priceType, with logic to adapt labels to the pricing model.
Indicate offering status (active/inactive) and adjust interactions (e.g., disable CTAs when inactive).
Provide primary calls-to-action: 'Request Proposal' (navigates to proposal_request.html) and 'Book Consultation' (navigates to book_consultation.html), including appropriate URL parameters.
Respect and surface the sourceType URL parameter for contextual messaging and analytics (e.g., from_plans, from_assessment).
Handle loading, not-found, and error states when resolving or fetching the offering.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Viewing an offering from Plans:
1) User clicks an offering on plans.html, which links to offering_detail.html with ?offeringId={id}&sourceType=from_plans.
2) On load, the page reads offeringId and sourceType from the URL.
3) The page calls getOfferingDetail with offeringId.
4) While waiting, a loading state is shown.
5) When data returns, the page shows name, type, description, objectives, deliverables, bullets, and structured details.
6) The pricing section renders based on price, priceType, and currency.
7) The CTA panel displays 'Request Proposal' and 'Book Consultation' if the offering status is active.
8) User may click 'Request Proposal', navigating to proposal_request.html?offeringId={resolvedId}&sourceType=from_plans.

Workflow 2 – Viewing an offering from Assessment:
1) User finishes the AI Readiness Assessment and clicks a recommended offering linking to offering_detail.html?offeringId={id}&sourceType=from_assessment.
2) The page reads offeringId and sourceType and calls getOfferingDetail with offeringId.
3) The page may show contextual text indicating that this offering is recommended based on the assessment (driven only by sourceType, not additional APIs).
4) User reviews objectives, deliverables, duration, and workshops.
5) User clicks 'Book Consultation'; the page navigates to book_consultation.html with URL parameters offeringId={resolvedId}, serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_offering, sourceReferenceId={resolvedId}.

Workflow 3 – SEO / direct traffic via slug:
1) User lands on offering_detail.html?slug={offering-slug} (e.g., from index.html or external link), optionally with sourceType.
2) The page resolves the offering using getOfferingDetail with slug.
3) If a valid offering is returned, the page displays all details and CTAs, using the returned id as the canonical offeringId for CTA URLs.
4) If getOfferingDetail fails or returns no offering, the page shows a not-found message and a link back to plans.html.

Workflow 4 – Handling inactive or unavailable offerings:
1) User visits offering_detail.html with offeringId or slug.
2) The page calls getOfferingDetail and receives a ConsultingOffering with status=inactive, or no offering is found.
3) If inactive, the page shows the basic information (name and type) plus a message that the offering is no longer available, and hides or disables the primary CTAs.
4) If no offering is found, the page shows an error/not-found state and suggested navigation back to plans.html, without showing pricing or CTAs.
interactions: On initial page load, the URL parameter reader determines whether to use offeringId or slug (preferring offeringId if both exist) and triggers getOfferingDetail.
While getOfferingDetail is pending, the main content area shows a loading indicator; all offering-dependent components listen for the loading state.
If getOfferingDetail resolves successfully, all bound components (header, structured summary, description, pricing, CTA panel) render using the ConsultingOffering data.
If getOfferingDetail fails or returns no offering, an error/not-found component is displayed and offering-specific components are hidden.
If ConsultingOffering.status is inactive, the page shows an availability notice and hides or disables 'Request Proposal' and 'Book Consultation' buttons.
Clicking 'Request Proposal' constructs a URL to proposal_request.html with query parameters offeringId={ConsultingOffering.id} and sourceType=from_plans and navigates the browser to that URL.

Clicking 'Book Consultation' constructs a URL to book_consultation.html with query parameters offeringId={ConsultingOffering.id}, serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_offering, and sourceReferenceId={ConsultingOffering.id}, then navigates to that URL.

When sourceType URL parameter equals from_assessment, the page shows an assessment-context note near the header or CTAs to explain why this offering is shown; other values such as from_plans or absence of sourceType result in default behavior.
If the user modifies the URL (e.g., changes slug or offeringId) and reloads, the page re-runs the resolution logic and refetches the offering via getOfferingDetail.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and the result of getOfferingDetail. On load, the URL is parsed for offeringId, slug, and sourceType. If offeringId is present, it is used as the primary identifier; otherwise, slug is used. The chosen identifier is passed to getOfferingDetail (with either offeringId or slug populated, the other left empty). While awaiting the response, a global loading flag is set. On success, the returned ConsultingOffering object (including id, name, offeringType, descriptions, objectives, deliverables, pricing, and status) becomes the single source of truth for all display components and for constructing navigation URLs. The canonical offeringId for outgoing links is always ConsultingOffering.id from the API response, even if the page was entered via slug. The sourceType URL parameter is not sent to getOfferingDetail but is stored as contextual state for analytics and conditional messaging. If getOfferingDetail returns no data or errors, the page sets an error state and suppresses all offering-specific components. No localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used; all contextual state is either in memory for the current session or encoded into URLs when navigating to proposal_request.html or book_consultation.html.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (offeringId, slug, sourceType), decides which identifier to use for loading the offering, and exposes a resolvedKey and contextSourceType for other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse window.location.search for offeringId, slug, and sourceType and emit state {resolvedOfferingId, resolvedSlug, contextSourceType}.
onUrlChange: If the URL query changes (e.g., via manual edit or internal link), re-parse parameters and update state, triggering a fresh load in the data loader.

components.functionality: Uses the resolved offeringId or slug from the state manager to call getOfferingDetail and maintains loading, success, and error states for the ConsultingOffering.
event_handlers: onResolvedKeyChange: When resolvedOfferingId or resolvedSlug changes, call getOfferingDetail({offeringId: resolvedOfferingId, slug: resolvedSlug}).
onLoadStart: Set loading=true, error=null, offering=null.
onLoadSuccess: Set loading=false, error=null, offering={ConsultingOffering} and propagate to child components.
onLoadError: Set loading=false, error={errorInfo}, offering=null and notify error-display component.

components.functionality: Displays a loading state while the ConsultingOffering is being fetched and hides itself when loading is complete.
event_handlers: onLoaderStateChange: When offering-detail-loader.loading is true, render a loading indicator; when false, hide it.

components.functionality: Shows an error or not-found message when the offering cannot be loaded or does not exist, and suggests navigation back to plans or the homepage.
event_handlers: onLoaderStateChange: When offering-detail-loader.error is not null or offering is null after load, render an error/not-found message and hide offering-specific components.
onClickGoToPlans: Navigate to plans.html (without relying on additional APIs).

components.functionality: Displays the main header information for the offering, including name, offeringType, tagline, and shortDescription, and may show a contextual label based on sourceType or status.
event_handlers: onOfferingLoaded: When offering-detail-loader.offering is set, render name, offeringType, tagline, and shortDescription.
onContextSourceTypeChange: If contextSourceType equals from_assessment, display an additional hint that this offering was recommended from the assessment; otherwise show default header.
onStatusChange: If ConsultingOffering.status is inactive, display a clear indicator that the offering is not currently available.

components.functionality: Shows structured summary information such as targetCompanySizeSegment, minEmployees, maxEmployees, includesWorkshops, workshopsCount, and typicalDurationWeeks in a concise overview.
event_handlers: onOfferingLoaded: Render structured fields if present; hide or omit any fields that are null or undefined.
onStatusChange: If ConsultingOffering.status is inactive, maintain read-only display but visually indicate unavailability (functionally, no change to links or buttons here).

components.functionality: Displays the detailed narrative content of the offering, including fullDescription, objectives, deliverables, and highlightBullets.
event_handlers: onOfferingLoaded: Render fullDescription if available; render objectives and deliverables in separate subsections if present; render highlightBullets as a list when provided.
onOfferingCleared: If offering-detail-loader.offering becomes null (e.g., due to an error), clear all displayed text.

components.functionality: Displays pricing information derived from ConsultingOffering.price, priceType, and currency, adapting labels such as fixed price, starting at, or custom quote based on priceType.
event_handlers: onOfferingLoaded: Read price, currency, and priceType. If priceType is fixed or starting_at and price is present, render price with currency and the appropriate label. If priceType is custom_quote or price is missing, render explanatory text indicating that pricing is available on request.

onStatusChange: If ConsultingOffering.status is inactive, optionally hide pricing or annotate it as reference-only, depending on business rules; functionally, prevent misleading signals about availability.

components.functionality: Provides primary actions 'Request Proposal' and 'Book Consultation', constructing navigation URLs with the required parameters based on the loaded ConsultingOffering and page context.
event_handlers: onOfferingLoaded: Enable the CTA buttons if ConsultingOffering.status is active and a valid ConsultingOffering.id is present.
onStatusChange: If ConsultingOffering.status is inactive, hide or disable the CTA buttons to prevent new requests for inactive offerings.
onClickRequestProposal: Navigate to proposal_request.html with query parameters offeringId={ConsultingOffering.id} and sourceType=from_plans, matching the defined outgoing connection.

onClickBookConsultation: Navigate to book_consultation.html with query parameters offeringId={ConsultingOffering.id}, serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_offering, and sourceReferenceId={ConsultingOffering.id}, matching the defined outgoing connection.

components.functionality: Displays small context messages based on sourceType (e.g., from_plans, from_assessment) without calling additional APIs, useful for analytics labeling and user understanding.
event_handlers: onContextSourceTypeChange: When contextSourceType is from_assessment, show a message that the offering was recommended based on assessment results; when from_plans, show that the user navigated from the plans overview; when absent or other, hide or show a generic message.
onDismissBanner: Allow the user to close the banner for the current page view (only in-memory; no localStorage).

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a hero-like two-column detail header at the top, echoing the mockup: left column for text-heavy core information (offering header and structured summary), right column for a compact but visually strong CTA panel (replacing the portrait). Beneath this hero band, content flows linearly top-to-bottom in a single main column: a contextual info banner, then a rich description section, followed by a more structured pricing section. Loading and error states temporarily replace the main content area, centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a hero-like two-column detail header at the top, echoing the mockup: left column for text-heavy core information (offering header and structured summary), right column for a compact but visually strong CTA panel (replacing the portrait). Beneath this hero band, content flows linearly top-to-bottom in a single main column: a contextual info banner, then a rich description section, followed by a more structured pricing section. Loading and error states temporarily replace the main content area, centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered max-width container (~1120px–1200px) sits below the global site header. Inside this container, the top 'detail hero' zone is split roughly 65% (left informational column) and 35% (right CTA column) on desktop, mapped to something like 8/12 and 4/12 grid columns. The hero consumes most of the initial viewport height with generous top padding (64–80px) under the site nav and ample bottom padding so the fold is clean. Below the hero, the contextual banner spans the full container width, while the description and pricing sections sit in a central column spanning about 70–80% of the container with equal left/right margins for a calm editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is low and editorial: large heading, multi-line but well-spaced body copy, and clear separation between clusters using 32–64px vertical gaps. The top hero is sparse and focused: header, concise key facts, then the call-to-action panel. The description and pricing sections have comfortable line lengths and line heights, with space between paragraphs and subheadings. All spacing respects an 8px baseline grid; typical vertical gaps between related elements are 16–24px, while gaps between major sections are 64–96px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the large offering title in the left column, then picks up the smaller context label above it. From there, attention moves down through key summary points, then hops horizontally to the right CTA panel (which is visually prominent and slightly elevated). As the user scrolls, the sticky CTA panel remains a right-hand anchor while the main reading flow continues downward: contextual banner (short, high-signal), into the long-form description, and finally into the structured pricing. In loading/error states, a centered indicator or message replaces this flow, avoiding distraction. Overall, the layout maintains a Z-pattern at the top (top-left title and summary, across to the top-right CTA) and then a vertical reading pattern below.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s dark-mode global header and navigation, the Offering Detail page opens into a tall, spacious hero-like detail section that fills most of the first viewport. A centered container defines the content width. On the left, occupying roughly two-thirds of the width, a small, all-caps context label and breadcrumb sit above a bold, multi-line offering title. Directly beneath, a structured summary presents key facts in a concise, card-like block, all stacked with generous breathing room. On the right, taking the remaining one-third width, a prominent CTA panel rises to the same top alignment as the title, acting as the right-hand counterweight to the text much like the portrait in the original mockup. This panel holds the primary actions (request proposal, book consultation) inside a subtle elevated card that can stick to the viewport as the user scrolls. Below this two-column hero, the layout collapses into a more linear, editorial flow: a full-width contextual banner gives quick, situational information, followed by a wide but centered description section with long-form copy, then a distinct pricing section that visually segments cost information without feeling dense. All elements sit on a dark charcoal background with high-contrast type and ample negative space. In loading or error states, the main content region is taken over by a centered loader or a minimalist error card, but the overall structural calm and spacing remain consistent with the rest of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates entirely behind the scenes with no visual footprint. It sits logically at the top of the Offering Detail page’s component tree, resolving URL parameters before any visual content is shown. There is no dedicated space reserved for it; instead, it determines whether the loader, error message, or main layout (header, summary, CTA, etc.) is rendered into the central content container below the global site header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural component with no rendered layout; it only controls which visual components occupy the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this component occupies the entire main content region beneath the global navigation, effectively replacing the hero and all subsequent sections. A centered loading indicator (such as a spinner and short label) sits in the middle of the viewport, both horizontally and vertically, within the same max-width container. There is generous empty space around the loader (at least 96px from any surrounding content or edges), reinforcing the minimalist, focused feel. The dark charcoal background from the site is visible behind it, maintaining continuity with the rest of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily takes over the primary content area; positioned centrally with flexbox-like behavior (center-center) within the viewport, and released once the data load completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator itself is a compact, centered element within the loader state: a circular spinner or subtle animated glyph accompanied by a short status label beneath it. It sits at the exact visual center of the viewport, with at least 24–32px spacing between the icon and text. The indicator’s width is modest (around 120–200px), creating a small island of activity surrounded by ample negative space inside the dark background, aligning with the low-density, modern aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the visual center of the viewport when active; does not scroll independently—if content is taller than the viewport (rare during loading), the indicator remains centered within the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On error or not-found states, a single card-like error message occupies the center of the main content area, replacing all standard hero and detail content. The card sits inside the same max-width container, centered horizontally, and roughly mid-viewport vertically. The card width is about 40–50% of the container on desktop, ensuring a compact, focused message block surrounded by generous margins (64–96px top and bottom). Inside the card, a short, bold error title is placed at the top, a brief explanatory paragraph sits below with clear spacing (16–24px separation), and an optional link or button to return to a safe page is aligned left or centered at the bottom. The surrounding dark background keeps the focus on this light or slightly elevated card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the standard content as a central, non-sticky card; scrollable only if content exceeds viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Once data resolves successfully, the offering header appears at the top-left of the main content container, forming the left column of a two-column hero. It begins approximately 64–80px below the global nav, aligning with the mockup’s generous top padding. The header spans about 65% of the container width (roughly 8 of 12 grid columns) and runs from the left edge inward. At the top of this area is a small, all-caps context label or breadcrumb with letterspacing, followed by a large, multi-line H1 offering title with strong contrast and ample line-height. The context label and title form a tight vertical cluster with 8–16px between them. Beneath the title, a short supporting line (e.g., a one-sentence positioning statement) can appear, separated by around 16–24px. The overall height of this header block (label, title, subline) occupies the upper third of the hero area, with sufficient breathing room below it before the structured summary begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the upper-left hero zone; participates in the two-column layout on desktop and collapses to full-width stacked above the CTA panel on smaller breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits directly beneath the offering header within the same left column, maintaining its 65% width alignment. There is a clear vertical gap (24–32px) separating it from the header’s last line, preserving the sectional feeling. Visually, it behaves like a lightly elevated summary card or a clearly delineated block of metadata: key facts such as duration, ideal client type, deliverables, and outcomes. These items may be arranged in 2–3 columns within this card-like area, but the overall block height remains compact compared to the hero, occupying roughly the middle third of the visible hero area. The left edge of this block aligns perfectly with the header’s left text edge, creating a strong vertical spine. The right edge stops before the CTA panel, leaving a clean gutter between the columns.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flows under the header in the left hero column; on smaller screens it becomes a full-width card stacked beneath the header and above the CTA panel or actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The CTA panel occupies the right-hand column of the hero area, mirroring where the portrait would sit in the original mockup. It begins vertically aligned with the top of the offering header’s title, creating a clear horizontal baseline across the page. Its width is roughly 35% of the content container (about 4 of 12 grid columns) and it is pushed flush to the container’s right side, leaving a consistent gutter between it and the left column content. The panel is a visually distinct card against the dark background—slightly elevated or subtly outlined—with internal padding (24–32px on all sides). Inside, the primary action button (e.g., “Request a Proposal”) sits near the top, spanning most of the panel width, with a secondary action (“Book a Consultation”) below, separated by 12–16px. Supporting microcopy or a short reassurance line can sit beneath the buttons with 16–24px spacing. Vertically, the CTA panel extends through roughly half to two-thirds of the hero height, leaving some empty space below it so the area doesn’t feel crowded. This makes the CTA panel a strong but controlled visual counterweight to the left-side copy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar on desktop: once the user scrolls, the panel remains pinned near the top-right of the viewport until the main content (description/pricing) has scrolled past its height. On tablet/mobile, it loses the sticky behavior and stacks below the header/summary as a full-width block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This banner sits immediately below the two-column hero area, spanning the full width of the main content container. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) between the bottom edge of the hero (typically the bottom of the structured summary on the left or the CTA panel on the right, whichever is lower) and the top of this banner, preserving a clear section break. The banner is a low-profile strip with a subtle background tint or border that contrasts gently with the page’s main background while staying minimalist. It contains a single line or short block of text—such as notes about availability, typical timelines, or AI tooling context—aligned left with comfortable padding (16–24px vertically, 24–32px horizontally). The height of the banner is modest, roughly 64–80px total, functioning as a quick-read contextual highlight before the user dives into the detailed description.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar within the container; scrolls normally with the page and does not stick. On smaller screens, text reflows into 1–2 lines but the component remains a thin horizontal band between hero and body content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section appears directly beneath the contextual info banner, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 48–64px. It is centered within the page container but is slightly narrower than the full container to create an editorial reading column: roughly 70–80% of the container width, with equal left and right margins, allowing for ideal line length. A clear section heading or label sits at the top of this block, aligned with the text column, followed by multiple paragraphs of body copy. Paragraphs are spaced with 16–24px between them, and any subheadings or lists are given additional breathing room (24–32px above and 16–24px below). The background remains the standard dark page background, relying on typography, spacing, and subtle dividers rather than heavy boxes. This section can extend vertically as needed, forming the main scrollable reading experience on the page, while the CTA panel on the right stays visible (on desktop) for much of this scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrollable main content block. On desktop, it flows beneath the hero while the right-hand CTA panel may remain sticky alongside the upper portion of this section; on smaller screens, it simply follows the stacked hero and CTA content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing section follows the description section as a distinct but related block, separated from the last paragraph of description by a clear vertical gap of 64px to signal a new focus area. It occupies the same central column width as the description (about 70–80% of the container), creating a consistent reading spine. At the top, a strong section heading (e.g., “Pricing & Engagement Models”) anchors the content. Beneath it, pricing details may be arranged in one or two columns: for example, a left column listing package names and short summaries, and a right column highlighting key figures, ranges, or terms. Cards or rows within this section are spaced with 24–32px vertical gaps, and internal padding follows the 16–24px standard. While more structured than the description, it still avoids feeling dense by limiting text per item and preserving generous whitespace. The overall height depends on the number of pricing options but is visually grouped as a single section before the page transitions into any subsequent content or footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable section following the description. On desktop, it continues beneath the sticky CTA panel; once the user scrolls past the CTA’s extent, the pricing section fills the full vertical view.

name: Proposal Request
title: Proposal Request
description: Page for requesting a detailed consulting proposal for a selected offering or project estimate, with optional pre-filled context from other pages.

core_features: Read URL parameters (offeringId, estimateId, sourceType) on load to determine request context.
Call getProposalRequestPrefill with offeringId and/or estimateId to obtain pre-filled offering and estimate information when available.
Display the selected offering and/or project configuration name and summary, with the ability to edit or confirm the package/plan field.
Provide a form to collect company size (number of employees), desired project start date or month, contact name, contact email, optional company name, and project description/notes.
Validate required fields (at minimum: contactName, contactEmail; optionally enforce a valid email pattern and numeric company size when provided).
Submit the proposal request via submitProposalRequest using the current form values and sourceType derived from the URL or set as direct when no source is provided.
Show submission progress, handle success and error responses from submitProposalRequest, and display confirmation messaging when the request is successfully submitted.
Provide a 'Return to Home' navigation link to index.html after successful submission.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Request from a plans or offering detail page
1. User clicks a 'Request proposal' action on plans.html or offering_detail.html, which opens proposal_request.html with offeringId and sourceType in the URL.
2. On page load, the state manager reads offeringId and sourceType from the URL and triggers getProposalRequestPrefill with offeringId and no estimateId.
3. When getProposalRequestPrefill resolves successfully, the page displays the pre-filled offering name in a dedicated field and, if provided, any associated estimate summary; the suggestedDesiredStartDate is used to pre-fill the desired start date field.
4. The user reviews and optionally edits the offering/plan name, then fills in company size, desired start date/month (if not already suitable), contact name, contact email, optional company name, and project description.
5. The user submits the form. The page validates required fields and, if valid, calls submitProposalRequest with offeringId from URL, the current offeringName field value, estimateId (if any), and other form values, along with sourceType taken from the URL.
6. On successful response (success = true), the page switches to a confirmation state, shows confirmation messaging using the returned proposalRequest data (without hardcoded content), and displays the 'Return to Home' link.
7. The user clicks 'Return to Home', and the browser navigates to index.html without additional parameters.

Workflow 2: Request from the project estimator
1. User completes a configuration on estimator.html and selects 'Request detailed proposal', which opens proposal_request.html with estimateId, optional offeringId, and sourceType in the URL.
2. On page load, the state manager reads estimateId, offeringId (when present), and sourceType, then calls getProposalRequestPrefill with both IDs.
3. When getProposalRequestPrefill returns, the page shows the offeringName (if provided), estimateSummary, and suggestedDesiredStartDate pre-filled in the corresponding fields.
4. The user confirms or edits the offering/plan field (for example, clarifying the package name), and enters company size, contact details, company name (optional), and a project description referencing the estimator configuration if desired.
5. The user submits the form. The page validates inputs and calls submitProposalRequest using the offeringId, offeringName, estimateId, and other form values, and passes sourceType from the URL (e.g., from_estimator).
6. On successful submission, the confirmation panel shows key fields from the returned proposalRequest for reference and hides the editable form.
7. The user may then navigate back via browser controls or click 'Return to Home' to go to index.html.

Workflow 3: Direct proposal request (no prior context)
1. User reaches proposal_request.html directly (e.g., from a generic navigation link) without offeringId or estimateId in the URL; sourceType may be absent as well.
2. On page load, no prefill call is made if both offeringId and estimateId are missing, or getProposalRequestPrefill is called with empty values depending on implementation policy; in either case, the offering/plan field is presented as an editable text field with no pre-populated offeringId.
3. The user manually enters a description of the desired package/plan or project in the offering/plan name field, then completes company size, desired start date/month, contact name, contact email, optional company name, and project description.
4. The user submits the form; the page validates required fields and calls submitProposalRequest with offeringId left empty or undefined, offeringName from the form, estimateId empty or undefined, and sourceType set to direct (since no URL sourceType is present).
5. On success, the confirmation state is shown and the form is locked or hidden; the user can then navigate away via the 'Return to Home' link.

Workflow 4: Handling prefill or submission errors
1. On page load, getProposalRequestPrefill is called when there is at least one of offeringId or estimateId in the URL.
2. If getProposalRequestPrefill returns an error or fails, the state manager records the error, the page shows an informational message indicating that automatic pre-filling is unavailable, and the user is still allowed to manually enter the offering/plan name and other fields.
3. When the user submits the form and submitProposalRequest returns an error (success = false or network error), the form remains visible, and an error message is shown; the user can adjust inputs and re-submit without reloading the page.
4. If the user changes the URL parameters (for example through navigation or link-clicks with new query parameters) and the page is reloaded, the state is re-initialized from the new URL parameters and getProposalRequestPrefill is called again as needed.
interactions: On page load, parse offeringId, estimateId, and sourceType from the URL query string; if at least one of offeringId or estimateId is present, trigger getProposalRequestPrefill with these values.

When getProposalRequestPrefill succeeds, map the returned offeringId, offeringName, estimateId, estimateSummary, and suggestedDesiredStartDate into the page state and bind them to the appropriate fields (offering/plan display, estimate summary panel, desired start date field).

If getProposalRequestPrefill fails, set a prefill error state flag and keep all fields editable, with the offering/plan field empty or preserving whatever minimal information is available from the URL.
When the user types into the offering/plan field, update the local offeringName state used later in submitProposalRequest, without changing the offeringId from the URL.
When the user enters or edits company size, validate that the value is numeric (if provided) and store it as companySizeEmployees in state.

When the user sets the desired start date or month, capture it in a date-compatible format that can be passed directly as desiredStartDate to submitProposalRequest; optionally default to suggestedDesiredStartDate while allowing override.
When the user fills in contactName and contactEmail, update state; for contactEmail, run a basic email-format validation and mark the field as invalid if it does not meet the pattern.
When the user types in companyName or projectDescription, persist the values to the form state without additional side effects.
On form submit, run validation for required fields; if validation fails, prevent the submitProposalRequest call and highlight errors in the corresponding fields.

On successful validation, construct the parameters object for submitProposalRequest from the current state (offeringId from URL or state, offeringName from the editable field, estimateId from URL or state, companySizeEmployees, desiredStartDate, contactName, contactEmail, companyName, projectDescription, and sourceType from URL or default direct) and call submitProposalRequest.
While submitProposalRequest is in progress, set a submission-in-progress state to disable the submit button and prevent duplicate submissions.
When submitProposalRequest returns success, update the page to a submitted state: store the returned proposalRequest object, display confirmation messaging, and hide or lock the input form.
When submitProposalRequest returns an error or success is false, show an error message in the feedback panel, keep the form visible, and allow the user to correct inputs and retry submission.
When the user clicks the 'Return to Home' link in the confirmation state, navigate to index.html (without additional query parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is derived entirely from URL parameters and in-page interactions, without relying on localStorage or other persistent client storage. On each load of proposal_request.html, the system reads offeringId, estimateId, and sourceType from the query string. If at least one of offeringId or estimateId is present, getProposalRequestPrefill is called with these values and its response is used to populate offering-related fields (offeringId, offeringName) and estimate-related fields (estimateId, estimateSummary, suggestedDesiredStartDate). The sourceType state is initialized from the URL when present; if it is missing, sourceType defaults to direct for use in submitProposalRequest. The internal form state (companySizeEmployees, desiredStartDate, contactName, contactEmail, companyName, projectDescription) is initialized as empty and then updated via user input. This form state is not written back to the URL. When the user submits the form, submitProposalRequest is invoked with a parameter set composed of current state values. After a successful submission, the page state transitions to a submitted mode, but the URL remains unchanged; using the browser back/forward controls will trigger a full reload of proposal_request.html, which will again reconstruct state from the URL query parameters and call getProposalRequestPrefill if applicable. Navigating via the 'Return to Home' link replaces the current page with index.html, without additional parameters. No cross-page state is stored besides what is encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates overall page behavior: parses URL parameters (offeringId, estimateId, sourceType), triggers getProposalRequestPrefill when applicable, maintains shared state for prefill data, form values, loading flags, error flags, and submission status, and passes this state to other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlParamChange
onPrefillRequestStart
onPrefillSuccess
onPrefillError
onFormStateChange
onSubmitRequestStart
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitError

components.functionality: Invokes getProposalRequestPrefill using offeringId and estimateId provided by the state manager and returns prefilled context (offeringId, offeringName, estimateId, estimateSummary, suggestedDesiredStartDate) to update shared state.
event_handlers: onInit
onReloadPrefill
onPrefillLoaded
onPrefillFailed

components.functionality: Displays the current offering/plan information and, when available, the associated estimate summary. Shows offeringName as an editable or confirmable field bound to state, and displays estimateSummary in a read-only area. Reflects whether data came from getProposalRequestPrefill or was manually entered.
event_handlers: onOfferingNameChange
onOfferingNameBlur

components.functionality: Collects all user inputs required to submit a proposal request: companySizeEmployees, desiredStartDate, contactName, contactEmail, companyName, and projectDescription. Performs validation (required fields and basic format checks) before delegating submission to the state manager, which calls submitProposalRequest.
event_handlers: onFieldChange
onFieldBlur
onValidate
onSubmit

components.functionality: Shows dynamic feedback based on submission state: in-progress indicators during submitProposalRequest, error messages when submission fails, and confirmation messaging when it succeeds. When successful, it can also present selected, non-sensitive fields from the returned proposalRequest object for user reference.
event_handlers: onSubmissionStateChange
onDismissError

components.functionality: Activated when submitProposalRequest returns success. Replaces or overlays the form with a confirmation state that indicates the request has been received and uses data from the returned proposalRequest object to provide contextual confirmation details. Exposes the navigation control to return to the home page.
event_handlers: onEnterConfirmation
onViewDetailsToggle

components.functionality: Provides a clickable control, visible in the confirmation state, that navigates the user to index.html when activated.
event_handlers: onClick

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same two-column hero logic from the mockup. The left column becomes the main interaction area: small all-caps context label, large H1 ('Request a Proposal'), a concise supporting paragraph, then the proposal request form stacked vertically. Inline feedback (errors, status) appears just below the intro copy and above the first form field. The right column is a single, focused panel summarizing the proposal context (offering details, prefilled info). Once the form is successfully submitted, the left column content switches to a confirmation view in the same footprint, with the return-home link positioned at the bottom of that panel. Non-visual components (state manager, loader) orchestrate which left-column panel is visible and how loading states are displayed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same two-column hero logic from the mockup. The left column becomes the main interaction area: small all-caps context label, large H1 ('Request a Proposal'), a concise supporting paragraph, then the proposal request form stacked vertically. Inline feedback (errors, status) appears just below the intro copy and above the first form field. The right column is a single, focused panel summarizing the proposal context (offering details, prefilled info). Once the form is successfully submitted, the left column content switches to a confirmation view in the same footprint, with the return-home link positioned at the bottom of that panel. Non-visual components (state manager, loader) orchestrate which left-column panel is visible and how loading states are displayed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a centered max-width container (~1120–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid. On desktop, allocate roughly 60–65% of the width (7–8 columns) to the left column (form/confirmation content) and 35–40% (4–5 columns) to the right summary panel. The main content block (left + right) starts beneath the global header with generous top padding (~64px) and bottom padding (~80–96px), visually forming a dark hero-like section that can extend beyond the initial viewport if the form is tall. The right summary panel stays relatively compact in height compared to the form, aligning to the top of the left column content and optionally staying visible as the user scrolls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and airy. Use the 8px base grid with vertical gaps of 24–32px between logical blocks (context label vs H1 vs paragraph vs form) and 16–24px between form groups. Buttons get 12–16px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding. The summary panel has generous internal padding (24–32px) and clean grouping of data points with 8–16px spacing between lines. Only one major interactive mode is visible at a time (form vs confirmation), reducing clutter. Feedback/status panels are concise and appear in a single horizontal band, not stacked alerts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye should first catch the large white H1 in the left column, then move slightly up to the compact all-caps context label above it. Next, attention moves down to the short explanatory paragraph, then continues into the form fields, finally landing on the primary submit CTA at the bottom of the left column. In parallel, a quick horizontal glance to the right reveals the proposal summary panel, which serves as a consistent contextual anchor while filling in the form. After submission, the flow restarts in the same left area, with a confirmation headline and explanation, then down to the return-home link and any secondary actions. The loader and feedback messaging appear near the top of the form area so status is seen immediately within the primary visual path.

layout.overall_description: The Proposal Request page sits on a dark, charcoal-toned background that spans the full viewport, echoing a dark-mode hero. A slim global header band is fixed at the very top (brand left, navigation center-right, social icons far right), but the main focus is the large content block directly beneath it. This block uses a two-column layout centered on the page within a ~1200px-wide container. On the left, occupying about two-thirds of the width, a slim all-caps label like 'PROPOSAL REQUEST' sits at the top, followed by a bold, multi-line white H1 that takes visual precedence. A brief, softer-weight paragraph sits below, separated by generous vertical spacing, introducing what the proposal request will cover. Immediately under this intro, a horizontal feedback strip appears when needed (for errors or status), and then the form fields cascade down the page in well-spaced groups, ending with a prominent primary submit button aligned to the left. On the right, a slightly inset, elevated summary panel occupies the remaining one-third of the width, aligning vertically with the top of the left column’s headline. This panel appears as a sleek card floating over the dark background, with comfortable padding and a structured summary of the selected service and any prefilled context. As the user scrolls through the form, the right summary panel remains in view (sticky) on larger screens, while on smaller screens it stacks above the form as a full-width block. While data is loading, a subtle inline loader appears near the top of the left column and/or as a skeleton state within the summary panel, without hijacking the entire viewport. After the user submits successfully, the left column’s form area transitions into a confirmation view: a new headline, concise explanation of next steps, and at the bottom-left of this panel, a return-home link styled as a subtle, text-based secondary action. Overall, the screen feels modern and deliberate, with a clear left-to-right flow between task and context, and ample breathing room around each content cluster.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state manager is a non-visible, structural layer that effectively wraps the entire main content region beneath the global header. Conceptually, it spans the full width of the central content container (the same width as the left and right columns combined) and controls which major panel is rendered in the left column at any given time. In its default state, it shows the proposal intro + feedback panel + request form in the left column and the summary panel in the right column. On successful submission, it hides the form and feedback panel, replacing that same left-column footprint with the confirmation view while keeping the summary panel on the right. During loading or prefill operations, it toggles the visibility of the data-loader and adjusts the summary panel and form fields to their loading states. Although it has no direct visual footprint, it defines the macro layout transitions within the hero-like section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as an invisible controller for conditional rendering across the entire main content block; it does not occupy its own space but determines whether the left column shows the form stack or the confirmation view, and when the loader and feedback panel are visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the prefill loader appears within the top portion of the left column, directly below the supporting paragraph and above the feedback panel and form fields. It takes the form of a slim, full-width horizontal strip aligned to the left column, spanning roughly 100% of that column’s width and about 40–56px in height. This strip contains a small spinner or pulsing indicator aligned to the left and a brief status message like 'Preparing your proposal details…'. Simultaneously, the right-side proposal-summary-panel can show a skeleton state: muted blocks where text would appear, but still within the same card boundaries. The loader itself respects the 8px grid, with 16px top and bottom padding and 20–24px left padding inside the left column. On larger screens, it stays inline and does not cover the headline or navigation; on smaller screens, it still sits between the intro text and the form/summary, preserving clear vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and transient; appears as a narrow band within the left column content stack without shifting the overall layout structure. It does not overlay or dim the full page, and disappears once prefill data is loaded, allowing the form and summary card to expand into that space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proposal summary panel occupies the right column of the main content area and mimics the visual weight of the portrait in the original hero. On desktop, it sits flush to the right edge of the central container, spanning roughly 35–40% of the total content width (about 4–5 out of 12 grid columns) and extending down as a medium-height card, generally not as tall as the full form. It aligns vertically so that its top edge is level with the baseline of the left-column headline or slightly below the all-caps context label, creating a cohesive horizontal line. The card has generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and a subtle elevation or border to separate it from the charcoal background, while still adhering to the dark-mode aesthetic (e.g., slightly lighter dark panel with crisp white type and one accent color for key labels). Content inside is arranged in a vertical stack: a small uppercase label ('Summary'), a concise title of the selected offering, then a list of key details (scope, rough timeline, source page) with 8–16px gaps between each line or group. On tablets and mobile, this panel stacks above the form, becoming a full-width block that appears immediately below the intro text and any loader/feedback messaging, maintaining at least 24–32px of vertical separation from adjacent elements. On tall forms with scrolling, the panel is visually anchored near the top-right and behaves as a sticky element, remaining in view while the left column scrolls past it (until the end of the main content).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned, sticky card within the main hero-like section on larger screens; it remains in place within the viewport as the user scrolls the left-column form (subject to container boundaries). On smaller screens, it becomes a standard block-level section stacked above the form, scrolling naturally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proposal request form is the dominant element in the left column and inherits the hero’s editorial flow. Starting just below the supporting paragraph (and loader/feedback strip when present), it spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the overall container, equivalent to 7–8 grid columns). Form fields are arranged in a single vertical stack with clear groupings: for example, project overview, company details, scope/timeline, budget, and any additional comments. Each section is separated by 24–32px of vertical space, with individual fields within a section separated by 16–24px, all aligned to the 8px grid. Labels sit directly above their corresponding input elements, left-aligned, using smaller, medium-weight text; inputs stretch to near the full width of the column but leave comfortable side padding within the main container. Where side-by-side fields are appropriate (e.g., first/last name, city/country), they occupy two equal halves of the left column, but still maintain the overall airy feel and never exceed the column boundaries. At the bottom of the form, a horizontal row contains the primary submit button aligned to the left and, if needed, a secondary text action (such as 'Cancel') to its right, separated by 16–24px. Vertical spacing above this button row (24–32px from the last input) ensures it feels like a distinct call-to-action band. On smaller screens, all fields automatically stack full-width with preserved spacing, and the column effectively becomes 100% width within the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the left column; it expands vertically as needed and pushes the bottom of the hero-like section downward. It is conditionally rendered by the state manager, disappearing when the confirmation view takes over the same left-column footprint after a successful submission.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission feedback panel appears as a slim, full-width band within the left column, positioned immediately below the supporting paragraph and any active loader, and directly above the first form field. It spans 100% of the left column width, with 16–20px vertical padding and 20–24px horizontal padding. The background is slightly contrasted against the charcoal hero background (for example a desaturated dark tone with a subtle colored border on the left edge) to indicate status without overwhelming the page. Text inside is concise, in a single or double line, aligned to the left and following the same max line length as the form. There is at least 16px of vertical spacing between this panel and the content above and below, so it reads as a distinct yet lightweight notification strip in the main visual path. When not needed, it collapses completely, allowing the form to sit closer to the paragraph while still maintaining a comfortable 24–32px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, transient band within the left column; appears and disappears without affecting the right summary panel. It does not float or overlay; instead it reflows the vertical stack just above the form fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful submission, the confirmation view replaces the entire form stack in the left column while preserving the overall layout rhythm. It occupies the same width as the form (about 60–65% of the central container) and begins at the same vertical position below the supporting paragraph and/or feedback panel (if the design chooses to show a final success message there). At the top of this panel, a new headline—slightly smaller than the hero H1 but still prominent—communicates success (e.g., 'Your proposal request is in'). This is followed by a concise paragraph or two outlining what happens next (response timeframe, contact method), spaced 16–24px below the headline. Below that, there may be a compact list of key details (reference ID, summary of request) arranged with 8–16px vertical gaps. Comprehensive padding is maintained: 24–32px from the supporting paragraph above, and 24–32px between each content block within the confirmation view. Near the bottom, after a modest gap, sits the row of follow-up actions where the return-home link will live. The overall confirmation block may feel slightly more vertically condensed than the form, often fitting within the initial viewport height on desktop, creating a sense of closure. The right summary panel remains visible and in the same position, now reinforcing what was requested rather than guiding the input.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same left-column footprint as the form and is conditionally shown instead of it. It scrolls with the page but is generally short enough to sit comfortably within a single viewport on desktop. No sticky behavior; the right summary panel retains its sticky behavior independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The return-home link is positioned within the lower portion of the confirmation view’s left-column panel. It appears as a text-based secondary action aligned to the left edge of the content, beneath the main confirmation text and any summary details. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the preceding content block and this link, giving it room to breathe but keeping it clearly related to the confirmation message.
