On desktop, it may appear at the start of a horizontal row of secondary actions (for example, 'Return home' on the left, with any additional text links or subtle buttons to the right). The link text is clearly legible and uses an accent color consistent with the brand, but its visual weight is lighter than a primary button. On smaller screens, it remains left-aligned and full-width, effectively sitting as the final line of interactive content before the end of the section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the confirmation view, not fixed or sticky. It stays anchored to the bottom segment of the confirmation content stack and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Case Studies
title: Case Studies
description: The Case Studies page lets users search, filter, and review AI-focused business case studies, see featured items, and manage a reading list of saved case studies.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options for industries, years, ROI thresholds, and project duration categories using getCaseStudyFilterOptions, and render them as interactive filters.
Search and filter case studies using searchCaseStudies with parameters derived from the current URL (industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, textQuery, sortBy).
Display case study results as a list of cards showing key metadata from CaseStudy plus the reading-list status (isSavedToReadingList) from searchCaseStudies.
Provide controls to adjust filters, perform text search, and change sorting; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new searchCaseStudies call.
Allow users to open a detailed case study view by navigating to content_detail.html with contentType case_study and contentId equal to the selected CaseStudy.id.
Enable users to save a case study to their Reading List by calling saveCaseStudyToReadingList and reflect the saved state in the UI using the returned SavedItem and the isSavedToReadingList flag.
Provide navigation to the Saved Items page (saved_items.html) pre-filtered to the reading_list by adding listType=reading_list to the URL.
Display a featured case studies module using getFeaturedCaseStudies, giving quick access to highlighted CaseStudy items with the same navigation and saving behaviors.
Support deep-linking and shareable URLs by keeping all filter and search state in URL parameters and restoring the state on page load or browser navigation.
user_workflows: Browse and filter case studies: 1) User opens case_studies.html (optionally with URL parameters such as industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent). 2) The page controller reads URL parameters and calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to populate filter controls. 3) The page controller calls searchCaseStudies using the parameters found in the URL. 4) The results list displays the returned case studies with their metadata and isSavedToReadingList status. 5) The user adjusts filters (industry, year range, ROI threshold, duration category), triggers a search, the URL is updated, and searchCaseStudies is called again with the new parameters.

Search by text and sort results: 1) The user types a query into the text search input linked to textQuery. 2) On submission, the page updates the URL to include textQuery and optionally sortBy. 3) The page calls searchCaseStudies with updated textQuery and sortBy along with any existing filters. 4) The results list re-renders with the new ordering and filtered set.

Open a case study detail: 1) From the results list or featured module, the user clicks on a case study card or its View details link. 2) The click handler reads the caseStudyId and navigates to content_detail.html with URL parameters contentType=case_study and contentId set to the selected CaseStudy.id. 3) The destination page uses those parameters to display the full case study content.

Save a case study to the Reading List: 1) In the results list or featured module, the user clicks a Save to Reading List control on a case study that is not yet saved (isSavedToReadingList=false). 2) The page calls saveCaseStudyToReadingList with caseStudyId set to the CaseStudy.id and optional notes (if provided by the user). 3) On success, the UI updates the item’s saved status locally (e.g., isSavedToReadingList becomes true) and may reflect metadata from the returned SavedItem. 4) Subsequent searches or reloads will receive updated isSavedToReadingList flags from searchCaseStudies.

View and refine from a campaign deep link: 1) The user arrives from index.html or another page via a link to case_studies.html that includes preset filters in the URL (for example, industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent). 2) The page controller reads these parameters, initializes the filter controls to match them, and calls searchCaseStudies with those values. 3) The user can then further refine filters or search text; updates are written back to the URL and trigger new calls to searchCaseStudies.

Review saved items via navigation: 1) The user clicks a View Reading List button or a Saved Items link visible from the Case Studies page. 2) The click triggers navigation to saved_items.html with listType=reading_list in the URL. 3) The Saved Items page then displays the reading list, which will include items saved via saveCaseStudyToReadingList from this page.

Explore featured case studies: 1) On page load, the controller calls getFeaturedCaseStudies with a configured maxItems value to populate a featured module. 2) The featured module displays the returned CaseStudy items with metadata and Save/View detail actions. 3) User interactions (view details, save to reading list) behave the same as in the main results list and use the same navigation and saveCaseStudyToReadingList calls.
interactions: Filter controls interaction: When the user changes any filter (industry, yearFrom/yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory), the filter form updates the URL parameters to reflect the current selection and triggers a new searchCaseStudies call with those parameters.

Text search interaction: When the user submits the text search input, the component updates the textQuery parameter in the URL, optionally resets other parameters as configured (e.g., removing previous textQuery), and invokes searchCaseStudies with textQuery plus any currently active filter parameters.
Sort interaction: When the user chooses a sort option, the sort control updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and triggers searchCaseStudies with the updated sortBy value.

Clear filters interaction: When the user uses a Clear or Reset action on the filters, the component removes corresponding parameters (industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, and optionally textQuery and sortBy) from the URL, then calls searchCaseStudies without those parameters.

Result card navigation interaction: When the user clicks the case study title area or a dedicated View details link in a card, the click handler redirects the browser to content_detail.html with contentType set to case_study and contentId set to the CaseStudy.id for that card.

Save to reading list interaction: When the user clicks the Save control for a card whose isSavedToReadingList is false, the card triggers saveCaseStudyToReadingList(caseStudyId, notes). On success, the card updates its local saved state and adjusts any visual indicator (for example, disabling the Save control or changing its label).

Saved state indicator interaction: Cards use the isSavedToReadingList boolean from searchCaseStudies to conditionally display a Saved indicator and to disable or change the behavior of the Save control. No additional API calls are made just for status; the state is derived from searchCaseStudies and updated in response to saveCaseStudyToReadingList.
View Reading List navigation interaction: When the user clicks a View Reading List link on the page, the click handler navigates to saved_items.html with listType=reading_list in the query string.

Featured module interaction: When the user interacts with a featured case study (either opening details or saving), the same navigation and saveCaseStudyToReadingList logic as in the main results list is used.

URL/back-forward navigation interaction: When the user uses the browser’s back or forward buttons, the page controller listens for navigation events, reads the current URL parameters, and re-applies them to the filter components and searchCaseStudies call so that the view always matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query parameters are the single source of truth for the page’s filter, search, and sort state. On initial load, the page controller reads the following parameters if present: industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, textQuery, and sortBy. These values are used to initialize the filter controls and are passed directly into searchCaseStudies. If a parameter is absent, it is simply omitted from the searchCaseStudies call. When the user changes a filter, enters a search query, or changes sort order, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters (using browser history APIs) and then calls searchCaseStudies with the updated values. The results from searchCaseStudies, including CaseStudy data and isSavedToReadingList flags, are stored in page state and rendered by the results list. The getCaseStudyFilterOptions response is stored in page state for populating filter controls; it is independent of the current URL state but influences which filter options are visible. The getFeaturedCaseStudies response is stored separately and is not affected by the current URL filter state unless the caller chooses to vary its maxItems or call conditions. The saveCaseStudyToReadingList interface does not change URL parameters; instead, it updates the state of the affected case study cards directly. Any subsequent call to searchCaseStudies will return the updated isSavedToReadingList flags, keeping the UI consistent with saved state. Deep links coming from index.html or other pages simply pre-populate the URL parameters; the state logic treats them the same as user-driven changes.

components.functionality: Central controller that initializes the page, synchronizes URL parameters with internal state, orchestrates calls to getCaseStudyFilterOptions, searchCaseStudies, and getFeaturedCaseStudies, and distributes data to child components.
data_binding: URLParameters(industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, textQuery, sortBy)
searchCaseStudies(industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, textQuery, sortBy)
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, read URL parameters, call getCaseStudyFilterOptions, then call searchCaseStudies with parameters from the URL, and call getFeaturedCaseStudies with a configured maxItems value.
onURLChange: When the URL changes due to browser navigation, re-read parameters and re-call searchCaseStudies so the state matches the URL.
onFiltersUpdated: When the filter bar reports updated filter values, update the URL accordingly and call searchCaseStudies with the new parameters.
onSearchSubmitted: When the search input is submitted, update textQuery (and possibly other related parameters) in the URL and call searchCaseStudies.
onSortChanged: When sortBy changes, update the URL and call searchCaseStudies.

onSaveSuccess: When saveCaseStudyToReadingList returns success, update the in-memory representation of the relevant case study (e.g., isSavedToReadingList=true) so that dependent components re-render correctly.

components.functionality: Displays and manages all filter inputs (industry, year range, ROI threshold, project duration category) and connects them to URL parameters and searchCaseStudies via the page controller.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize control values based on URLParameters so the filters reflect the current state when the page loads.
onIndustryChange: When the user selects a different industry, emit an event with the new industry value so the page controller can update the URL and call searchCaseStudies.
onYearRangeChange: When the user adjusts yearFrom or yearTo, emit updated year values for URL and searchCaseStudies.
onRoiThresholdChange: When the user selects a new ROI threshold, emit the new minRoiPercent for URL and searchCaseStudies.
onDurationCategoryChange: When the user selects a project duration category, emit the new projectDurationCategory for URL and searchCaseStudies.
onClearFilters: When the user clears filters, emit an event indicating that all filter-related parameters should be removed from the URL and that searchCaseStudies should be called without them.

components.functionality: Provides a text input for searching case studies by keywords and maps the user input to the textQuery parameter used by searchCaseStudies.
event_handlers: onInit: Set the input value from URLParameters(textQuery) if present.
onChange: Update an internal value as the user types without immediately triggering a search (unless configured otherwise).
onSubmit: When the user submits the form, emit the current query so the page controller can update textQuery in the URL and call searchCaseStudies.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose the sorting mode for case study results, mapping the selection to the sortBy parameter for searchCaseStudies.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize the selected sort option based on URLParameters(sortBy).
onSortOptionChange: When the user selects a different sort option, emit the new sortBy value so the page controller can update the URL and invoke searchCaseStudies.

components.functionality: Displays a list of case study cards based on the searchCaseStudies response, including reading list status, and wires up navigation and saving actions.
event_handlers: onCardViewDetailsClick(caseStudyId): Navigate to content_detail.html with contentType=case_study and contentId=caseStudyId.

onCardSaveClick(caseStudyId, optionalNotes): Request saving the case study by calling saveCaseStudyToReadingList and, on success, notify the page controller so that the local isSavedToReadingList state can be updated.
onResultsUpdated: When new results arrive from searchCaseStudies, re-render the list and any related metadata such as totalCount.

components.functionality: Represents a single case study within the results list or featured module, showing metadata and interactive controls (view details, save to reading list) driven by CaseStudy data and isSavedToReadingList.
event_handlers: onTitleClick: Trigger navigation to content_detail.html with contentType=case_study and contentId set to this CaseStudy.id.
onViewDetailsClick: Same behavior as onTitleClick, providing an explicit action link.

onSaveClick: If isSavedToReadingList is false, call saveCaseStudyToReadingList with this CaseStudy.id and optional notes, then inform parent components of a successful save so they can update isSavedToReadingList.
onSavedStateChanged: When the saved state is updated by parent components (e.g., after a successful API call), re-render the card to reflect the new state.

components.functionality: Displays a subset of featured case studies returned from getFeaturedCaseStudies and allows the same view and save actions as the main results list.
data_binding: DerivedSavedStateForFeaturedItems(isSavedToReadingList, where available from subsequent searchCaseStudies results or internal state)
event_handlers: onInit: Request featured case studies from the page controller once getFeaturedCaseStudies data is available.
onFeaturedViewDetailsClick(caseStudyId): Navigate to content_detail.html with contentType=case_study and contentId=caseStudyId.
onFeaturedSaveClick(caseStudyId, optionalNotes): Call saveCaseStudyToReadingList for the selected case study and update the local saved state for the corresponding featured item.
onFeaturedSavedStateSync: When main search results update saved states for case studies that also appear in this module, synchronize the featured item’s saved indicator.

components.functionality: Provides a direct navigation control from the Case Studies page to the Saved Items page focused on the reading list.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate to saved_items.html with the query parameter listType=reading_list.

components.functionality: Responsible for keeping the browser URL query string in sync with the current filter, search, and sort state, and for re-applying that state when the URL changes.
data_binding: URLParameters(industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory, textQuery, sortBy)
event_handlers: onFilterStateChange: When the filter bar reports a change, update the relevant URL parameters (industry, yearFrom, yearTo, minRoiPercent, projectDurationCategory) and notify the page controller to call searchCaseStudies.
onSearchStateChange: When the search input is submitted, set or update textQuery in the URL and notify the page controller to call searchCaseStudies.
onSortStateChange: When the sort control changes, set or update sortBy in the URL and notify the page controller to call searchCaseStudies.

onPopState: When the browser history state changes (back/forward), read the updated URL parameters and instruct the filter bar, search input, and sort control to update their displayed values, then notify the page controller to call searchCaseStudies with the new parameters.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The Case Studies page adopts the hero-like two-column structure from the mockup as a page-level controller section. Directly below the global header, a dark hero band presents the page context and controls: on the left, a vertical stack of small context label, large H1, supporting paragraph, and a primary search input; on the right, a visually strong featured case studies module occupies a single block. Beneath this band, a full-width sticky controls row holds filters on the left and sort on the right. Below that, the results list occupies most of the page, presenting case-study cards in a spacious grid/list. The reading list link aligns into the existing top navigation on the far right, matching the header pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The Case Studies page adopts the hero-like two-column structure from the mockup as a page-level controller section. Directly below the global header, a dark hero band presents the page context and controls: on the left, a vertical stack of small context label, large H1, supporting paragraph, and a primary search input; on the right, a visually strong featured case studies module occupies a single block. Beneath this band, a full-width sticky controls row holds filters on the left and sort on the right. Below that, the results list occupies most of the page, presenting case-study cards in a spacious grid/list. The reading list link aligns into the existing top navigation on the far right, matching the header pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered container around 1100–1200px wide with generous side padding (~48–64px on large screens). Within the hero band, roughly 60–65% of the width is dedicated to the left column (page title, description, search), and 35–40% to the right column (featured case studies). Vertically, the hero/controller section takes up about 60–70% of the initial viewport height, with the header taking a slim band at the top and the results area extending below the fold. The filters/sort row forms a low-profile strip separating the hero from the results.
layout_strategies.content_density: Following the mockup, content is airy and editorial. Each vertical cluster (hero text, search input, filters bar, results list, individual cards) is separated by clear gaps in the 24–48px range. The search bar and feature module are large and uncluttered, with minimal decoration. Case-study cards are not tightly packed; there is visible whitespace between cards and around the results list edges, avoiding a cramped, table-like feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first hits the large white H1 in the left hero column, then moves slightly up to register the smaller all-caps context label. Next, the gaze flows down to the supporting paragraph and then to the prominent search input, which acts as the primary interactive CTA. The featured case studies module on the right provides a strong horizontal counterbalance, drawing attention across the page. Above all this, the header and the reading list nav link participate in a subtle Z-pattern: brand and navigation at top-left across to the reading list link at top-right, then down diagonally to the hero headline, across to the featured module, and finally down into the filters/sort bar and the scrolling results list.

layout.overall_description: The Case Studies page sits on a dark, charcoal-toned background with a centered content column framed by generous side padding. At the top, the global header spans the full width, with brand and navigation on the left/center and a dedicated Reading List link on the far right. Immediately below, a hero-like controller section fills most of the initial viewport: on the left, a narrow all-caps label introduces the section above a large, multi-line H1 and a short, high-contrast paragraph explaining the case studies offering. Beneath this text, a wide search input behaves like the primary CTA, visually anchored in the lower portion of the left column. On the right, a tall featured case studies module occupies about one-third of the width, visually echoing the portrait in the original hero but now filled with key case studies instead of a person. Below this band, a slim, sticky control row spans the container: filters fan out from the left, while the sort control snaps to the right. Under this bar, the main results list scrolls vertically, showing case-study cards arranged in a clean grid or single column depending on screen size, each separated by generous whitespace. The overall effect is a modern, minimalist, consulting-style layout where the hero-like controller section and search take center stage, with supporting controls and content arranged in a clear, top-down reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-width dark background and a centered content container that follows a 12-column grid with a max width of roughly 1100–1200px. It sits directly beneath the global header, leaving a vertical gap of about 48–64px before the hero/controller section begins. Inside this container, the first major block is the hero-like controller band that occupies about 60–70% of the initial viewport height. This band is split into a left column occupying about 7–8 of the 12 grid columns and a right column taking the remaining 4–5 columns. The left column stacks the context label, H1, supporting paragraph, and the search input with 16–32px gaps between each. The right column hosts the featured case studies module, aligned to the top. Beneath this hero band, the page controller introduces a full-width sticky controls row (filters and sort) and then the results list, both confined to the same centered content width and respecting the 8px spacing system (24–32px gaps between major sections).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static structural wrapper for the page content, managing the two-column hero band, the sticky filters/sort bar, and the scrolling results area within a centered, max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filters bar appears as a horizontal band directly below the hero/controller section and above the results list, spanning the full width of the centered content container. Vertically, it is a slim strip with about 16–24px top and bottom padding, visually separated from the hero by a 24–32px gap. Within this band, filter controls (chips, dropdowns, or toggles) are left-aligned and occupy roughly the left two-thirds of the row. Filters are arranged in a single horizontal line on larger screens and wrap to a second line if needed, maintaining at least 8–16px between elements. The rightmost third of the row is reserved for the sort control, which aligns to the far right, leaving clear whitespace between filters and sort. The overall bar is visually subtle but distinct (e.g., slightly lighter/darker strip than the background) so it reads as a control header for the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the main content area: when the user scrolls down, the filters bar remains fixed at the top of the viewport just below the global header, ensuring filters are always accessible while the results list scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search input is positioned inside the left column of the hero/controller section, forming the final element in the vertical content stack after the context label, H1, and supporting paragraph. It is aligned to the left grid edge of the column and spans approximately 70–80% of that column’s width, making it visually substantial without touching the right edge. There is a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px between the paragraph and the search input, reinforcing its role as the primary action on the page. Below the input, a smaller gap of 16–24px separates it from the bottom of the hero band. The search field itself is tall enough to feel like a button (e.g., 48px height), with ample horizontal padding to match the minimalist, corporate style. On mobile, the search input becomes full-width and remains the main CTA within the top section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left hero column; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page while its results are further controlled via the sticky filters/sort bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control lives within the filters/sort horizontal band beneath the hero section. It is right-aligned within this row, sitting flush with the right edge of the content container. The control itself is compact (e.g., a dropdown or segmented switch) with 8–12px of padding around its label. On wider screens it occupies roughly 20–30% of the row’s width, leaving sufficient whitespace to its left so it doesn’t crowd the filters. It aligns vertically centered relative to the filters bar height, and on smaller screens it may wrap onto a second line below the filters but remains right-aligned, preserving the left-to-right flow from filters to sort.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky together with the filters bar: remains attached to the top of the viewport (under the global header) while the results list scrolls, ensuring sorting is always accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list sits directly below the filters/sort band, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content container, with consistent left and right margins that align with the grid used in the hero section. On desktop, the list presents case-study cards in a grid-like layout with one or two columns depending on the card design: either a single column of wide cards spanning 8–10 grid columns with ample side margins, or a two-column layout where each card occupies about 5–6 grid columns, with 24–32px gutters between cards. Vertically, cards are separated by 24–32px to maintain a low-density, editorial feel. The list extends downward and becomes the primary scrollable content area of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally within the page, beneath the sticky filters/sort bar. On smaller screens, it automatically reflows to a single-column list to maintain readability and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each case-study card is a self-contained block within the results list, aligning to the underlying grid. On larger screens, a card either spans nearly the full content width in a single-column layout or half the width in a two-column grid, always maintaining at least 24px of horizontal and vertical whitespace around it. Inside the card, elements follow a top-to-bottom hierarchy: a subtle category or industry label at the top, a bold title taking one or two lines, a brief summary paragraph, and a bottom row with metadata (e.g., time to read, industry tags) and a control to add/remove the case study from the reading list. Internal padding is generous—typically 16–24px on all sides—reflecting the 8px grid. Cards sit on a slightly lifted surface (e.g., subtle border or elevation) against the dark background, but the overall look remains minimalist and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static child elements within the scrollable results list; they line up according to the responsive grid and adjust from two columns to one column as the viewport narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured case studies module occupies the right column of the hero/controller section, visually mirroring the portrait area from the original mockup. It is aligned to the top of this section and spans about 35–40% of the content width (roughly 4–5 grid columns). Vertically, it matches or slightly exceeds the height of the left column’s content stack, creating a balanced rectangular block that visually anchors the right side of the hero band. Inside the module, one to three featured case studies are arranged vertically with ample spacing (16–24px) between them, possibly with a highlighted primary feature at the top and smaller entries below. The module has its own internal padding (16–24px) and a subtle visual frame (e.g., border or soft shadow) to distinguish it from the background, making it a strong but not overwhelming focal point. On smaller screens, this module stacks below the left column’s content, maintaining a clear separation with a 32–40px vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the hero/controller band; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down to the filters and results. On narrow viewports, it reflows to a full-width block beneath the hero text and search input.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list nav link is placed in the global header’s navigation area at the top of the page, aligned toward the far right to match the social/utility pattern from the mockup. It sits on the same baseline as other nav items but is visually set apart—either by an icon plus label (e.g., a bookmark or list icon) or by a pill-style button treatment—without overpowering the brand logo. The link has horizontal padding of about 12–16px and vertical padding of 8–12px, consistent with other nav elements. It may include a small badge count positioned at the top-right corner of the icon or label, indicating the number of saved items, which subtly draws attention. In the overall layout, it participates in the top horizontal Z-pattern, serving as the final call to action on the header’s right edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the global header, which is typically sticky at the very top of the viewport. As users scroll the page, the reading list nav link remains visible, providing quick access to the reading list from any point on the Case Studies page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and embedded within the page controller’s logic rather than occupying any visible space on the canvas. Conceptually, it sits behind the filters bar, search input, sort control, and results list, listening to user interactions and reflecting the combined state (search query, selected filters, sort order, and pagination) in the page URL. It ensures that when a user refreshes, shares, or returns to a URL, the hero search field, filters bar, sort control, and visible results all rehydrate to match the encoded state, creating a seamless experience without any visible UI element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-layout-affecting; it operates in the background to bind the UI controls to the URL and browser history while the visible layout behaves as described.

name: Content Detail
title: Content Detail
description: Displays a single case study or blog post with all metadata, and lets users save it to their Reading List or Reading Queue and navigate back to the originating list.

core_features: Load and render a single content item (case study or blog post) based on URL parameters using getContentDetail.
Display content-type-specific metadata: industry, client size, ROI, project duration, and related metrics for case studies; publish date, topic tags, and estimated read time for blog posts.
Show the full body of the content, with summary fallback when needed.
Provide a primary action to add a case study to the Reading List or a blog post to the Reading Queue, using saveCaseStudyToReadingList or saveBlogPostToReadingQueue.
Optionally collect and submit user notes when saving an item.
Indicate whether the item is already saved (using isSavedToReadingList / isSavedToReadingQueue) and adjust actions accordingly.
Offer navigation back to the originating list (Case Studies, Blog, or Saved Items) based on URL parameters and content type.
Provide navigation to the Saved Items page after an item has been saved.
user_workflows:
View a case study from Case Studies list:
1. User clicks a case study in case_studies.html; browser navigates to content_detail.html with contentType=case_study, contentId (or slug), and sourcePage=case_studies.
2. Content Detail reads URL parameters and calls getContentDetail with contentType and contentId or slug.
3. Page shows loading state, then renders the case study title, metadata (including ROI and duration where available), and full body.
4. Page shows a primary action labeled 'Add to Reading List' (or a saved state if already in the Reading List).
5. User can click 'Add to Reading List', optionally enter notes, and submit; on success, the page updates saved state and shows a 'View Saved Items' navigation option.
6. User can click 'Back to Case Studies' to return to case_studies.html, preserving any list-level state via that page's own URL parameters.

View a blog post from Blog list:
1. User clicks a blog item in blog.html; browser navigates to content_detail.html with contentType=blog_post, contentId (or slug), and sourcePage=blog.
2. Content Detail calls getContentDetail accordingly.
3. Page renders the blog title, publish date, topic category, tags, and estimated read time, plus full body.
4. Page shows a primary action labeled 'Add to Reading Queue' (or saved state if already in the Reading Queue).
5. User can add the post to the Reading Queue with optional notes; on success, saved state is updated and 'View Saved Items' navigation is available.
6. User can click 'Back to Blog' to return to blog.html.

Open content from Saved Items:
1. User clicks a saved case study or blog post in saved_items.html; browser navigates to content_detail.html with contentType, contentId, and sourcePage=saved_items.
2. Content Detail calls getContentDetail and renders the corresponding content.
3. Page detects the current saved status from isSavedToReadingList / isSavedToReadingQueue and reflects it (e.g., button disabled or marked as saved).
4. User can review or update notes only when re-saving (if such UI is exposed) or just read the content.
5. User can click a context-aware back link to return to saved_items.html.

Access content via index or direct link:
1. User clicks a case study or blog teaser on index.html; browser navigates to content_detail.html with contentType and slug (sourcePage may be omitted or set to index).
2. Content Detail calls getContentDetail with the slug.
3. Page renders the content and saved state.
4. Back navigation defaults based on contentType when sourcePage does not map to a list page (e.g., 'Back to Case Studies' for case_study, 'Back to Blog' for blog_post), or stays generic if needed.
5. User may save the item and then navigate to Saved Items.

Save item and go to Saved Items:
1. After content is loaded, user clicks 'Add to Reading List' or 'Add to Reading Queue'.
2. User optionally enters notes in the save interaction and confirms.
3. Page calls the appropriate save* interface with the content id and notes.
4. On success, the page updates its internal saved flags and may show a confirmation message.
5. User clicks 'View Saved Items' to navigate to saved_items.html.
interactions: Initial load: On component initialization, read contentType, contentId, slug, and sourcePage from the URL and call getContentDetail; show a loading state until the content and saved status are returned.

Back navigation click: When the user clicks the back link, the page checks sourcePage and contentType to decide whether to navigate to case_studies.html, blog.html, saved_items.html, or a type-based default target.

Save case study interaction: When a case study is displayed and isSavedToReadingList is false, clicking 'Add to Reading List' opens or focuses the notes input (if present) and, on confirmation, calls saveCaseStudyToReadingList with caseStudyId and notes; on success, update saved state and enable 'View Saved Items' navigation.

Save blog post interaction: When a blog post is displayed and isSavedToReadingQueue is false, clicking 'Add to Reading Queue' similarly collects notes and calls saveBlogPostToReadingQueue; on success, update saved state and enable 'View Saved Items' navigation.

Saved state indication: If getContentDetail indicates the item is already saved, the save button is shown in a saved state (e.g., disabled or relabeled) and optionally allows updating notes via another explicit user action if designed.

View Saved Items navigation: After a successful save, a 'View Saved Items' link/button is available; clicking it navigates to saved_items.html without additional parameters (the Saved Items page is responsible for its own filtering).

Error handling: If getContentDetail returns no content for the given parameters, show an error message section and present a fallback navigation option (e.g., back to Case Studies or Blog depending on contentType). If a save* call fails, show a non-blocking error message and keep the item unsaved in the UI.

Content-type-specific rendering: The metadata component conditionally renders case-study-specific or blog-specific fields based on contentType and the presence of data fields in the returned CaseStudy or BlogPost object.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and API responses. On load, the page reads contentType, contentId, slug, and sourcePage from the URL. The contentType parameter determines whether the page expects a CaseStudy or BlogPost. The contentId parameter, when present, is used with contentType in the getContentDetail call; if contentId is missing and slug is present, slug is used instead. The sourcePage parameter indicates where the user came from: 'case_studies', 'blog', or 'saved_items'. It is used to determine the behavior and label of the primary back navigation: if sourcePage is 'case_studies', the back target is case_studies.html; if 'blog', the target is blog.html; if 'saved_items', the target is saved_items.html. If sourcePage is absent or set to another value, the back navigation falls back to a default target based on contentType (case_study -> case_studies.html, blog_post -> blog.html). After getContentDetail resolves, the page inspects the returned contentType and either the 'caseStudy' or 'blogPost' object. It also reads isSavedToReadingList and isSavedToReadingQueue to initialize the save buttons' state: for case studies, isSavedToReadingList controls whether 'Add to Reading List' is shown as active or already saved; for blog posts, isSavedToReadingQueue controls the 'Add to Reading Queue' button. When a save* action succeeds, the page updates its local saved state flags to reflect the response; subsequent renders respect this state without needing to change URL parameters. All navigation away from this page (Back to list or View Saved Items) is done via link clicks that change the browser URL to the appropriate target page with any required parameters; no localStorage or other persistent client state is used.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters, invoking getContentDetail, and managing loading and error states.
event_handlers: onInit: Read contentType, contentId, slug, and sourcePage from the URL. Validate that contentType is either 'case_study' or 'blog_post'. Invoke getContentDetail with contentType and either contentId or slug depending on which is present.

onLoadSuccess: When getContentDetail resolves successfully, store the returned contentType, caseStudy or blogPost object, and saved flags (isSavedToReadingList, isSavedToReadingQueue) in component state. Notify dependent components (header, metadata, body, reading actions) to render.
onLoadError: If getContentDetail fails or returns no content for the given parameters, set an error state that the content-detail-error component can render, and disable save actions.

components.functionality: Displays the title of the content and a back navigation control that returns to the originating list or a content-type-based default.
event_handlers: onRender: Use the loaded CaseStudy or BlogPost object to display the title. Use URL parameter sourcePage and page-level contentType state to determine back-label text (e.g., 'Back to Case Studies', 'Back to Blog', or 'Back to Saved Items') and target URL.

onClickBack: When the back link is clicked, navigate to the determined target page (case_studies.html, blog.html, or saved_items.html) using standard URL navigation. Optionally include any list-level parameters if they are part of the current URL and needed by the originating page.

components.functionality: Shows metadata specific to the content type: industry, client attributes, ROI, project duration, and other metrics for case studies; publish date, topic category, tags, and estimated read time for blog posts.
event_handlers: onRender: Inspect the page-level contentType. If 'case_study', render only CaseStudy fields that are present in the loaded caseStudy object. If 'blog_post', render only BlogPost fields from the loaded blogPost object. Hide or omit any field whose value is null or undefined to avoid empty placeholders.

components.functionality: Renders the main textual content (body) of the case study or blog post, optionally using summary as a fallback if body is not available.
event_handlers: onRender: For the active contentType, attempt to display the body field from the loaded object. If body is not available but summary is, render summary instead. Handle long content by enabling internal scrolling or relying on page-level scrolling; no additional logic is required.
onContentChange: Re-render when the loader updates the CaseStudy or BlogPost object (e.g., if the same page instance is reused across navigations).

components.functionality: Provides the main 'Add to Reading List' or 'Add to Reading Queue' action depending on content type, reflects current saved status, and coordinates the capture of optional notes.
event_handlers: onRender: Based on page-level contentType and saved flags from getContentDetail (isSavedToReadingList, isSavedToReadingQueue), decide which primary button to show ('Add to Reading List' for case studies, 'Add to Reading Queue' for blog posts) and whether the button is enabled, disabled, or shown in a saved state.

onClickPrimaryAction: When the primary button is clicked for an unsaved item, reveal or focus the notes input (managed by the reading-notes-input component) and prepare for submission. If the item is already saved, either disable the button or handle an explicit 'update notes' flow if designed.

onSaveSuccess: After a successful saveCaseStudyToReadingList or saveBlogPostToReadingQueue call, update the saved-state flags in this component and make the 'View Saved Items' control visible (delegated to saved-items-navigation component).
onSaveError: If a save call fails, show an inline error message and leave the saved-state flags unchanged.

components.functionality: Collects optional notes from the user when saving a case study or blog post to their Reading List or Reading Queue.
event_handlers: onChange: Update a local notes value as the user types. This value is not persisted until the save action is triggered.
onSubmit: When the user confirms the save action from the reading-action-panel, provide the current notes value to the appropriate save interface call.
onClear: Allow the user to clear notes before submission, resetting the local notes value.

components.functionality: Invokes saveCaseStudyToReadingList for case studies, using the current content id and optional notes captured from the notes input.
event_handlers: onInvoke: Called by reading-action-panel when the user confirms saving a case study. Calls saveCaseStudyToReadingList with caseStudyId set to the loaded CaseStudy.id and notes set to the current notes value from reading-notes-input.

onSuccess: When the interface returns success=true, update page-level saved flags (isSavedToReadingList) and store the returned SavedItem object for future reference (e.g., notes display). Notify reading-action-panel and saved-items-navigation of the updated state.
onFailure: If success is false or an error occurs, propagate an error state to reading-action-panel for user feedback.

components.functionality: Invokes saveBlogPostToReadingQueue for blog posts, using the current content id and optional notes captured from the notes input.
event_handlers: onInvoke: Called by reading-action-panel when the user confirms saving a blog post. Calls saveBlogPostToReadingQueue with blogPostId set to the loaded BlogPost.id and notes set to the current notes value from reading-notes-input.

onSuccess: When the interface returns success=true, update page-level saved flags (isSavedToReadingQueue) and store the returned SavedItem object. Notify reading-action-panel and saved-items-navigation of the updated state.
onFailure: If success is false or an error occurs, propagate an error state to reading-action-panel for user feedback.

components.functionality: Displays a 'View Saved Items' navigation option when the current item has been saved to the Reading List or Reading Queue.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Listen to saved-state updates from the save-to-reading-list-handler and save-to-reading-queue-handler. When a save succeeds, make the 'View Saved Items' link/button visible.
onClick: Navigate the user to saved_items.html using standard URL navigation, without relying on client-side storage for state.

components.functionality: Displays error information if the content cannot be loaded or if required URL parameters are missing or invalid.
event_handlers: onErrorState: When content-detail-loader sets an error state (e.g., getContentDetail failure, invalid contentType), render an error message and provide basic navigation options, such as links back to case_studies.html and blog.html based on the requested contentType.
onClickFallbackNav: Handle user clicks on fallback navigation links, changing the browser URL to the chosen destination page.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: Repurpose the hero's two-column structure into an editorial detail view. The left column becomes the main reading surface: content header (title, subtitle), followed immediately by the content body. The right column becomes a stacked utility rail: metadata at the top, followed by the reading-action panel and then the notes input. Navigation back to saved items anchors the bottom of the page as a horizontal band. Loaders and error states occupy the same central content region, temporarily replacing the usual layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Repurpose the hero's two-column structure into an editorial detail view. The left column becomes the main reading surface: content header (title, subtitle), followed immediately by the content body. The right column becomes a stacked utility rail: metadata at the top, followed by the reading-action panel and then the notes input. Navigation back to saved items anchors the bottom of the page as a horizontal band. Loaders and error states occupy the same central content region, temporarily replacing the usual layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a 12-column grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px). Allocate roughly 60–65% of the width (7–8 columns) to the left reading column and 35–40% (4–5 columns) to the right utility column. Vertically, the header and top metadata together occupy the upper third of the initial viewport, the body and right-side panels fill the remaining height, and the saved-items navigation forms a low-height band near the bottom. Generous top/bottom padding (64–96px) keeps everything airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use the same low-density, editorial feel as the hero: large, readable typography, clear vertical spacing between header, metadata, and content body, and distinct separation between the right-column cards (metadata, actions, notes). Avoid visually dense clusters; each section should feel like its own block with 24–48px breathing room above and below.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Adapt the hero's Z-pattern: users start top-left on the content header, then glance right to the metadata panel, then back down the main body text on the left. From there, the eye moves right again to the reading-action panel and notes input as secondary interactions. Finally, the gaze drops down to the saved-items navigation at the bottom, offering a way back to lists or the next item. Loaders and errors, when active, interrupt and centralize attention in the main content area.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, charcoal background, with a centered content container that feels like a sleek reading surface. At the top of this container, the content header stretches predominantly across the left two-thirds of the width, presenting the case study or article title in large, high-contrast white type with a short subtitle or kicker just above or below, echoing the hero’s typographic hierarchy. To its right, aligned to the top of the header, a compact but prominent metadata panel occupies the remaining third, styled as a clean card that summarizes key details (date, reading time, tags, status). Below this top band, the layout resolves into a clear two-column structure: the left column continues downward with the full content body, flowing in generous paragraphs with ample line spacing, while the right column becomes a vertical rail of functional cards. At the top of this rail sits the metadata panel, followed immediately by the reading-action panel (buttons like “Save to Reading List” and “Add to Queue”), and then the notes input area for personal annotations. The action panel is subtly sticky within the viewport, so it stays near the top-right as users scroll down the article, mirroring the portrait’s persistent presence in the original hero. Near the bottom of the page, after the article concludes, a full-width saved-items navigation band appears, lightly separated from the content with extra vertical spacing, giving users a clear, calm way to return to their reading list or queue. Overlays for loading and error states occupy the central portion of this layout, slightly dimming the background while keeping the page’s structure intact. The overall effect is a modern, minimalist, consulting-grade reading experience that balances a strong primary narrative on the left with a focused set of reading tools on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as an overlay centered within the main content container, covering both the left content column and the right utility column but not the global site header. It spans roughly 80–90% of the container’s width, centered horizontally, and sits vertically in the middle third of the viewport. The background of the overlay is a slightly darker translucent charcoal scrim over the existing page surface, with skeleton lines or a simple spinner aligned to where the content header and top of the body would be (left side, upper center). There is generous padding (32–48px) around the loader elements, in line with the spacious layout, and the underlying structure remains perceptible but visually de-emphasized.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed within the bounds of the main content container; it sits above all other page components in the stacking order while data is loading and disappears once content is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content header is positioned at the top of the main content container, aligned to the left edge of the grid. It occupies approximately 60–65% of the container width, corresponding to about 7–8 grid columns, and sits with a generous top padding of 64–80px from the global header. Within this block, a small all-caps context label (e.g., “CASE STUDY”, “INSIGHT”) appears at the top-left, followed by a large, multi-line H1 headline in high-contrast white type, and optionally a short supporting subtitle or abstract directly below. Each textual element is vertically stacked with clear spacing: 8–12px between context text and headline, 16–24px between headline and subtitle, and 24–32px separating the header block from the content body beneath. The right edge of the header aligns visually with the start of the right column, forming a clean vertical division between main content and utility panels.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the natural document flow, always at the top of the content detail view. On smaller screens, it expands to nearly full width, with the right-column components stacking below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content metadata panel is positioned to the right of the content header, aligned to the same top baseline. It occupies roughly 35–40% of the container width (about 4–5 grid columns) and sits flush with the right padding of the main container. The panel presents as a compact, card-like block on the dark background, with a slightly lighter charcoal panel and subtle border or glow, using 16–24px internal padding. Inside, metadata items (e.g., author, date, reading time, tags, status) are arranged in a vertical list with 8–12px spacing between rows, using subdued but legible typography that doesn’t compete with the main headline. There is a 24px gap between the bottom edge of this panel and the next element in the right column (the reading-action panel), reinforcing separation between ‘information about the content’ and ‘actions on the content’. On scroll, the top edge of this panel sits just below the global header area, visually aligning with the top of the sticky reading-action panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls normally with the page, but is visually anchored at the top of the right column above other utility cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content body flows directly beneath the content header, aligned to the same left column and indentation. It spans the same 60–65% width as the header, maintaining a consistent left margin and right boundary that line up with the vertical division between columns. The top of the body begins approximately 24–32px below the bottom edge of the header, giving the impression of a clearly separated but continuous reading section. Paragraphs use a comfortable line length within this column, with line spacing tuned for readability and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs or subsections. Any inline media (images, charts, quotes) are kept within this column width and separated from surrounding text by 24–32px. As the user scrolls, the body occupies the majority of vertical space, while the right column utilities stay visible to the side (where viewport height allows), preserving an editorial feel where the article dominates and tools remain peripheral but accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing naturally as the main scrollable content. On narrow viewports, it becomes the first full-width block beneath the header, with the utility components stacking below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-action panel lives in the right column, directly beneath the content metadata panel, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It mirrors the size and card treatment of the metadata panel, spanning the same 35–40% width and using 16–24px internal padding. Inside, primary actions like “Save to Reading List” and “Add to Queue” are arranged in a vertical or tight horizontal stack near the top of the card, with primary CTAs given full-width or near-full-width buttons for emphasis, using the brand accent color against the dark background. Secondary text (e.g., confirmation state, short helper copy) appears just below, with 8–12px spacing. The panel’s top edge is visually aligned to sit roughly 96px below the global header when in its sticky state, so as the user scrolls, it appears anchored near the top-right of the viewport, creating a stable counterpart to the left-column reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right column: once the user scrolls past the initial fold, the panel sticks near the top-right of the viewport with an offset (around 80–96px from the top), ensuring actions remain visible while the article scrolls. On small screens, it loses stickiness and appears as a standard block below the content body.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-notes input sits in the right column beneath the reading-action panel, separated by a 24–32px gap to keep the utility cards clearly distinct. It spans the same 35–40% width, forming a vertically oriented card with a text area or note field that fills most of the card’s height. The card uses a slightly lighter panel tone against the charcoal background, with 16–24px padding inside and a clear label at the top (e.g., “Notes” or “Your Takeaways”) aligned to the left. The text input area itself occupies the remaining vertical space, with comfortable padding inside and a height that visually matches a few paragraphs of content (not too small or cramped). Any save/status indicator is tucked at the bottom-right of the card with subtle text. When the page is tall, this notes card scrolls naturally within the right column beneath the sticky action panel, so it may partially move in and out of view as the user scrolls deeper into the article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, positioned below the sticky reading-action panel. It scrolls with the page, appearing and disappearing as the user moves up and down, while the action panel remains pinned near the top-right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual behavioral component associated with the reading-action panel’s Save to Reading List control. Conceptually, it is anchored to the same right column area as the action panel, triggering state changes (e.g., button states, subtle confirmation text) within that panel rather than taking up its own space. Any visual feedback (like a brief confirmation message) appears inline at the bottom of the reading-action panel or as a small toast aligned to the right column boundary, not as a separate block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; functions as an event and state handler tied to the buttons in the reading-action panel. It does not alter layout dimensions but may cause minor, inline state changes within the action panel (e.g., button label changes, success messages).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Like the reading-list handler, this is a non-visual behavior component attached to the Add to Queue control inside the reading-action panel. It shares the same spatial association with the right column and surfaces its effects only through small state changes inside that panel (e.g., toggled button style, short confirmation copy just beneath the queue button). It does not appear as a separate visual element or card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates behind the scenes for interactions within the reading-action panel. Any visual feedback is inline within the panel, maintaining the existing layout without additional blocks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-items navigation forms a horizontal band near the bottom of the main content container, appearing after the content body concludes. It spans nearly the full width of the container (90–100%), aligning with both the left content column and the right utility column, effectively unifying them at the bottom. There is a generous vertical gap of 48–64px between the end of the content body and this band, so it feels like a distinct footer-like navigation zone. Inside, navigation controls (e.g., Back to Reading List, Back to Queue, Next Saved Item) are centered horizontally or slightly biased to the left, arranged in a horizontal row with 16–24px spacing between buttons or links. The band is slightly lighter or more outlined than the background to subtly differentiate it, but it remains understated so the main article retains the primary focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow, anchored at the bottom of the content detail view. On shorter content where the viewport is taller than the content, it may appear closer to mid-screen, but it always remains below the content body, not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error panel replaces the usual content area when a load or retrieval failure occurs. It is centered within the main content container, occupying roughly 60–70% of the container width and sitting in the middle third of the viewport vertically, similar to the loader. The panel takes the form of a prominent message card with high-contrast text on a dark, slightly elevated background, featuring a concise error headline, a short explanation, and a primary action (e.g., Retry or Back to Reading List). There is ample white space within (24–32px padding) and around the panel (at least 48px margin to surrounding edges), and it visually aligns to where the content header would normally be, reinforcing that it is the main content in this state. The right-column utilities and body content are hidden or dimmed behind this panel to avoid distraction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily central and blocking: it appears in place of the standard content and right column when an error occurs, effectively taking over the main content region until the error is resolved or the user navigates away.

name: Saved Items
title: Saved Items
description: Page for viewing and managing all saved content, split into a Reading List (case studies) and a Reading Queue (blog posts). Users can open items to read them and remove items they no longer need.

core_features: Display saved case studies in a Reading List section by calling getSavedItems with listType = 'reading_list'.
Display saved blog posts in a Reading Queue section by calling getSavedItems with listType = 'reading_queue'.
Use the incoming URL parameter listType to determine which section is active when the page loads.
Allow users to switch between Reading List and Reading Queue via tab controls that update the URL listType parameter.
Allow users to open any saved item, navigating to content_detail.html with contentType, contentId, and sourcePage=saved_items URL parameters.
Allow users to remove individual saved items by calling removeSavedItem with the savedItemId and updating the list without a full page reload.
Handle loading and empty states for each list type based on the results of getSavedItems.
user_workflows: View Reading List: 1) User navigates to saved_items.html (optionally with ?listType=reading_list). 2) Page-controller component reads listType or defaults to 'reading_list'. 3) Page-controller calls getSavedItems(listType='reading_list'). 4) When data returns, the Reading List tab is shown as active and the list displays the returned SavedItem records. 5) If no items are returned, an empty-state message is shown for the Reading List.

View Reading Queue: 1) User navigates to saved_items.html?listType=reading_queue OR clicks the Reading Queue tab. 2) Page-controller sets activeListType to 'reading_queue'. 3) If items for this listType have not yet been loaded in the current session, page-controller calls getSavedItems(listType='reading_queue'). 4) When data returns, the Reading Queue tab shows the SavedItem records. 5) If no items are returned, an empty-state message is shown for the Reading Queue.

Open a saved item to read: 1) User is on Saved Items and sees a list of SavedItem rows. 2) User clicks the title or main row area of one SavedItem. 3) The click handler reads savedItem.contentType and savedItem.contentId. 4) Browser navigates to content_detail.html with URL parameters contentType=savedItem.contentType, contentId=savedItem.contentId, and sourcePage='saved_items'. 5) The user reads the full content on content_detail.html.

Remove a saved item: 1) User is viewing either Reading List or Reading Queue. 2) User clicks the remove action associated with a specific SavedItem. 3) The remove handler calls removeSavedItem(savedItemId = savedItem.id). 4) On success, the item is removed from the in-memory list so it disappears from the current tab without a page reload. 5) If the last item in that list is removed, the list switches to showing an empty-state message for that listType.

Return to Saved Items after saving items elsewhere: 1) User is on case_studies.html, blog.html, content_detail.html, or index.html and saves content (functionality defined on those pages). 2) The user navigates or is redirected to saved_items.html with an optional listType parameter set according to what they just saved (e.g., 'reading_list' for case studies, 'reading_queue' for blog posts). 3) Saved Items page initializes with the corresponding tab active and calls getSavedItems for that listType so the newly saved items are visible.
interactions: Tab click (Reading List / Reading Queue): Clicking a tab updates the active listType state, updates the URL parameter listType via a client-side navigation (e.g., using history.replaceState or a full reload), and triggers getSavedItems for the new listType if its data has not yet been loaded.

Saved item row click: Clicking on a saved item row or its title triggers navigation to content_detail.html with URL parameters contentType=savedItem.contentType, contentId=savedItem.contentId, and sourcePage='saved_items'.

Remove item click: Clicking the remove control for a saved item calls removeSavedItem(savedItemId = savedItem.id). On success, the item is removed from the current list in memory; on failure, an error message is displayed within the page.

Page load: When saved_items.html loads, the page-controller parses the listType URL parameter. It determines the initial active tab and triggers getSavedItems for at least that active listType. Optionally, it may prefetch the other listType depending on implementation.

URL change (listType): If the URL listType parameter changes while the user is on the page (e.g., via client-side navigation), the page-controller updates the active tab and ensures that getSavedItems has been called for that listType.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by the URL parameter listType and the results of the getSavedItems and removeSavedItem interfaces. On initial load, the page reads window.location.search to get the listType parameter. If listType equals 'reading_queue', the Reading Queue tab is active; if it equals 'reading_list' or is missing/invalid, the Reading List tab is active. For each listType, the page maintains local state: loading (before or while getSavedItems(listType) is in progress), loaded (when getSavedItems returns an array of SavedItem records, which can be empty), and error (if the interface call fails). The active tab’s visual state is derived solely from the current listType value. When a user clicks a tab, the code updates the URL to saved_items.html?listType=<newListType> (either via full navigation or history API) and triggers state updates as if the page were loaded with that listType. No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used; any refresh of the page recomputes state from the URL and fresh getSavedItems calls. Removing an item with removeSavedItem only affects the in-memory list and the backend; the current URL and listType remain unchanged.

components.functionality: Initializes the Saved Items page, parses the listType URL parameter, manages which tab is active, orchestrates calls to getSavedItems for each listType, and maintains loading and error states for Reading List and Reading Queue.
event_handlers: onLoad (parse URL, set activeListType, call getSavedItems for active listType)
onUrlChange (when listType in URL changes, update activeListType and ensure corresponding data is loaded via getSavedItems)
onListTypeChange (triggered by tab-switcher; updates URL listType and active tab)
onItemsReload (optional refresh action to recall getSavedItems for the currently active listType)

components.functionality: Provides two tabs corresponding to the Reading List (case studies) and Reading Queue (blog posts). Reflects which tab is active based on the page-controller’s activeListType and allows users to switch between them, updating URL and triggering data loading as needed.
event_handlers: onClickReadingListTab (requests page-controller to set activeListType='reading_list' and update URL listType)
onClickReadingQueueTab (requests page-controller to set activeListType='reading_queue' and update URL listType)

components.functionality: Displays the list of SavedItem records that belong to the Reading List (listType='reading_list'). Shows loading state while getSavedItems is in progress, shows an empty-state message if no items are returned, and renders each SavedItem as a clickable row with a remove control.
event_handlers: onItemClick (navigates to content_detail.html with contentType=savedItem.contentType, contentId=savedItem.contentId, sourcePage='saved_items')
onRemoveClick (calls removeSavedItem with savedItem.id and removes the item from the Reading List on success)

components.functionality: Displays the list of SavedItem records that belong to the Reading Queue (listType='reading_queue'). Behavior mirrors the Reading List section: handles loading, empty, and error states and renders each SavedItem with navigation and removal actions.
event_handlers: onItemClick (navigates to content_detail.html with contentType=savedItem.contentType, contentId=savedItem.contentId, sourcePage='saved_items')
onRemoveClick (calls removeSavedItem with savedItem.id and removes the item from the Reading Queue on success)

components.functionality: Represents a single SavedItem within a list. Uses SavedItem fields (such as titleSnapshot, summarySnapshot, contentType, savedAt, and notes) to show concise information about the saved content. The entire row is clickable for navigation, and it includes a separate control for removal.
event_handlers: onClick (navigates to content_detail.html using savedItem.contentType and savedItem.contentId with sourcePage='saved_items')
onClickRemove (invokes removeSavedItem with savedItem.id and notifies the parent list component to update its in-memory collection)

components.functionality: Displays a simple message when getSavedItems returns an empty array for the currently viewed listType, indicating that there are no saved items in the Reading List or Reading Queue.
Displays an error message if getSavedItems or removeSavedItem fails. Allows the user to retry loading items for the current listType.
event_handlers: onRetryClick (re-invokes getSavedItems for the active listType via the page-controller)

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a hero-like controller at the top and a content body beneath. At the top of the main container, the page-controller acts as a compact hero for 'Saved Items': a small all-caps label, a prominent H1, a short supporting description, and any high-level actions or counts in a single vertical stack, left-aligned. Directly below this, the list-type-tab-switcher runs horizontally, functioning like a segmented control for 'Reading List' and 'Reading Queue'. Beneath the tabs, a single active saved-items-list section is shown at a time (either the reading-list-section or the reading-queue-section), each composed of a vertical stack of saved-item-row components. Within each saved-item-row, content is organized in a two-column feel: primary textual information on the left (title, description, metadata) and key actions (open, remove) aligned on the right. Empty and error states occupy the same footprint as the list content: the error state appears pinned to the top of the list area; the empty state appears centered within that list's vertical space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a hero-like controller at the top and a content body beneath. At the top of the main container, the page-controller acts as a compact hero for 'Saved Items': a small all-caps label, a prominent H1, a short supporting description, and any high-level actions or counts in a single vertical stack, left-aligned. Directly below this, the list-type-tab-switcher runs horizontally, functioning like a segmented control for 'Reading List' and 'Reading Queue'. Beneath the tabs, a single active saved-items-list section is shown at a time (either the reading-list-section or the reading-queue-section), each composed of a vertical stack of saved-item-row components. Within each saved-item-row, content is organized in a two-column feel: primary textual information on the left (title, description, metadata) and key actions (open, remove) aligned on the right. Empty and error states occupy the same footprint as the list content: the error state appears pinned to the top of the list area; the empty state appears centered within that list's vertical space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) on a full-width dark background. The page-controller occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height, giving the title and description ample breathing room. The tab-switcher sits immediately below with modest vertical separation (16–24px). The list area below fills the remaining viewport height and can scroll. Horizontally, the grid follows a 12-column layout: the main text and list content typically span 8–10 central columns (about 70–85% of the container width), keeping comfortable margins on left and right. Each saved-item-row uses a 60–65% / 35–40% split: the left portion for content, the right portion for meta and actions. Vertical spacing between the page-controller, tabs, list sections, and rows follows the 8px base unit, with larger section gaps in the 48–64px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: The design preserves the mockup’s low content density: large typography for the page title, generous line-height for descriptive copy, and clear separation between clusters (controller vs tabs vs list content). Each saved-item-row feels like an individual, airy card: comfortable padding (16–24px inside), distinct spacing between rows (16–24px), and no crowded text blocks. Lists never feel like compact tables; instead they read as editorial entries in a reading library. Empty and error states use prominent but minimal messaging, centered or top-aligned with plenty of whitespace, avoiding visual clutter even when the user has many items saved.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the top-left with the small all-caps context label and then immediately drops into the large 'Saved Items' headline, mirroring the hero flow from the mockup. From there, it moves downward to the supporting description and any high-level actions. Next, the visual flow continues straight down to the horizontal tab-switcher, where the user decides between 'Reading List' and 'Reading Queue'. Once a tab is selected, the gaze moves down into the list area, scanning saved-item-rows from top to bottom. Within each row, attention starts at the left with the item title, moves slightly down to any secondary text, and then sweeps right to the key actions (e.g., open, remove). Error messages briefly intercept attention at the top of the list area; empty states draw attention to the center of the list area when there are no items, guiding the user back to exploration.

layout.overall_description: The Saved Items page sits on a charcoal dark-mode background, with all content constrained to a centered max-width column that feels like an elegant reading environment rather than a dashboard. At the very top of this column, a compact hero-style page-controller introduces the section: a subtle, all-caps label like 'Library' at the top-left, a large, high-contrast 'Saved Items' headline below, and a short, single-paragraph description explaining the split between Reading List and Reading Queue. The typography is generous and airy, and the block occupies the upper-left two-thirds of the container, leaving visual breathing room to the right. Below this, a horizontal tab-switcher runs across the main content width, with two pill-like tabs aligned left: 'Reading List' and 'Reading Queue'. The active tab is clearly highlighted; the inactive one is subdued but legible. Underneath, the active list section stretches vertically, filling the rest of the viewport and beyond if necessary. Saved-item-rows are laid out as minimalist cards: each row spans the central width, with the left ~60–65% dedicated to the item’s title and brief details, and the right ~35–40% hosting concise actions and metadata. Rows are separated by consistent vertical gaps, and the surrounding dark background further emphasizes their structure. If an error occurs, a slim but noticeable error banner appears at the top of the list area; if the list is empty, a centered, calm empty-state message replaces the rows, framed by generous padding. The overall feel mirrors the original mockup: modern, spacious, and confidently minimal, with attention naturally flowing from top-left controller down into the curated reading lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-controller is positioned at the top of the main content container, directly below the global site header/navigation. It is left-aligned within a centered max-width wrapper (~1100–1200px) on a charcoal background. The content forms a vertical stack anchored to the top-left: at the top, a small all-caps label (e.g., 'LIBRARY') sits with a slight top margin (24–32px below the global header) and spans roughly 3–4 grid columns. Below it, the primary H1 'Saved Items' occupies roughly 6–8 columns, using large, high-contrast typography that takes up about one-third of the controller’s vertical area. Beneath the H1, a single supporting paragraph spans a similar width but uses smaller type and comfortable line-height, introducing the concept of Reading List vs Reading Queue.
