If high-level stats or a secondary action (e.g., 'Clear all') exist, they align on the same baseline as the bottom of the paragraph, tucked to the right side of the text block (around columns 7–12) but still within the same top band, keeping the left side dominant. The vertical padding above and below this controller block is generous (around 48–64px total), so the section feels like a mini hero band that introduces this page’s purpose before the user reaches the tabs and lists.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned in the normal document flow at the top of the page content, always occupying full width of the main container. It does not scroll independently; as the user scrolls, it moves out of view with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab-switcher is placed immediately below the page-controller, with a distinct vertical gap (16–24px) to separate it but still feel connected. It runs horizontally across the main content width, left-aligned with the page-controller’s text block. On wide screens, the tab controls span roughly 4–6 columns, starting at the left edge of the text baseline, leaving negative space to the right. Two primary tabs—'Reading List' and 'Reading Queue'—are styled as pill-shaped segments within a single bar, with the active tab appearing filled or more luminous while the inactive tab is outlined or lower contrast. The overall height of the tab control is modest (about 40–48px), with 8–12px vertical padding inside the pills and 16–24px horizontal padding per label. A subtle bottom border or baseline aligns visually with the top of the list sections below, signaling that this is the control for what appears underneath. On narrow screens, the tabs remain horizontal but can expand to nearly 100% of the container width, maintaining consistent vertical spacing above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the tab-switcher can be sticky to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past the page-controller, so it remains visible as a context anchor for switching between Reading List and Reading Queue. On mobile or if desired, this can degrade to normal static behavior while preserving its position just above the list content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-section occupies the main content area directly below the tab-switcher and extends downward. It is horizontally centered within the same max-width container and shares the same left alignment as the page-controller text and tabs. When the 'Reading List' tab is active, this section is visible and spans the full usable width reserved for content (about 70–85% of the viewport width, depending on screen size). A top margin of ~24–32px separates it from the tab-switcher, giving a clear breathing zone. Within this section, individual saved-item-row components are stacked vertically in a single column, each row spanning roughly 10 out of 12 grid columns. The left margin is consistent with the tab-switcher; the right side maintains a safe margin to avoid edge crowding. Vertical spacing between rows is consistent (16–24px), creating an even rhythm as the user scrolls. If several items exist, the list scrolls as one continuous column. If the section is in an error or empty state, the first visible element is either the error banner (top-aligned, just under the section’s top padding) or the empty state message centered both horizontally and within the visible vertical space of this list section. When the 'Reading List' tab is inactive, this entire section is hidden from view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable page content. Only one saved-items-list section is visible at a time; when 'Reading List' is selected, this section is displayed while the reading-queue-section is hidden (e.g., display: none or equivalent).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-queue-section occupies the exact same footprint and spatial relationship as the reading-list-section. It is positioned directly below the tab-switcher, sharing the same top margin (24–32px) and horizontal alignment. When the 'Reading Queue' tab is active, this section becomes visible in place of the Reading List. It also spans roughly 10 out of 12 grid columns, centered with consistent left and right margins. Inside, saved-item-row components are stacked in a vertical list with identical spacing and styling to the Reading List: rows span most of the width, with clear separation between them and ample padding inside each. The vertical extent of the section is determined by the number of items; if there are many queued posts, the list continues downward in a smooth, scrollable column. In error conditions or when empty, the error or empty state messages appear in the same manner as in the Reading List—error at the top, empty state centered—ensuring parallel behavior between the two list types.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable content. It alternates visibility with the reading-list-section based on the active tab, occupying the same spatial slot in the layout (only one list section visible at any time).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each saved-item-row is a horizontally oriented card that spans most of the content width, aligned with the main grid. On desktop, the row occupies about 10 of 12 columns, leaving slim gutters on either side. The card has a dark, slightly elevated background or a subtle outline, with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides, following the 8px spacing system. Inside, the row content is divided into two primary zones along a 60–65% / 35–40% split: the left content block and the right action/metadata block. The left block starts flush with the card’s left padding and spans approximately 7–8 grid columns. At its top, the item title is set in a strong, white or near-white font, typically single or double line, with enough line-height for readability. Directly under the title, an optional short description or snippet appears in slightly smaller, lower-contrast type. Below that or inline, metadata such as content type (e.g., 'Case Study'), reading time, or tags are presented using muted tones, with 4–8px vertical spacing between lines. The right block is right-aligned within the card’s remaining width (about 4–5 grid columns). At the top-right, primary actions (e.g., an 'Open' button or icon and a 'Remove' or 'Unsave' icon) are aligned horizontally with 8–16px spacing. Below or aligned under these actions, secondary details like the date saved or source may appear, right-aligned and using smaller, subdued text. Vertically, the content is centered within the card’s height so the card never feels top-heavy. The result is a clean two-column feel where the user reads the item details on the left and naturally scans to the right for actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its parent saved-items-list section. Rows stretch to fill the available width defined by the grid and shrink/wrap responsively on smaller screens—on mobile, the right-side actions can stack beneath the text, maintaining top-to-bottom reading order while preserving clear separation via vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears within whichever list section (reading-list-section or reading-queue-section) is currently active when there are no items. Instead of stacked rows, the empty state occupies the central portion of the list area. Horizontally, it is centered within the same max-width container, typically constrained to about 6–8 grid columns in the middle so it feels compact and intentional rather than stretching edge-to-edge. Vertically, a generous top margin (48–64px from the tab-switcher or the section’s top) positions the empty state roughly in the upper-middle of the visible list area, ensuring it is immediately noticeable without feeling cramped at the top. The content itself is a vertical stack: an optional small icon or glyph at the top, a clear headline (e.g., 'No items saved yet') in a medium-large type size, and a short, calm explanatory line beneath it. Spacing between these elements follows the 8px scale (8–16px between icon and headline, 8–12px between headline and body text). If there is a call-to-action (such as 'Explore case studies'), it appears as a single prominent button or subtle text link below the description, centered and separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Surrounding whitespace is abundant, mirroring the minimalism of the hero and preserving a sense of calm even when the list is empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list section, replacing the normal stack of saved-item-rows. It is only rendered when the corresponding list has zero items; otherwise, it is not visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-message appears at the top of the active list section when a loading or retrieval error occurs. It is presented as a full-width banner aligned with the list content, spanning roughly the same 10 of 12 grid columns as the item rows, with equal left alignment and right margins. The banner height is modest but noticeable, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Inside, content is arranged horizontally: a small alert icon on the far left, followed by a short, concise error message in high-contrast text, and optionally a retry action (button or text link) aligned to the far right or directly below the text on smaller screens. Vertical spacing within the banner is compact but not cramped, and the surrounding area has a small margin (16–24px) separating the banner from the tab-switcher above and the first potential content element below. If the list cannot be loaded at all, no saved-item-rows appear beneath; the banner stands alone in the list area, surrounded by the dark background, keeping the overall appearance clean despite the error. The color treatment remains consistent with a modern dark-mode alert (e.g., slightly warmer accent or subtle red edge) but avoids heavy blocks of color that would break the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored to the top of whichever list section is active. It does not overlay or float; instead, it pushes list content downward. It is only visible while an error state is present and disappears once the error is resolved or retried successfully.

name: AI Readiness Assessment
title: AI Readiness Assessment
description: Interactive assessment page where organizations answer AI readiness questions, submit their responses, view a computed readiness result, and navigate to a recommended consulting service or book a consultation.

core_features: Load and display all active AI readiness questions and their dynamic answer options using getAIReadinessQuestions.
Allow users to select answers for each question, supporting single choice, multiple choice, and scale question types as returned by the API.
Validate that all required questions have at least one selected option before submission (based on the existence of the question in the loaded set, without hardcoding which are required).
Collect the selected answer option IDs and submit them via submitAIReadinessAssessment.
Display the returned assessmentSession details, including overallScore and readinessLevel, after a successful submission.
Display the recommendedOffering returned by submitAIReadinessAssessment in a primary recommended service card, including its key fields and status.
Provide a primary CTA from the recommendedOffering card to navigate to offering_detail.html with URL parameters including offeringId and sourceType=from_assessment.

Provide a secondary CTA on the results state to navigate directly to book_consultation.html with URL parameters including serviceType, offeringId, sourceType=from_assessment, and sourceReferenceId set to assessmentSession.id.
Handle error states for loading questions and submitting answers, allowing users to retry without losing already selected answers where possible.
Use URL parameters to represent high-level page state (e.g., viewing questions vs. viewing results) without relying on localStorage.
user_workflows:
New assessment from navigation:
1. User clicks 'AI Readiness Assessment' in the header or footer navigation.
2. Browser loads assessment.html with no query parameters.
3. The page initialization component calls getAIReadinessQuestions to retrieve all active questions and their answer options.
4. The question list component renders the questions in the order returned, including helpText where present.
5. The user selects answer options for each question; the component tracks selected AIReadinessOption.id values.
6. When the user clicks the submit action, the page validates that every displayed question has at least one selected option (according to its questionType) and, if valid, calls submitAIReadinessAssessment with the collected answerOptionIds.
7. On success, the page stores assessmentSession and recommendedOffering in page state and updates the URL to assessment.html?view=results (preserving or replacing any existing query params as needed).
8. The page switches to the results view, showing the computed overallScore and readinessLevel, along with the recommendedOffering card.
9. The user can click 'Learn More' or 'Book Now' on the recommended service card to go to offering_detail.html with offeringId and sourceType=from_assessment in the URL, or click 'Book Consultation Now' to go directly to book_consultation.html with the required URL parameters based on the returned assessmentSession and recommendedOffering.

Viewing results in the same session:
1. After submitting the assessment, the user remains on assessment.html?view=results.
2. The results component reads the current in-memory assessmentSession and recommendedOffering.
3. The user can review the summary and, if desired, use a 'Retake Assessment' action within the results view.
4. On 'Retake', the page updates the URL back to assessment.html?view=questions (or removes the view parameter) and reuses the already loaded questions (or reloads them via getAIReadinessQuestions if needed), clearing any previous answer selections in the UI.
5. The user can then complete and submit a new assessment, repeating the submission workflow.

Direct navigation to results URL without existing session state:
1. A user manually visits or reloads assessment.html?view=results without having an assessmentSession stored in the current page state.
2. The page initialization logic detects view=results but finds no in-memory assessmentSession.
3. An empty-state message is shown informing the user that results are not available in this session and prompting them to start a new assessment.
4. When the user clicks 'Start Assessment', the page updates the URL to assessment.html?view=questions (or removes the view parameter) and triggers getAIReadinessQuestions to load questions.
5. The normal new assessment workflow then applies.

Navigation from results to offering detail and booking:
1. While on the results view, the user reviews the recommendedOffering details.
2. The user clicks the 'Learn More' or 'Book Now' CTA on the recommended service card.
3. The browser navigates to offering_detail.html with query parameters: offeringId set to recommendedOffering.id and sourceType=from_assessment.
4. Alternatively, the user clicks a 'Book Consultation Now' CTA in the results view.
5. The browser navigates to book_consultation.html with query parameters: serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, offeringId=recommendedOffering.id, sourceType=from_assessment, and sourceReferenceId=assessmentSession.id.
6. The user completes the booking process on book_consultation.html (out of scope of this page).
interactions: On page load, assessment-init-loader automatically calls getAIReadinessQuestions and sets the initial view based on the URL parameter view (defaulting to questions if not present or invalid).

When a user selects or deselects an answer option, the assessment-question-list component updates its internal selection state for the associated questionId and AIReadinessOption.id, respecting the questionType (single_choice, multiple_choice, or scale).

When the user clicks 'Submit Assessment', the assessment-submit-bar component triggers validation of the selections and, if valid, invokes submitAIReadinessAssessment with the collected answerOptionIds.

On successful submission, the page stores the returned assessmentSession and recommendedOffering objects in page state and updates the URL parameter view=results, then switches the visible component from questions to results.
On submission error (e.g., network or backend error), an error message is displayed and the user can click a 'Retry' action that re-invokes submitAIReadinessAssessment with the same answerOptionIds.
On questions load error (from getAIReadinessQuestions), an error state is displayed with a 'Reload Questions' action that re-calls getAIReadinessQuestions.
When the user clicks 'Retake Assessment' from the results view, the page clears any previous selections, updates the URL parameter to view=questions, and shows the assessment-question-list again.

When the user clicks 'Learn More' or 'Book Now' on the recommended service card, the assessment-recommended-service-card constructs a navigation URL to offering_detail.html with offeringId and sourceType=from_assessment and instructs the browser to navigate.

When the user clicks 'Book Consultation Now' in the results view, the assessment-results-panel constructs a navigation URL to book_consultation.html with serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, offeringId=recommendedOffering.id, sourceType=from_assessment, and sourceReferenceId=assessmentSession.id and instructs the browser to navigate.

When a user lands on assessment.html?view=results but no assessmentSession is present in the client state, the assessment-empty-results-state component shows a prompt with a 'Start Assessment' button; clicking it updates the URL to show the questions view and initiates loading questions if they are not already loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters to control high-level view state but does not rely on localStorage. The primary parameter is `view`, which determines whether the user sees the questionnaire or the results. Supported values: `view=questions` (or missing/other) means the assessment-question-list is shown, `view=results` means the assessment-results-panel or assessment-empty-results-state is shown. On initial load, the assessment-init-loader reads `view` from the URL; if `view=results` and an in-memory `assessmentSession` is available (from the current navigation flow), it shows the results; if `assessmentSession` is not available, it shows the empty-results state with an option to switch back to questions. After a successful call to submitAIReadinessAssessment, the page updates the URL to include `view=results` (using history.pushState or similar) so that the results view is reflected in the URL. When the user chooses to retake the assessment or start a new one from any state, the page sets `view=questions` or removes the `view` parameter and switches back to the question list. Outgoing navigation to offering_detail.html and book_consultation.html also uses URL parameters: offering_detail.html receives `offeringId` and `sourceType=from_assessment`; book_consultation.html receives `serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation`, `offeringId` from `recommendedOffering.id`, `sourceType=from_assessment`, and `sourceReferenceId` from `assessmentSession.id`. The page does not depend on any incoming URL parameter other than `view`, and does not attempt to reload past assessment sessions by ID because no such interface is available.

components.functionality: Initializes the AI Readiness Assessment page by reading URL parameters, loading all active questions and answer options using getAIReadinessQuestions, and setting the initial view state (questions or results). It also manages global page state for questions, current selections, assessmentSession, and recommendedOffering.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read URL parameter `view` to determine whether to start in questions or results mode.

onLoad: Call getAIReadinessQuestions(), store the returned questions array (each with question and options) in page state, and signal the assessment-question-list to render them when in questions view.
onQuestionsLoadError: Set an error flag in state to show a retry option via the assessment-question-list or a dedicated error state.
onRetryLoadQuestions: Re-invoke getAIReadinessQuestions() and clear the previous load error state.
onSetView(view): Update internal view state and synchronize it with the URL query parameter `view`, switching between the question list and results components.

components.functionality: Displays the list of AI readiness questions and their dynamic answer options, allowing users to select answers according to each questionType and preparing the selected answerOptionIds for submission.
event_handlers: onRender: Receive an ordered list of question objects and their associated option lists from assessment-init-loader and display them according to question.order.

onOptionSelect(questionId, optionId): For questionType=single_choice, set the selection for that questionId to the chosen optionId, replacing any previous choice. For questionType=multiple_choice, toggle the presence of optionId in the selection set for that questionId. For questionType=scale, set the selection to the chosen optionId, ensuring only one option is selected.
onSelectionChange: Update an internal and shared selection structure that maps each questionId to one or more AIReadinessOption.id values.

onValidateBeforeSubmit: Check that every question in the loaded set has at least one selected option consistent with its questionType. If any question is missing a selection, expose validation errors per question and block submission.
onResetAnswers: Clear all selections for all questions (used when retaking the assessment) and clear any validation error messages.

components.functionality: Provides submission and control actions (submit, reset/retake) for the assessment form and coordinates calling submitAIReadinessAssessment with the selected answerOptionIds.
event_handlers: onClickSubmit: Request validation from assessment-question-list. If validation passes, collect all selected AIReadinessOption.id values from the shared selection state and call submitAIReadinessAssessment({ answerOptionIds }).

onSubmitSuccess(response): Receive the response containing success, assessmentSession, and recommendedOffering. If success is true, store assessmentSession and recommendedOffering in shared page state and instruct assessment-init-loader to update the URL parameter to view=results and switch to the results view.
onSubmitError(error): Set an error state to show an inline error message and provide a 'Retry' button that re-calls submitAIReadinessAssessment with the same answerOptionIds.

onClickReset: Clear current selections by invoking assessment-question-list.onResetAnswers and clear any submission error state. This may be used before initial submission or when the user wants to clear the form without switching views.

components.functionality: Shows the AI readiness assessment results using the returned AIReadinessAssessmentSession and the recommended ConsultingOffering. It presents score and readiness information, explains that the recommendation is based on the assessment, and exposes navigation CTAs.
event_handlers: onRender: Read assessmentSession and recommendedOffering from shared page state. If both are present, display overallScore and readinessLevel fields from assessmentSession and key fields from recommendedOffering such as name, shortDescription, offeringType, price, priceType, and any highlightBullets, without hardcoding specific values.
onMissingSession: If assessmentSession or recommendedOffering is not available when view=results, delegate to assessment-empty-results-state instead of rendering results.

onClickRetakeAssessment: Clear assessmentSession and recommendedOffering from shared state if necessary, call assessment-question-list.onResetAnswers, and instruct assessment-init-loader to change URL parameter to view=questions and switch back to the questions view.

onClickBookConsultationNow: Construct a navigation URL to book_consultation.html that includes `serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation`, `offeringId` taken from recommendedOffering.id, `sourceType=from_assessment`, and `sourceReferenceId` taken from assessmentSession.id, then invoke browser navigation to that URL.

components.functionality: Encapsulates the display and interactions for the recommended consulting service derived from the assessment, including CTAs to learn more or proceed to booking.
event_handlers: onRender: Receive a ConsultingOffering object (recommendedOffering) and render its fields such as name, tagline, shortDescription, offeringType, price, currency, priceType, typicalDurationWeeks, and any highlightBullets, relying entirely on data passed in rather than hardcoded content.
onClickLearnMore: Navigate to offering_detail.html with query parameters `offeringId` set to recommendedOffering.id and `sourceType=from_assessment`.
onClickBookNow: Navigate to offering_detail.html with the same parameters as onClickLearnMore, allowing offering_detail.html to handle further booking or inquiry actions.

onClickBookConsultationNowFromCard: Optionally, if exposed from within the card rather than the surrounding panel, navigate directly to book_consultation.html with query parameters constructed from the bound assessmentSession and recommendedOffering: `serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation`, `offeringId=recommendedOffering.id`, `sourceType=from_assessment`, and `sourceReferenceId=assessmentSession.id`.

components.functionality: Handles the case when the URL indicates a results view (view=results) but there is no assessmentSession data available in the current page state, guiding the user to start a new assessment.
event_handlers: onRender: Check if the global state has an assessmentSession and recommendedOffering. If not, display an informational state instead of the normal results panel content.

onClickStartAssessment: Instruct assessment-init-loader to update the URL parameter to view=questions (or remove it) and switch to the questions view, triggering getAIReadinessQuestions if questions have not yet been loaded in this session.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The assessment page reuses the hero’s two‑column composition: a content‑heavy left column and a visually prominent right column. At the top of the assessment section (below the global header), a compact page header mirrors the hero structure: a small all‑caps context label (e.g., “AI READINESS ASSESSMENT”), a large, multi‑line H1, and a short explanatory paragraph, all stacked with generous vertical spacing. Directly under this header, the left column continues with the assessment-question-list as a vertical stack of question blocks. The right column, aligned to the top of the intro, is dedicated to the dynamic outcome experience: the assessment-results-panel sits at the top, and the assessment-recommended-service-card sits beneath it. The assessment-submit-bar anchors the bottom of the viewport on the left, echoing the hero’s horizontal CTA row but implemented as a sticky action bar that stays visible while scrolling. The assessment-init-loader and assessment-empty-results-state are layered states on top of this structure, temporarily replacing or overlaying the core content as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The assessment page reuses the hero’s two‑column composition: a content‑heavy left column and a visually prominent right column. At the top of the assessment section (below the global header), a compact page header mirrors the hero structure: a small all‑caps context label (e.g., “AI READINESS ASSESSMENT”), a large, multi‑line H1, and a short explanatory paragraph, all stacked with generous vertical spacing. Directly under this header, the left column continues with the assessment-question-list as a vertical stack of question blocks. The right column, aligned to the top of the intro, is dedicated to the dynamic outcome experience: the assessment-results-panel sits at the top, and the assessment-recommended-service-card sits beneath it. The assessment-submit-bar anchors the bottom of the viewport on the left, echoing the hero’s horizontal CTA row but implemented as a sticky action bar that stays visible while scrolling. The assessment-init-loader and assessment-empty-results-state are layered states on top of this structure, temporarily replacing or overlaying the core content as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses the same centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) on a dark, charcoal background. Within this container, the left column (questions and intro) occupies roughly 60–65% of the width, while the right column (results and recommended service) uses the remaining 35–40%. Vertically, the assessment section fills nearly the full initial viewport height, with 64–96px padding above and below the main content to maintain the spacious hero feel. The page header elements (eyebrow, H1, intro text) together take about the top third of the visible left column above the fold, with the question list beginning just below. On the right, the results panel dominates the upper portion (roughly two-thirds of the right column height above the fold), while the recommended service card occupies the lower portion. The submit bar runs horizontally across the bottom of the left column area, with enough height (~64px) to feel substantial but not bulky.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low and editorial. The intro header elements have clear separation: 8–12px between eyebrow and H1, 16–24px between H1 and supporting copy, and 32–40px before the first question. Each question block in the assessment-question-list is treated like a card with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px vertical spacing between questions. The results panel and recommended service card both have generous internal padding and distinct separation (24–32px) from each other. There are no tightly packed lists; each piece of information has breathing room, aligning with the 8px baseline grid. The submit bar uses a clean, single-row layout with clearly separated primary and secondary actions, avoiding clutter. Loader and empty states are minimalist, with a single central focal element (spinner or icon) and succinct text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup’s Z-pattern. The user’s eye moves from the top-left context label and bold H1 down to the supporting paragraph, then into the first question in the left column. From there, attention is drawn horizontally to the right column where the results panel (or its empty state) lives as a strong counterbalance, similar to the hero portrait. After noticing the results, the eye naturally continues downward to the recommended service card below. The sticky submit bar at the bottom-left becomes the persistent call to action, anchoring the lower line of the Z and remaining in peripheral awareness as users scroll through questions. On initial load, if the loader is visible, it temporarily becomes the primary focal point, centered in the assessment area. Before results exist, the empty-results-state gives the right column a clear, inviting placeholder that subtly signals the payoff for completing the questions.

layout.overall_description: The AI Readiness Assessment page sits on a dark, charcoal canvas with a centered, max-width container that inherits the site’s minimalist, tech-forward aesthetic. Below the global header and navigation, the assessment section opens with a compact, hero-like intro on the left: a small all-caps label, a large white H1 headline spanning two or three lines, and a short, high-contrast paragraph explaining what the assessment will provide. This text block occupies roughly two-thirds of the left column’s upper area, with generous negative space around it. Directly beneath, the questions flow downward as a column of spacious, card-like blocks. To the right, aligned with the top of the intro text, a substantial results panel occupies about a third of the page width, forming a strong visual counterweight much like the portrait in the original hero. Beneath that, a recommended service card continues the right-column stack, maintaining a clear gap between elements. A sticky, horizontally oriented submit bar anchors to the bottom of the left column, ensuring the primary CTA is always visible as users scroll through questions. Prior to question load, a centered loader overlays the main assessment area; before submission, the right column is filled by a muted but clear empty-state panel rather than being left blank. The overall effect is a calm, spacious, and professional interface where the left-side interaction (answering questions) and right-side payoff (readiness score and consulting recommendation) feel tightly connected, framed within a modern, corporate dark-mode layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, the assessment-init-loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay covering the entire assessment section beneath the global header, spanning the full width of the centered container and extending from just below the page’s main navigation down to the bottom padding of the assessment area. The background of the overlay subtly darkens the underlying two-column layout without obscuring its structure completely, preserving a sense of depth. At the visual center of the viewport (both horizontally within the container and vertically within the visible area), a minimalist circular spinner or progress indicator is displayed, with a short status line directly beneath it. The loader respects the spacious design: the spinner and text are not cramped, with at least 24–32px of padding around them. All interactive elements underneath (questions, submit bar, results, service card) are visually dimmed and functionally disabled while the loader is present, making the loader the clear primary focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a temporary, modal-like overlay anchored to the assessment container; it is positioned fixed relative to the viewport within the container bounds so it remains centered even when the page scrolls, and it disappears once initial data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment-question-list occupies the left column of the main assessment area, directly below the intro H1 and supporting paragraph. It starts approximately 32–40px beneath the intro text to create a clear transition from explanatory content to interactive content. Horizontally, it spans about 60–65% of the max-width container, aligned flush with the left safe margin, leaving a generous gutter between it and the right column. The list is a vertical stack of card-like question blocks, each full width within the column, with 24–32px of vertical space between blocks. Each question card has 16–24px of internal padding and uses the full available width, with no side-by-side questions, emphasizing a linear, focused flow. As the user scrolls, the question list extends downward, with the viewport showing a subset of questions at any time while the sticky submit bar remains visible at the bottom-left. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses to a single column, the question list stretches to 100% of the container width and remains positioned directly below the intro, with the same vertical stacking behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally within the page; its top position is static relative to the intro header, while the bottom can extend well beyond the viewport. It works in tandem with the sticky assessment-submit-bar, which overlays the bottom edge of this column on taller lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment-submit-bar is a horizontally oriented strip anchored at the bottom of the left column, echoing the hero’s row of CTAs but as a persistent utility bar. On desktop, it spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the container width) and is aligned with the left safe margin. Vertically, it is about 56–64px tall, with 16–20px of padding inside. A subtle top border or soft shadow visually separates it from the scrolling question content above, giving the impression of a docked control area. Inside the bar, the primary action button (e.g., “Calculate Readiness”) sits on the left, occupying roughly one-third of the bar’s width, with a secondary action (e.g., “Save for later” or a text link) positioned to its right with clear spacing. The bar respects the minimalist aesthetic: no excessive labels, just core actions and perhaps a small, subdued line of helper text aligned to the right or beneath the secondary action if needed. On mobile, the submit bar spans 100% of the container width at the bottom of the viewport; the primary button stretches nearly full width, with secondary actions stacked below or right-aligned, maintaining clear 8–16px spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom of the viewport within the assessment area. As the user scrolls the questions, the bar remains fixed at the bottom-left portion of the screen (or full width on mobile), staying always visible until the user reaches the page footer or leaves the assessment section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment-results-panel occupies the upper portion of the right column, visually analogous to the portrait in the original hero. It is aligned to the top of the main content area, starting level with or slightly below the H1 on the left, so that the top edges of the intro content and the results panel feel aligned across the two columns. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the right column (approximately 35–40% of the container), with a consistent gutter separating it from the question list. The panel is styled as a prominent card on the dark background, with a slightly lighter panel tone, soft radius corners, and generous internal padding (24–32px). It should be tall enough to comfortably contain a visual score element (like a gauge, bar, or large numeric score) at the top, with summary text and key breakdowns beneath, while still leaving vertical space below for the recommended service card. Above the fold on a typical desktop viewport, the results panel occupies roughly the top two-thirds of the right column’s visible height. On smaller screens, the results panel moves below the question list in the reading order, becoming a full-width block with the same padded, card-like appearance and at least 48–64px of spacing separating it from the last question or the submit area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls naturally with the page. On tall viewports with short content, it appears to sit comfortably centered in the right column; on long pages, it anchors the top of the right column content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment-recommended-service-card sits directly below the assessment-results-panel in the right column, forming the second piece of the right-side vertical stack. It maintains a clear vertical gap of about 24–32px from the bottom of the results panel, preserving the airy, editorial feel. The card matches the width of the results panel, filling the right column from left gutter to right safe margin, and uses similar corner radius and a slightly lighter panel tone to tie the two elements together visually. Its height is moderate—roughly one-third to one-half the height of the results panel—designed to hold a concise label (e.g., “Recommended Next Step”), a bold service name or package title, one or two short lines of descriptive copy, and a prominent CTA button aligned either at the bottom-right or full-width across the card. Internally, there is generous padding (24px) all around, and the content is stacked top-to-bottom with 8–16px between lines. On mobile, this card follows immediately after the results panel in the single-column flow, separated by the same 24–32px, and spans 100% of the container width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the right column stack; scrolls with the page, always appearing directly after the results panel and remaining visually connected as a follow-up action area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Before the user submits the assessment, the assessment-empty-results-state occupies the same spatial footprint as the assessment-results-panel within the right column so that the layout remains balanced from the start. It appears as a subtle, low-intensity card aligned at the top of the right column, matching the width of the future results panel and leaving room below for the recommended service card area (which may be hidden or subdued until a result exists). The empty state card has generous padding (24–32px) and centers its content both vertically and horizontally within the panel: a small, minimalist icon or abstract illustration near the top center, followed by a concise heading (e.g., “Your AI readiness will appear here”) and a short line of body text encouraging the user to complete the questions. The typography is lighter and slightly smaller than the main H1, ensuring that the user’s primary focus remains on the left column questions while still understanding that this is where outcomes will show. On mobile, the empty state sits below the question list or just above the submit area, occupying a full-width card with the same centered content and ample whitespace around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static placeholder state in the right column; it is replaced or visually transformed into the full assessment-results-panel once the user submits their responses. It does not stick or float; it scrolls with the page like the results panel it represents.

name: Events & Webinars
title: Events & Webinars
description: Browse and filter events and webinars by date range, topic, and time of day, and navigate to detailed event information.

core_features: Load available filter options (date ranges, topic categories, time-of-day presets) using the getEventFilterOptions interface and render them in a filter form.
Initialize filter state from URL parameters (dateRangeKey, topicCategory, timeOfDayFrom, timeOfDayTo) so that the page supports deep-linking and bookmarked views.

Execute event searches using the searchEvents interface based on the current filter state (date range, topic category, time range, optional text query) and display the resulting list of Event records.
Highlight events and webinars that match AI marketing–relevant topic categories within the search results, based on configured mappings to Event.topicCategory.

Allow users to refine filters (date range, topicCategory, optional explicit startDate/endDate, time-of-day range, and text query) and update the displayed results without requiring a full navigation away from the page.
Provide navigation from each listed event to an event detail page (event_detail.html) via a link that passes the selected Event.id as the eventId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Load page with optional preset filters from URL
1. User navigates to events.html, potentially with URL parameters (dateRangeKey, topicCategory, timeOfDayFrom, timeOfDayTo).
2. The page reads the current URL parameters and constructs an initial filter state.
3. The page calls getEventFilterOptions to retrieve available date range, topic, and time-of-day slot options.
4. The filter form is populated using the data from getEventFilterOptions and pre-selected according to the initial filter state derived from the URL.
5. The page calls searchEvents with the initial filter state (mapping dateRangeKey, topicCategory, timeOfDayFrom, timeOfDayTo and any derived startDate/endDate or textQuery) to obtain the initial set of events.
6. The results list is rendered using the returned Event objects; AI marketing–relevant events are flagged within the list according to configured topicCategory mappings.

Workflow 2: User refines filters and updates results
1. User adjusts one or more filter controls: selects a different date range option, changes topicCategory, chooses a time-of-day preset (mapped to timeOfDayFrom/timeOfDayTo), manually sets a custom time range, and/or enters a free-text query.
2. On filter change or on explicit filter form submission (depending on implementation), the filter state object is updated in memory.
3. The URL query string is updated to reflect the new filter state, ensuring that dateRangeKey, topicCategory, timeOfDayFrom, timeOfDayTo, and any additional filter parameters (such as explicit startDate/endDate or textQuery) are synchronized.
4. The page calls searchEvents with the updated filter parameters.
5. The results list is refreshed with the new Event results and totalCount; highlighted AI marketing events are recalculated based on the new result set.

Workflow 3: User focuses on AI marketing–relevant webinars
1. User either selects a topicCategory that corresponds to AI/marketing personas (for example, a category that backend configuration defines as GenAI marketing–related) or uses a dedicated control that sets topicCategory to that value.
2. The filter state is updated, URL parameters are synchronized, and searchEvents is called with the updated topicCategory.
3. The results list displays events filtered to that topic; events that match the AI marketing–relevant categories are visually highlighted according to functional rules.
4. User reviews highlighted items and chooses one to view in detail.

Workflow 4: Navigate from event list to event detail
1. Within the results list, the user clicks on an event title or a dedicated “View details” action.
2. The click handler retrieves the corresponding Event.id from the bound data.
3. The browser navigates to event_detail.html with eventId set to that Event.id in the URL (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId=<Event.id>).
4. The event_detail.html page (outside the scope of this specification) uses eventId to load and render full event details.

Workflow 5: Use time-of-day presets from filter options
1. User opens the time-of-day filter control.
2. The control shows presets retrieved from getEventFilterOptions.timeOfDaySlots (each with key, label, startTime, endTime).
3. User selects a preset; the client maps the preset’s startTime and endTime to timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo in the filter state.
4. URL parameters timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo are updated accordingly.
5. searchEvents is called with the updated time-of-day bounds, and the results list is refreshed to show only events that fall within the selected time window.
interactions: On initial page load, a lifecycle handler reads URL parameters, calls getEventFilterOptions, initializes the filter form, and then calls searchEvents to populate the event list.

When the user changes the date range selection, the dateRangeKey in the filter state is updated, the corresponding URL parameter is updated, and searchEvents is re-invoked with the new dateRangeKey and any existing filters.
When the user selects a topic category, the topicCategory in the filter state and URL is updated, and searchEvents is called to return events for that topic.

When the user selects a time-of-day preset, the selected slot’s startTime and endTime are mapped to timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo in the filter state, those values are written to the URL, and searchEvents is called with the updated time-of-day range.
When the user manually edits custom time-of-day inputs (if provided), the new timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo values are validated, applied to filter state and URL, and used to call searchEvents.

When the user optionally enters or updates a free-text query, the textQuery field in filter state is updated; on search action, URL parameters are synchronized (including textQuery), and searchEvents is called.

When searchEvents returns, the results list component receives the Event array and totalCount, updates its internal state, and re-renders the list, including any highlighting logic for AI marketing–relevant events.
When the user clicks on an event title or a dedicated details link, an onClick handler reads the bound Event.id and navigates to event_detail.html with eventId set in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s authoritative state is stored in the URL query parameters and a synchronized in-memory filter state object. On load, the page parses dateRangeKey, topicCategory, timeOfDayFrom, and timeOfDayTo from the URL. If any of these parameters are missing, the implementation may define defaults, but these defaults are not hardcoded in this specification; instead they are derived from application configuration or from the options returned by getEventFilterOptions. The filter state also includes optional values for startDate, endDate, and textQuery, which are maintained in memory and mirrored to URL parameters when set.

The URL-to-state mapping works as follows:
- dateRangeKey: When present in the URL, is used directly as the dateRangeKey parameter for searchEvents and to select the matching option in the date range filter. When the user changes the date range in the UI, dateRangeKey is updated in both filter state and URL.
- topicCategory: When present, initializes the topic filter and is passed to searchEvents. Any UI changes to topicCategory update the URL and trigger a new search.
- timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo: When present, initialize the time-of-day constraints. These values are passed through as parameters to searchEvents. When the user selects a time-of-day preset from getEventFilterOptions.timeOfDaySlots, the slot’s startTime and endTime values are assigned to timeOfDayFrom and timeOfDayTo, which are then written to the URL and used in subsequent calls to searchEvents.
- Additional optional parameters such as startDate, endDate, and textQuery are included in the query string when present and are passed through to searchEvents.

Any change initiated by the user in the filter controls updates the in-memory filter state and then updates the URL query parameters to match. After the URL is updated, searchEvents is called with the current filter state. The Event results returned by searchEvents, along with totalCount, are considered derived state and are not encoded into the URL; they are just held in memory for rendering. Highlighting of AI marketing–relevant events is computed client-side from each Event’s topicCategory (and any other relevant attributes) within the current results and does not affect URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, URL parameter parsing, filter state management, API calls to getEventFilterOptions and searchEvents, and distribution of data to other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad
onUrlChange
onFilterStateChange
onSearchResultsReceived

components.functionality: Displays and manages the filters for date range, topic category, time-of-day (preset and/or custom range), optional explicit date range, and optional text query; triggers searches when users update filters.
event_handlers: onDateRangeChange
onTopicCategoryChange
onTimeOfDayPresetChange
onTimeOfDayFromChange
onTimeOfDayToChange
onStartDateChange
onEndDateChange
onTextQueryChange
onFilterSubmit
onFilterReset

components.functionality: Receives the array of Event objects and totalCount from searchEvents and renders a list of events including key data: title, start/end date-time, topicCategory, format, deliveryMode, and status; supports navigation to event_detail.html.
event_handlers: onResultItemClick
onViewDetailsClick
onResultsUpdate

components.functionality: Given the current Event results, identifies events whose topicCategory is configured as AI marketing–relevant (for example, generative AI for marketing topics) and surfaces them in a dedicated highlighted subset for quick access.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate
onHighlightedEventClick

components.functionality: Displays an informative message and optional suggestions (such as adjusting filters) when searchEvents returns zero results for the current filter state.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate

components.functionality: Shows the totalCount of events returned by the most recent call to searchEvents, providing context for the user about how many events match the current filters.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page inherits the mockup’s structure by placing a horizontal control band near the top of the content (filter bar + metadata) and a two-column main area beneath it. The left column is a vertically stacked flow: filter bar label (implicitly via layout), then the filters, then a small textual summary of the results (total-count-indicator), followed by the results-list or empty-state. The right column plays the role of the hero portrait area from the mockup, but here it is a vertical ai-marketing-highlight-strip that showcases featured AI/marketing events. All interactive controls and primary reading content follow a top-to-bottom, left-to-right hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page inherits the mockup’s structure by placing a horizontal control band near the top of the content (filter bar + metadata) and a two-column main area beneath it. The left column is a vertically stacked flow: filter bar label (implicitly via layout), then the filters, then a small textual summary of the results (total-count-indicator), followed by the results-list or empty-state. The right column plays the role of the hero portrait area from the mockup, but here it is a vertical ai-marketing-highlight-strip that showcases featured AI/marketing events. All interactive controls and primary reading content follow a top-to-bottom, left-to-right hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), the main content is split into a 60–65% left column for the events-results-list (and empty-state) and a 35–40% right column for the ai-marketing-highlight-strip. The top control band (events-filter-bar + total-count-indicator) spans nearly the full container width and sits above the two-column split. Generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separates the page header from the filter band, and another 24–32px separates the filter band from the results section, mirroring the breathable hero layout from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout stays low-density and editorial: each component sits in its own clearly defined block with ample white (or dark) space surrounding it. Filters are presented in a single, clean horizontal row with roomy padding and spacing between controls. Event cards in the results-list are separated by 24–32px vertical gaps, and the highlight strip uses distinct card-like items spaced similarly to avoid visual clutter. The total-count-indicator is a small, quiet element with extra breathing room above and below to prevent crowding the filters or list.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern: the eye first lands on the page title (outside this spec), then moves slightly down and across the top control band from left to right (events-filter-bar, then count, then any right-aligned filter controls). From there, the gaze drops diagonally to the top of the results-list in the left column and then scans horizontally to the right column where the ai-marketing-highlight-strip resides as a visual counterbalance. When the list is empty, the empty-state message becomes the focal point in the left column, with the highlight strip on the right still acting as an alternate attention target. The events-state-manager is invisible but orchestrates changes in this flow, ensuring smooth transitions as filters change the list, count, and empty state.

layout.overall_description: Within a dark, modern, centered viewport, the Events & Webinars page opens beneath the global header with a spacious top margin before the main content. A single max-width container holds everything: at the top of this container, a sleek horizontal filter band runs nearly edge-to-edge, visually similar to a minimalist toolbar. Its controls are laid out in a single row with clear spacing, aligned to the left, leaving comfortable negative space on the right for balance or secondary filter elements. Just below this band and aligned to the left, a subtle line of text shows the current number of matching events, acting like a contextual tag and gently anchoring the list. The main body then splits into two columns: on the left, a tall vertical stack of event cards fills about two-thirds of the width, scrolling down the page; on the right, a slightly narrower vertical strip presents highlighted AI/marketing events in a compact, high-contrast style reminiscent of the portrait area from the hero in the mockup. The spacing between the filter band, count text, list, and highlight strip is generous, so each cluster feels distinct yet cohesive. When filters yield no matches, the left column is taken over by a centered empty-state card that sits at roughly mid-height of the viewport, while the right column remains as a steady visual anchor with suggested AI-focused content, maintaining the balanced two-column composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-state-manager is a non-visual, structural layer that conceptually sits just inside the main content container, above all other events-related components. Think of it as occupying a zero-height strip at the very top of the page’s events section, aligned with the left edge of the content grid and spanning the same width as the container. It does not render any visible UI but governs the interactions between the filter bar, total count, results list, empty state, and highlight strip. As users adjust filters, this controller updates the results-list content, the total-count-indicator, and decides whether the events-empty-state should replace the list area. It also determines which items appear in the ai-marketing-highlight-strip if these are contextually linked to the current filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically positioned at the top of the events module in the DOM hierarchy, but with no visual footprint; it does not affect layout spacing and behaves as a normal, non-sticky, non-floating controller layer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-filter-bar sits near the top of the Events & Webinars content area, directly beneath the page title/intro copy (if present) and above the two-column content split. It is centered within the main container and spans roughly 90–100% of the container width, with 24–32px of padding above and below. The bar itself appears as a single horizontal row of filter controls, aligned to the left, following the minimal, high-contrast design language: a subdued dark background panel (slightly lighter than the page background) with crisp, white labels and subtle accent outlines. From left to right, controls are arranged as: a small, all-caps label such as “Filters” or icon-label cluster, then a date-range selector, a topic dropdown or pill-group, and a time-of-day selector, each separated by 16–24px gaps. Vertical height of the bar including padding is around 72–88px, giving the controls plenty of breathing room without feeling bulky. On larger screens, the bar visually reads as a continuous strip that anchors the top of the main content, similar in weight to a sub-header. There is clear space (24–32px) below the bar before the total-count-indicator and the start of the results list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the filter bar can be configured as sticky, pinning itself to the top of the viewport once scrolled into place, with an offset to sit just below the global navigation (e.g., 64–80px from the top). On smaller screens, it becomes full-width and non-sticky to avoid consuming too much vertical space, stacking its controls in 1–2 rows as needed while maintaining generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-total-count-indicator is positioned directly beneath the events-filter-bar within the left side of the container, aligned to the same left edge as the list content. It sits in a slender horizontal row that occupies roughly 60–65% of the container width (matching the left column), with 8–16px of vertical space above and below to keep it visually separated. The text is relatively small and understated, styled similarly to the all-caps context line from the mockup’s hero: light weight, slightly condensed, and in a muted off-white or gray to avoid competing with primary headlines. The content might read “12 upcoming events” or “3 webinars matching your filters,” and is left-aligned, forming a clear anchor above the first event card. On larger screens, there is ample negative space to the right of this indicator before the right column begins, reinforcing its association with the results list rather than the highlight strip. On mobile, it remains just above the list, full-width, but retains its modest vertical footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document order beneath the filter bar; non-sticky and non-floating. When the empty-state is shown, the count indicator still appears above it, potentially with a different message (e.g., “0 events found”), maintaining its position and serving as contextual confirmation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-results-list occupies the primary left column of the main content area, beginning immediately below the events-total-count-indicator. It spans approximately 60–65% of the container width on desktop, starting flush with the container’s left padding and ending around two-thirds across the page, where a clear vertical gutter (24–32px) separates it from the right column. Vertically, the list forms a clean, scrollable stack of event cards that extend down the page. Each event card is full-width within this column, with 24–32px vertical spacing between cards, echoing the mockup’s spacious, editorial feel. The first card appears about 16–24px below the total-count-indicator, preserving a clear hierarchy from metadata to content. Cards themselves can use a left-to-right internal layout (date/time on the left, title and description in the center, action on the right) but maintain generous padding (16–24px) and minimal borders to stay in line with the minimalist style. On large screens, the top of the list visually aligns with the top of the ai-marketing-highlight-strip on the right, forming a balanced horizontal axis. On smaller screens (tablet and below), the list expands to nearly full width, and the right column content drops beneath it, preserving the vertical reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the page, not sticky. On desktop, it remains constrained to the left column, while the right column’s highlight strip may use sticky positioning; the list itself simply continues to scroll beneath the fixed filter bar (if the bar is set to sticky). When no results are available, this list area is fully replaced by the events-empty-state component, occupying the same spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ai-marketing-highlight-strip resides in the right column of the main content region, acting as the visual counterweight to the events-results-list in the same way the portrait balanced the hero text in the mockup. On desktop, it starts at the same vertical position as the first event card (aligned with the top edge of the list area) but is horizontally positioned to the right, occupying the remaining 35–40% of the container width. A vertical gutter of about 24–32px separates it from the left column, maintaining clear separation and breathing room. Within this strip, 2–4 compact highlight cards stack vertically, each representing featured AI or marketing-related events or series. Each card uses a slightly different background shade or subtle accent border to differentiate it from the main list, with 16–24px padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. The overall height of the strip is typically shorter than a full viewport but tall enough to feel substantial; as the user scrolls down the results list, this column remains visible, echoing the fixed portrait presence in the mockup. On smaller screens, the highlight strip breaks out of the two-column layout and appears beneath the main results-list as a full-width section, separated by 32–48px of vertical space so it reads as a distinct, secondary section rather than a competing primary column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, the highlight strip should use sticky positioning within its column, locking to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, with an offset that matches or slightly exceeds the filter bar’s sticky offset (e.g., 96px from the top). This ensures it stays in view while the longer left column scrolls. On smaller screens, it loses sticky behavior and becomes a normal block element flowing beneath the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events-empty-state occupies the same spatial area as the events-results-list and is only rendered when there are no events matching the current filters. It sits in the left column below the events-total-count-indicator, maintaining the same left alignment and column width (about 60–65% of the container). Instead of a vertical stack of event cards, a single centered empty-state card appears. This card is generously sized, with 32–48px of internal padding and ample margin above and below so it feels like a focal point rather than a small alert. It is horizontally centered within the left column and vertically positioned so that, on larger screens, its main message sits roughly in the upper-middle third of the viewport, still clearly below the filter bar. The design mirrors the hero’s spaciousness: a simple icon or illustration at the top, a clear headline like “No events match your filters,” a brief explanatory line, and optionally a small CTA (e.g., “Reset filters” or links to featured content). Throughout, there is generous vertical spacing (16–24px) between these elements. To the right, the ai-marketing-highlight-strip remains visible in its usual position, giving the page balance and offering alternative AI/marketing content even when the main list is empty.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the events-results-list in the layout when active, occupying its space in the normal document flow. It scrolls normally if its content is taller than the viewport but is typically short enough to fit comfortably without scrolling on most displays. It does not impact the sticky behavior of the filter bar or the right-column highlight strip.

name: Event Detail & Registration
title: Event Detail & Registration
description: Displays details for a single event or webinar and provides an embedded registration flow tied to that event.

core_features: Read the eventId URL parameter and load the corresponding event using getEventDetail.
Display event details including title, description, date, time, time zone, format, delivery mode, topic category, tags, target audience, and status.
Provide a primary registration call-to-action that scrolls or jumps to the on-page registration form.
Embedded registration form that collects attendeeName, attendeeEmail, attendeeRoleTitle, attendeeCompany, and receiveUpdates, tied to the current eventId.
Validate registration inputs before submission (e.g., required fields and email format).
Submit registrations via submitEventRegistration, handling loading, success, and error states.
Display registration confirmation state and basic next-step instructions after successful submission.
Provide a contextual post-registration CTA that navigates to book_consultation.html with appropriate URL parameters referencing the current event.
Allow navigation back to the main events listing (events.html).
user_workflows: Event discovery from Events or Home: User clicks an event from events.html or index.html, which navigates to event_detail.html with the corresponding eventId in the URL. On page load, the event detail data loader reads eventId and calls getEventDetail. The page displays loading feedback until the event data is returned, or an error state if loading fails.

Reviewing event information: Once getEventDetail succeeds, the page shows event basics (title, date/time, timezone, format, target audience, status) and full description. The user can navigate back to events.html via a 'Back to all events' link or proceed to register via the primary 'Register' button.

Starting registration from the CTA: When the user clicks the primary 'Register' button, the page scrolls or focuses to the registration form section tied to the current eventId. If the event status indicates it is not open for registration (e.g., completed or cancelled), the CTA can be disabled or replaced with informational messaging, while still displaying event details.

Completing and submitting the registration form: The user fills in name, email, role/job title, company, and optionally toggles receiveUpdates. On submit, client-side validation checks required fields and email format. If validation passes, the form calls submitEventRegistration with eventId and the entered values, then shows a submitting state until the response is received.

Handling registration success: If submitEventRegistration returns success, the page updates its registration state to 'success', stores the returned EventRegistration object in memory, optionally updates the URL (e.g., adding registrationStatus=success and registrationId) for sharable confirmation state, replaces or supplements the form with a confirmation message, and displays contextual instructions (e.g., that the user will receive an email with joining details based on their provided email). A 'Talk to an AI strategist' CTA becomes visible, linking to book_consultation.html with serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_event, and sourceReferenceId set to the current eventId.

Handling registration errors: If submitEventRegistration returns success=false or an error occurs, the form shows an error message, keeps user-entered values in the fields, and allows resubmission after the user corrects any problems.

Post-registration consultation workflow: After successful registration, the user can click the 'Talk to an AI strategist' CTA. The page navigates to book_consultation.html, appending URL parameters serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_event, and sourceReferenceId equal to the current eventId, enabling the consultation page to pre-contextualize the request as coming from this event.
interactions: Page load (onInit): Read eventId from the URL query string and trigger getEventDetail; update page state based on loading, success, or error.
Click 'Back to all events': Navigate to events.html without additional parameters.
Click 'Register' primary button: Scroll or jump the viewport to the registration form section; optionally set a focus state on the first form field.
Typing into registration fields: Update local form state for attendeeName, attendeeEmail, attendeeRoleTitle, attendeeCompany, and receiveUpdates as the user types or toggles controls.
Submitting the registration form: Validate inputs; if valid, call submitEventRegistration with eventId and the captured field values, then handle response states (loading, success, error).

Handling registration API response: On success, store the returned EventRegistration in page state, optionally update URL parameters to reflect registration success, hide or disable the form, and show confirmation content. On failure, set an error state and display feedback while keeping form values.

Click 'Talk to an AI strategist' CTA (post-registration): Navigate to book_consultation.html with query parameters serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_event, and sourceReferenceId set to the current eventId.

Handling events with external registrationUrl: If Event.registrationUrl is present and configured to be used, the primary 'Register' CTA can open this URL in a new tab or redirect the user, while still allowing the page to display event details; the embedded form can be hidden in this scenario depending on business rules.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads the eventId query parameter from the URL (e.g., event_detail.html?eventId=...). If eventId is missing, the page enters an 'invalid state' and can display a message prompting navigation back to events.html. When a valid eventId is present, the page calls getEventDetail(eventId) and stores the resulting Event in memory. The event data drives conditional UI states, such as whether the registration CTA is enabled (for upcoming events) or disabled. The registration flow maintains its own in-memory state: form field values, validation errors, submissionInProgress flag, and registrationResult. After a successful submitEventRegistration call, the registrationResult holds the returned EventRegistration. At that point, the page can optionally update the browser URL via the History API to include registrationStatus=success and registrationId=[EventRegistration.id] while preserving eventId, so that a refreshed or shared link can restore the confirmation state. If such parameters are detected on load, the page can show a confirmation view instead of an empty form and, if allowed, perform a lightweight check (e.g., by inferring that a registration exists without re-calling any other interfaces). All significant navigation away from this page (back to events.html or forward to book_consultation.html) is done via URL changes that include relevant parameters, without using localStorage for state persistence.

components.functionality: Reads eventId from the URL, calls getEventDetail(eventId), manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded Event object to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL query string to extract eventId; if missing, set 'invalidEventId' error state.
onEventIdDetected: Invoke getEventDetail(eventId); set loading=true, error=null before the call.
onEventDetailLoaded: When getEventDetail resolves successfully, set loading=false and store the returned Event in component state.
onEventDetailError: When getEventDetail fails (or returns no event), set loading=false and set an appropriate error state to inform the user.

components.functionality: Displays core event information (title, topicCategory, status, format, deliveryMode, tags, targetAudience) and provides a link back to the events listing.
event_handlers: onClickBackToEvents: Navigate to events.html, preserving no additional parameters.
onEventDataChange: Whenever the Event object changes (e.g., after loading), update displayed fields such as title and status accordingly.

components.functionality: Shows the event schedule details based on Event.startDateTime, Event.endDateTime, Event.timeZone, and any other timing-related metadata; may also reflect Event.status to indicate whether the event is upcoming, completed, or cancelled.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When Event is loaded or updated, render the schedule section with the correct date/time and timeZone.
onStatusChange: When Event.status indicates completed or cancelled, update the schedule section to reflect that the event is no longer open for registration.

components.functionality: Displays the main descriptive content for the event, such as Event.description and target audience details, to help users understand the focus and relevance of the event.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Update the description content when the Event data is loaded or changed.

components.functionality: Provides a primary 'Register' or 'Sign Up' button that scrolls to or focuses the registration form. The CTA may be disabled or replaced with an information state if the event is not open for registration.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Determine whether the event is open for registration (e.g., Event.status is upcoming) and enable or disable the CTA accordingly.

onClickRegister: When clicked and enabled, programmatically scroll the page to the registration form component and set focus to the first input field; optionally set a local 'registrationFormActive' flag used by layout logic.

components.functionality: Handles scenarios where the Event specifies an external registrationUrl. In such cases, it can either use this URL as the primary registration path or present it as an alternative to the embedded form, depending on configuration.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: If Event.registrationUrl is present, configure the 'Register' CTA to open this URL or show an additional 'Register on external site' option.
onClickExternalRegister: Open Event.registrationUrl in a new tab or redirect the browser to this URL when used as the primary registration method.

components.functionality: Collects attendee information for the current event and submits it via submitEventRegistration. Handles validation, submission state, and passes results to the status/confirmation component.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize local form state (attendeeName, attendeeEmail, attendeeRoleTitle, attendeeCompany, receiveUpdates) as empty values and set submission state to idle.
onChangeField: Update the relevant field in local form state when the user types or toggles receiveUpdates.

onValidate: Before submission, check required fields (at minimum attendeeName and attendeeEmail) and validate the email format; if validation fails, set validation error messages and prevent submission.

onSubmit: If validation passes, set submissionInProgress=true and call submitEventRegistration with eventId (from Event.id or URL), attendeeName, attendeeEmail, attendeeRoleTitle, attendeeCompany, and receiveUpdates from form state.

onRegistrationSuccess: When submitEventRegistration returns success=true, set submissionInProgress=false, store the returned EventRegistration object in shared state, clear validation errors, mark the form as 'submitted', and optionally disable further edits.

onRegistrationError: When submitEventRegistration returns success=false or the call fails, set submissionInProgress=false, capture the error message from the response (if provided), and keep form values intact so the user can correct and resubmit.

components.functionality: Displays dynamic messaging about the registration state (idle, submitting, success, error) and surfaces messages or instructions based on submitEventRegistration responses.
event_handlers: onSubmissionStateChange: When the registration form enters or leaves submitting state, toggle a loading indicator.
onRegistrationSuccess: When a valid EventRegistration object is received, show a confirmation message and optionally summarize key details such as the attendeeEmail and eventId.
