onRegistrationError: When an error state is set by the form, display the error message and, if appropriate, provide a link or hint to retry.

components.functionality: Keeps the browser URL query parameters in sync with the registration state so that a successful registration can be reflected in the URL (e.g., registrationStatus=success, registrationId=...) without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onRegistrationSuccess: After a successful submitEventRegistration, update the browser history (pushState/replaceState) to include registrationStatus=success and registrationId set to EventRegistration.id, preserving the existing eventId parameter.

onInitFromUrl: On page load, inspect the URL for registrationStatus and registrationId; if registrationStatus indicates success, set the local registration state to 'confirmed' so that the confirmation view is displayed even on page refresh (without re-submitting).

components.functionality: After a successful registration, provides a contextual CTA that links to book_consultation.html with parameters indicating that the consultation request originates from this event.
event_handlers: onRegistrationSuccess: When EventRegistration state indicates a successful registration, render the CTA button and enable it.

onClickConsultationCTA: Navigate to book_consultation.html with a query string including serviceType=ai_strategy_consultation, sourceType=from_event, and sourceReferenceId set to the current Event.id (or eventId URL parameter).

components.functionality: Handles and displays error or empty states for the event detail view, such as missing eventId, failed getEventDetail call, or an event that cannot be shown.
event_handlers: onInvalidEventId: If no eventId is present in the URL, display a message and present a link or button to return to events.html.
onEventDetailError: If getEventDetail fails or returns no event, show an error message and provide the 'Back to all events' navigation.
onClickBackToEventsFromError: Navigate to events.html when the user chooses to leave the error state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page adopts the same dark, two-column hero pattern as the mockup. At the top of the content, a full-width hero band presents the event in a left-heavy layout: a small all-caps event label, large event title, concise summary, and a horizontal registration CTA cluster stacked vertically in the left column. On the right, a compact but visually strong schedule card mirrors the portrait placement from the mockup. Below the hero, the page continues in a two-column structure: event description and details flow down the left column, while the registration form (or external registration card) sits in a right column that stays visible as the user scrolls. Utility/system components (status banner, loader, error state) occupy slim bands above or instead of the hero, preserving the main layout rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page adopts the same dark, two-column hero pattern as the mockup. At the top of the content, a full-width hero band presents the event in a left-heavy layout: a small all-caps event label, large event title, concise summary, and a horizontal registration CTA cluster stacked vertically in the left column. On the right, a compact but visually strong schedule card mirrors the portrait placement from the mockup. Below the hero, the page continues in a two-column structure: event description and details flow down the left column, while the registration form (or external registration card) sits in a right column that stays visible as the user scrolls. Utility/system components (status banner, loader, error state) occupy slim bands above or instead of the hero, preserving the main layout rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content is constrained to a centered container of ~1100–1200px max width, aligned on a 12-column grid. On desktop, the hero and main body use roughly a 7/12 vs 5/12 split (about 60–65% width left for text and 35–40% right for registration/schedule). Vertically, the hero fills most of the initial viewport, with 80–96px top padding below the global header and similar bottom padding before the next section. Major sections (hero, main details & form, post-registration CTA) are separated by 64–96px vertical gaps. CTAs, cards, and form elements use 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding based on the 8px grid, maintaining comfortable touch targets.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is presented in an editorial, low-density style: one major idea or component per visual cluster. The hero left column has clear separation between eyebrow label, headline, supporting copy, and CTA row, each with generous spacing (16–32px) between them. The schedule card on the right is a single, well-structured card instead of multiple small widgets. In the body, the description is broken into digestible paragraphs with ample line height and white space, and the registration form is wrapped in a card with clear grouping of fields. System banners and status states are minimal and unobtrusive, using compact but readable typography.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern similar to the mockup: users first see the large white event title in the left hero column, then glance upward slightly to the smaller all-caps context label. Their gaze moves down through the summary paragraph to the registration CTA row, then across to the right-hand schedule card. From there, the eye moves downward into the left-column event description and across to the right-column registration form, which acts as the persistent interactive focal point (similar to the original portrait/chat pairing). When present, a slim status banner sits just below the global header and is scanned first, but it is visually lighter so it doesn’t overpower the hero. Scroll-based behavior (sticky CTA bar or form card) keeps registration entry points in the user’s peripheral vision as they read details.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a charcoal or very dark background, with a centered content column that feels precise and controlled. At the very top is the existing global header: brand name on the left, navigation toward the center/right, and discreet social icons at the far right. Directly below, a slim status banner may appear, spanning the content width to communicate registration state without dominating the viewport. The primary hero band of the page fills most of the first screen: on the left, a vertical stack of small all-caps event category, a large multi-line white event title, a concise supporting paragraph, and a horizontal row of registration CTAs. On the right, aligned with this stack, a tall schedule card presents date, time, format, and key information, echoing the portrait block in the mockup. As users scroll, they move into the main details section where the left column continues with the event description and any agenda or speaker details, while the right column houses a light-surfaced registration form card (or external registration card) that appears slightly elevated against the dark background and can remain stickily positioned within the viewport. Below this, a post-registration consultation CTA occupies a full-width band, using a simpler two-column layout to invite deeper engagement after sign-up. Throughout, sections are separated by generous vertical spacing and aligned on an 8px grid, giving the page a calm, high-end consulting feel that balances narrative content on the left with action-oriented elements on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-permanent overlay that appears centered within the main content area while event data is being fetched. When active, it covers the hero region and the area where the event summary and schedule card would normally appear. Visually, it can be represented as a semi-transparent dark scrim that spans the full width of the central container and at least the height of the hero section (roughly the initial viewport height, minus the global header), with a centered spinner or skeleton structure aligned both horizontally and vertically. The global header remains visible above it; the loader starts immediately beneath the header (and status banner, if present) and extends down until just past the fold, signaling that the page’s primary content is still loading. Once data is available, this overlay disappears entirely, relinquishing its space to the hero content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed within the content column; acts as an overlay that blocks interaction with underlying hero components until loading completes, then fully removed from the DOM flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event header summary forms the left column of the hero, occupying roughly 60–65% of the content width. It is positioned immediately below any status banner and sits flush with the left edge of the main container. Vertically, it has generous top padding (about 80–96px from the bottom of the header/status band) and bottom padding to ensure the hero feels tall and spacious. Inside this block, content is stacked vertically: a small all-caps label (e.g., “LIVE WEBINAR” or “EXECUTIVE BRIEFING”) at the top, followed by a large multi-line event title that takes up a significant portion of the hero height, then a supporting paragraph of 2–4 lines that stretches to about 70% of the column width for comfortable reading. Below the paragraph, the space is reserved for alignment with the registration-cta-bar, which visually reads as part of the summary stack (even though it is a separate component), ensuring the CTA feels integrated with the event message. The right edge of this summary column aligns vertically with the left edge of the schedule card on desktop, creating a clean two-column hero. On tablet and mobile, this summary block shifts to full width and appears above the schedule information, maintaining the same vertical stack order but with slightly reduced top/bottom padding to fit smaller screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero on desktop; on smaller viewports, it becomes a full-width top block in a vertically stacked hero, maintaining its proportional dominance over other hero elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event schedule info is rendered as a prominent card in the right column of the hero, occupying roughly 35–40% of the content width on desktop. It aligns to the top of the hero so that its top edge matches roughly with the top of the event title block, and it extends downward to be visually comparable in height to the content cluster on the left, though it may be slightly shorter depending on content. The card is horizontally inset from the right container edge by the same left/right padding used site-wide, and vertically it has internal padding of 24–32px, giving date, time, timezone, format (online/in-person), and perhaps a simple icon or status badge room to breathe. The background of the card is slightly lighter than the hero’s charcoal background, or subtly bordered, to create a clear yet minimalist separation. On mobile and smaller tablets, this card drops beneath the header summary, stretching to nearly full width (90–100% of the content width) with consistent horizontal padding, and maintains a comfortable vertical gap (24–32px) from the content above and below, preserving the editorial spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero on desktop, forming the right column; on smaller screens, it automatically stacks below the event-header-summary as a full-width card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event description section anchors the left side of the main body content just below the hero. There is a generous vertical gap (64–80px) between the bottom of the hero band and the top of this section to clearly separate the introductory hero from detailed content. On desktop, the description occupies roughly 60–65% of the content width, aligned with the hero’s left column so the left edges are perfectly flush. The top of the description may begin with a secondary heading (e.g., “What You’ll Learn” or “Event Overview”), followed by paragraphs of copy that are set within a comfortable reading width (no more than ~70 characters per line). Additional subsections like bullet-point takeaways or a concise agenda can be stacked beneath, each separated by 24–32px vertical spacing. To the right of this section, in the remaining 35–40% width, the registration form or external registration card sits in parallel, so the user can read and register without scrolling horizontally. On mobile, this description section becomes full width, and its vertical spacing is slightly compressed but still generous (32–48px between clusters), and it typically appears below the registration form card to keep the primary CTA visible earlier in the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, forming the left column on larger screens and shifting to a full-width stacked block on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration CTA bar is visually attached to the bottom of the hero’s left column, appearing immediately beneath the event summary paragraph and aligned with the same left/right boundaries as the text. It spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the content width on desktop) and consists of a horizontal row of one or two primary buttons (e.g., “Register Now”) and possibly a secondary text link (e.g., “Add to Calendar”), arranged left-to-right. The buttons have generous padding (12–16px vertical, 24px horizontal) and enough spacing (16–24px) between each to keep the layout airy. Vertically, there is a clear gap (16–24px) between the summary text above and the CTA bar, and another gap (24–32px) before the bottom edge of the hero, preserving the hero’s open feel. On scroll, particularly on tablet and mobile, this bar can convert into a slim sticky CTA that pins to the top or bottom of the viewport: it then spans nearly the full width of the screen, reduces vertical padding slightly to keep height compact, and maintains the primary registration button in view while users scroll through the details below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero on desktop; becomes a sticky bar (at the top or bottom of the viewport) on smaller viewports after the user scrolls past the hero, ensuring constant visibility of the primary registration action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an event requires registration on an external platform, this component occupies the same spatial footprint as the normal registration form on the right side of the main content. On desktop, it is a card aligned to the right column (35–40% width), vertically aligned with the top of the event description section on the left. The card has a clean, elevated look with a slightly lighter background than the page and a subtle border or shadow, with internal padding of around 24–32px. Within the card, a concise title (e.g., “Register on Partner Site”), a one- or two-line explanation, and a prominent external registration button are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them. The primary button aligns visually with the main “Register” CTA in the hero CTA bar, creating cohesion between entry points. On mobile, this card becomes a full-width block, typically appearing directly beneath the hero (and above the long-form description), with consistent horizontal padding and 32–48px vertical margin separating it from adjacent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card that replaces the event-registration-form in the right column when applicable; follows the same responsive behavior, stacking as a full-width block on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration form is housed in a dedicated card on the right side of the main content, mirroring the hero’s right-column position. On desktop, it occupies about 35–40% of the content width and begins roughly aligned with the top of the event description section to its left, creating a cohesive two-column body. The card itself has a slightly lighter surface than the dark page background (or uses a subtle border) and rounded corners that sit on the 8px grid (e.g., 8px radius). Inside, form fields are stacked vertically with 16px spacing, grouped into logical sections (e.g., contact info, company info) separated by 24–32px. A section title at the top (e.g., “Reserve Your Spot”) uses stronger typography but is still secondary in size to the hero headline. The primary submit button sits at the bottom, full width within the card, with ample top margin (24–32px) from the last field. On desktop and larger tablets, this card can be set to a sticky behavior so that as the user scrolls down the description on the left, the form remains in view on the right until the bottom of its container is reached. On mobile, the form card becomes a full-width section and is placed directly below the hero (and CTA bar) so users encounter it early; the description content then follows below with comfortable spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky right-column card on larger screens, staying in view while the left-column description scrolls; on smaller screens, it becomes a non-sticky full-width block positioned near the top of the scroll (just under the hero).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration status banner is a slim, full-width band that appears at the top of the content area, directly beneath the global header navigation. It spans the entire central container width, respecting the same left/right padding as the main content. Vertically, it is relatively compact—approximately 40–56px tall depending on text length—with a subtle but distinct background tint (for example, a muted accent color) to differentiate it from the darker hero below. The text is centered vertically within the band and left-aligned within the container, making it easy to scan as soon as the page loads. When present (e.g., indicating “Registration Closed,” “Limited Seats Remaining,” or “You’re Registered”), this banner pushes the hero section downward, maintaining a clear separation of about 16–24px between the bottom of the banner and the start of the hero. On mobile, it retains its full-width nature and height but may wrap text to multiple lines while keeping comfortable 8–12px vertical padding inside.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static band anchored directly below the global header; always spans the content width and remains at the top of the page content on all viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render visible UI but influences the state of other visible components (such as pre-selecting ticket types or reflecting registration outcomes). Spatially, it occupies no explicit space in the layout and does not alter the visual structure; instead, it ensures that the hero, form, CTA bar, and status banner reflect the current URL parameters (for example, showing a ‘Thank you’ state or error message). Any changes it triggers—like swapping the registration form for a confirmation message—occur within those respective components’ existing layout footprints.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; operates entirely behind the scenes without affecting physical placement but can toggle the visibility or state of other components within their defined layout areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-registration consultation CTA appears as a distinct section below the main event content and registration card. There is a generous vertical gap (64–80px) between the bottom of the description/form block and the top of this section, giving it a clear, standalone feel. It spans the full width of the central container and can use either a slightly lighter or slightly darker band than the rest of the page to subtly distinguish it as a new chapter in the flow. Internally, it follows a balanced two-column layout: on the left (about 60% width) a brief heading (e.g., “Plan Your AI Strategy Session”) and 1–2 short paragraphs explain the consultation offering; on the right (about 40% width) a bold CTA card or button cluster invites users to “Book a Consultation” or similar. The right-side CTA is visually aligned with the form’s bottom edge above when possible, maintaining a columnar rhythm. On mobile, this section stacks vertically, with the explanatory text appearing first and the CTA button/card centered beneath it, all with ample 24–32px vertical spacing between elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the page’s primary content; follows the same responsive behavior as other two-column sections, collapsing into a vertically stacked layout on smaller devices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an unrecoverable error occurs (such as the event not being found), the event error display occupies the main content area in place of the normal hero, description, and registration components. It is centered within the content container with generous top and bottom padding (at least 96px from the global header and a similar amount before the page footer), effectively creating a standalone hero-like block. The message is vertically stacked: a clear, concise headline (e.g., “This Event Is No Longer Available”) centered or left-aligned within the container, a brief explanatory paragraph beneath it, and a secondary action (such as a button or link back to the events list or homepage). The width of this content is constrained to a narrower column (~50–60% of the container width) to keep the text readable and to accentuate the isolation of the error state. No schedule card, form, or registration CTA is shown; their allocated spaces remain empty, keeping the page visually clean and unambiguous. On mobile, the error block is full width but still uses ample vertical spacing, making it easy to read and act upon without clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the standard hero and main content when active; appears as the main central block beneath the global header, with all other event-related components hidden or suppressed.

name: Project Estimator
title: Project Estimator
description: Interactive page where users configure an AI consulting project (industry, scope, modules, team size, timeline, and budget) to generate a rough investment estimate and optionally proceed to a detailed proposal request.

core_features: Load dynamic configuration options (industries, engagement scope options, module options, budget presets) via getEstimatorConfigOptions and bind them to form controls.
Allow users to select a target industry from a dropdown populated by getEstimatorConfigOptions.industries.
Allow users to choose one or more engagement scope items from options returned by getEstimatorConfigOptions.engagementScopeOptions.
Allow users to select one or more project modules from getEstimatorConfigOptions.moduleOptions, explicitly ensuring that the module whose value is 'mlops_setup' is not preselected by default.
Provide inputs for team size and project timeline in weeks, with validation to ensure required numeric inputs before creating an estimate.
Provide budget range controls bound to numeric min/max and currency fields, with optional quick selection via budget presets returned by getEstimatorConfigOptions.budgetPresets.

On user action (e.g., clicking 'Calculate Estimate' or 'Generate Plan'), call createProjectEstimate with the current configuration and display the returned ProjectEstimate data (estimatedCostMin, estimatedCostMax, summary, and other relevant fields).
Display the calculated estimated investment range and a textual summary, allowing the user to review and adjust configuration and re-run the estimate.

Provide a 'Request Detailed Proposal' / 'Talk to Sales' CTA that is enabled after a successful estimate is generated and navigates to proposal_request.html with URL parameters estimateId and sourceType=from_estimator.
Synchronize key configuration fields with URL query parameters so that the estimator can be prefilled or shared via URL, without using localStorage.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – First-time estimate: (1) User navigates to estimator.html via header/footer. (2) On page load, the app calls getEstimatorConfigOptions to fetch industries, engagement scopes, modules, and budget presets. (3) Once options are loaded, the form is enabled with no modules preselected (in particular excluding 'mlops_setup'). (4) User selects an industry, chooses engagement scope(s), and optionally selects modules. (5) User enters team size and timeline in weeks, and either selects a budget preset or manually enters budget min/max and currency. (6) User clicks the primary action button (e.g., 'Calculate Estimate'). (7) The app validates required inputs; if valid, it calls createProjectEstimate with the current configuration. (8) On success, the app stores the returned estimate object in page state, shows the estimated investment range and summary, and enables the 'Request Detailed Proposal' CTA. (9) User reviews the estimate and decides whether to adjust inputs and recalculate or proceed to the proposal request.

Workflow 2 – Adjusting configuration and recalculating: (1) After seeing the initial estimate, user changes one or more parameters (e.g., adds a module, adjusts team size, or changes budget). (2) Each change updates the internal state and synchronizes query parameters in the URL using history.replaceState. (3) User clicks 'Recalculate' (same button as 'Calculate Estimate'). (4) The app calls createProjectEstimate again with the updated configuration. (5) The new estimate replaces the previous one in the result panel, and the CTA continues to point to the latest estimate.id.

Workflow 3 – Using URL parameters to prefill and optionally auto-calculate: (1) User receives a link to estimator.html that includes query parameters such as industry, scopes, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency, and optionally autoCalculate. (2) On page load, the app calls getEstimatorConfigOptions and, once loaded, parses the URL parameters and pre-populates corresponding form fields (matching values against available options). (3) If autoCalculate is present and set to a truthy value and required fields are present, the app automatically calls createProjectEstimate with the parsed configuration; otherwise, it waits for the user to click 'Calculate Estimate'. (4) The user can then fine-tune parameters and recalculate as needed.

Workflow 4 – Proceeding to detailed proposal: (1) User has a successful estimate in the result panel (estimate object is present in state). (2) User clicks the 'Request Detailed Proposal' or 'Talk to Sales' CTA. (3) The app constructs a navigation URL to proposal_request.html including query parameters estimateId=<estimate.id> and sourceType=from_estimator. (4) Browser navigates to proposal_request.html, where the pre-populated proposal request form (on that page) can use the passed estimateId to link back to the stored ProjectEstimate record.
interactions: On page load: Call getEstimatorConfigOptions with no parameters. While loading, disable form inputs and show a loading state. If the call fails, show an error message and allow the user to retry the load.

Industry selection: User chooses an industry from a dropdown bound to getEstimatorConfigOptions.industries. On change, the app updates internal state, updates the URL parameter industry, and clears any estimate result if previously shown (or marks it as outdated until recalculated).

Engagement scope selection: User selects or deselects engagement scopes via checkboxes or multi-select bound to getEstimatorConfigOptions.engagementScopeOptions values. On change, the app updates internal engagementScope array, updates the URL parameter scopes (comma-separated values), and clears or marks the current estimate as outdated.

Module selection: User selects or deselects modules via checkboxes or multi-select bound to getEstimatorConfigOptions.moduleOptions values. On initial render, no modules are auto-selected, and the option with value 'mlops_setup' remains unselected unless the user explicitly checks it. On change, the app updates the modules array in state, updates the URL parameter modules (comma-separated values), and clears or marks the current estimate as outdated.

Team size input: User enters or adjusts a numeric team size (e.g., using a numeric input or stepper). On change, the app validates the value (e.g., positive integer), updates state, updates the URL parameter teamSize, and clears or marks the estimate as outdated.

Timeline input: User enters or adjusts the project timeline in weeks via numeric input or slider. On change, the app validates the value, updates state, updates the URL parameter timelineWeeks, and clears or marks the estimate as outdated.

Budget range and presets: User can either (a) select a preset from a dropdown populated from getEstimatorConfigOptions.budgetPresets, which sets budgetMin, budgetMax, and currency in state and in URL parameters; or (b) manually enter budgetMin, budgetMax, and select a currency. Any change updates state and synchronizes the URL parameters budgetMin, budgetMax, and currency.

Calculate / Generate button: When the user clicks the primary action button (labelled 'Calculate Estimate' or 'Generate Plan'), the app validates that all required fields (industry, at least one engagementScope, teamSize, timelineWeeks) are present and valid. If validation passes, the app calls createProjectEstimate with current state values. While the call is in progress, the button shows a loading state and is temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.

Estimate result display: On successful response from createProjectEstimate, the app stores the returned estimate (ProjectEstimate) in state and populates the result panel with data such as estimatedCostMin, estimatedCostMax, currency, summary, and key configuration values. Errors from createProjectEstimate are shown in an error area without leaving the page.

Proposal CTA: The 'Request Detailed Proposal' or 'Talk to Sales' button is disabled or hidden until a valid estimate is present in state (estimate.id exists). On click, it builds a navigation link to proposal_request.html?estimateId=<estimate.id>&sourceType=from_estimator and navigates there.

URL-state sync: When the user modifies any configuration field (industry, engagementScope, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency), the app updates the URL query parameters using history.replaceState without reloading the page. When the page loads, it reads these parameters and pre-populates fields, then optionally auto-calls createProjectEstimate if autoCalculate is set and validation passes.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on in-memory state for the current configuration and latest ProjectEstimate, and it uses URL query parameters (not localStorage) to represent and restore configuration. Supported query parameters include: industry (single value matching an industry option value from getEstimatorConfigOptions.industries), scopes (comma-separated engagement scope values matching getEstimatorConfigOptions.engagementScopeOptions.value), modules (comma-separated module values matching getEstimatorConfigOptions.moduleOptions.value), teamSize (numeric), timelineWeeks (numeric), budgetMin (numeric), budgetMax (numeric), currency (string matching one of the allowed ProjectEstimate.currency values), and autoCalculate (boolean-like string such as 'true' or '1'). On page initialization, the app calls getEstimatorConfigOptions first. Once the options are available, it parses the query parameters and initializes form state from them where they match valid option values. The module with value 'mlops_setup' is never auto-selected; even if the modules query parameter includes 'mlops_setup', the app only selects it if it is explicitly present and valid, but it is not set as a default when no URL value is present. If autoCalculate is present and truthy and all required fields are populated and valid, the app automatically invokes createProjectEstimate using values from the initialized state; otherwise, the user must click the primary action button to trigger the estimate. The ProjectEstimate returned by createProjectEstimate (including estimate.id) is stored in memory only for the current page session; it is not encoded back into the URL and is not persisted in localStorage. When navigating to proposal_request.html, the app passes estimateId and sourceType=from_estimator as URL parameters in the target URL, as specified by the outgoing navigation configuration.

components.functionality: On page load, fetch estimator configuration options via getEstimatorConfigOptions and provide industries, engagementScopeOptions, moduleOptions, and budgetPresets to child components. Handle loading and error states and prevent form usage until configuration data is available.
event_handlers: onLoad: invoke getEstimatorConfigOptions and store the returned configuration in component state.
onRetryClick: re-invoke getEstimatorConfigOptions if an error occurred.
onConfigLoaded: notify dependent components (e.g., estimator-form, url-state-sync) that configuration options are ready so that URL parameters can be applied to form controls.

components.functionality: Parse and apply URL query parameters to initialize the estimator form state once config options are loaded, and keep the URL updated when the user changes configuration fields. Optionally triggers auto-calculation when autoCalculate is set and validation conditions are met.
data_binding: getEstimatorConfigOptions (to validate and map parameter values to available options)
event_handlers: onConfigOptionsReady: read window.location.search, parse supported query parameters (industry, scopes, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency, autoCalculate), and dispatch initial values to estimator-form controls where they match valid options.

onFormFieldChange: when estimator-form reports changes (industry, engagementScope, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency), update the URL query parameters via history.replaceState without reloading.
onAutoCalculateEligible: if autoCalculate is truthy and required fields are valid after initialization, emit an event to trigger estimator-form's onSubmit handler to call createProjectEstimate.

components.functionality: Provide a dropdown to select the project industry, populated from getEstimatorConfigOptions.industries. This value is required for createProjectEstimate.
event_handlers: onChange: update the estimator-form state with the selected industry value and notify url-state-sync to refresh the industry query parameter.
onBlur: optionally trigger validation to ensure a selection is made before estimation.

components.functionality: Allow users to select one or more engagement scope values for the project (e.g., strategy, implementation, training, governance) based on options from getEstimatorConfigOptions.engagementScopeOptions. This array is required when calling createProjectEstimate.
event_handlers: onChange: toggle selected engagementScope values, update estimator-form state, and notify url-state-sync to update the scopes query parameter (comma-separated).
onValidate: ensure that at least one scope is selected before permitting estimation.

components.functionality: Display available modules returned by getEstimatorConfigOptions.moduleOptions and let the user select or deselect them. The module whose value is 'mlops_setup' is never preselected by default; it is only selected if the user explicitly chooses it or if it is explicitly provided via URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: render all module options from getEstimatorConfigOptions.moduleOptions with no preselected values, explicitly ensuring that the option with value 'mlops_setup' has its selected state set to false when no URL configuration is present.
onChange: update the modules array in estimator-form state when the user selects/deselects options and notify url-state-sync to update the modules query parameter as a comma-separated list of values.

onApplyUrlParams: when url-state-sync provides an initial modules list from the URL, select only the matching options (including 'mlops_setup' if present), without introducing any default module selections beyond what the user (or URL) specified.

components.functionality: Capture numeric inputs for teamSize and timelineWeeks required by createProjectEstimate, along with basic validation (e.g., positive integers).
event_handlers: onTeamSizeChange: update teamSize in estimator-form state, validate numeric constraints, and notify url-state-sync to update the teamSize query parameter.
onTimelineChange: update timelineWeeks in estimator-form state, validate numeric constraints, and notify url-state-sync to update the timelineWeeks query parameter.
onBlur: perform per-field validation and expose errors to the estimator-form for aggregated validation.

components.functionality: Provide both manual budget range inputs (budgetMin, budgetMax, currency) and quick-fill via budget presets from getEstimatorConfigOptions.budgetPresets. These values are optional for createProjectEstimate but influence the resulting estimate if provided.
event_handlers: onPresetSelect: when a preset is selected, set budgetMin, budgetMax, and currency in estimator-form state based on the selected preset object, and notify url-state-sync to update budgetMin, budgetMax, and currency query parameters.
onBudgetMinChange: update budgetMin in state and notify url-state-sync.
onBudgetMaxChange: update budgetMax in state and notify url-state-sync.
onCurrencyChange: update currency in state and notify url-state-sync.
onValidate: ensure that if both budgetMin and budgetMax are provided, budgetMax is greater than or equal to budgetMin.

components.functionality: Aggregate all input sections (industry-selector, engagement-scope-selector, module-selector, team-timeline-inputs, budget-inputs) into a single configuration object and handle validation and submission to createProjectEstimate.
data_binding: ProjectEstimate (all input-relevant fields: industry, engagementScope, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency)
event_handlers: onSubmit: when the user clicks the primary 'Calculate Estimate' or 'Generate Plan' button, validate required fields, then call createProjectEstimate with the current state values (industry, engagementScope, modules, teamSize, timelineWeeks, budgetMin, budgetMax, currency).
onSubmitSuccess: receive the response from createProjectEstimate, store estimate in internal state, clear any previous error, and notify estimate-result-panel and proposal-cta of the new estimate.
onSubmitError: capture error message from createProjectEstimate and display it in an error area without leaving the page.
onFieldChange: central handler for all field changes, coordinating validation and emitting events to url-state-sync.

components.functionality: Provide primary and secondary action buttons for running the estimate (e.g., 'Calculate Estimate', 'Generate Plan') and potentially resetting the form. Manage disabled/loading states during createProjectEstimate calls.
event_handlers: onCalculateClick: trigger estimator-form.onSubmit to invoke createProjectEstimate.
onResetClick: optionally clear form fields and URL query parameters (excluding any non-estimator parameters) and reset internal state, including clearing the current estimate result.

components.functionality: Display the results of the latest created estimate, including estimatedCostMin, estimatedCostMax, currency, summary, and a recap of key configuration inputs. This panel is only visible or populated after a successful createProjectEstimate call.
event_handlers: onEstimateUpdated: when estimator-form reports a new estimate from createProjectEstimate, refresh all displayed fields in the panel.
onClearEstimate: when estimator-form is reset, hide or clear the result panel so it does not show stale information.

components.functionality: Offer a CTA such as 'Request Detailed Proposal' or 'Talk to Sales' that sends the user to proposal_request.html with the current estimateId and sourceType=from_estimator in the URL, only after an estimate has been successfully created.
event_handlers: onEstimateAvailable: enable and show the CTA when a valid ProjectEstimate with an id has been received from createProjectEstimate.
onClick: navigate the browser to proposal_request.html?estimateId=<estimate.id>&sourceType=from_estimator using the current estimate.id from state.
onEstimateCleared: disable or hide the CTA if the estimate is cleared or if form reset removes the current estimate from state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page reuses the two-column hero pattern from the mockup. The main estimator experience becomes the "hero": a left column with a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack of intro text, configuration controls, and an action bar; and a right column with a vertically centered, visually strong estimate summary. The left column acts like the original headline/copy/CTA stack, while the right column replaces the portrait with a clean summary panel that updates as users configure their project. Invisible functional components (loader, URL sync) sit logically near the top of the content structure but have no visual presence.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page reuses the two-column hero pattern from the mockup. The main estimator experience becomes the "hero": a left column with a linear, top‑to‑bottom stack of intro text, configuration controls, and an action bar; and a right column with a vertically centered, visually strong estimate summary. The left column acts like the original headline/copy/CTA stack, while the right column replaces the portrait with a clean summary panel that updates as users configure their project. Invisible functional components (loader, URL sync) sit logically near the top of the content structure but have no visual presence.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within a centered max‑width container (~1120–1200px), the content splits into a 12‑column grid: roughly 7–8 columns (about 60–65% width) for the left configuration form and 4–5 columns (about 35–40%) for the right estimate result panel. The vertical space from just under the global header down to the fold is dominated by this two‑column estimator layout, designed to feel like a full‑height hero. Above and below sections use generous vertical spacing (64–96px) to maintain the editorial, low‑density feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls are grouped into clear sections with generous breathing room: each major form section (industry, scope, modules, team/timeline, budget) is separated by 32–48px of vertical space. Individual inputs typically span the full width of the left column or, when paired, split into two balanced halves with 16–24px gaps. The right summary panel has a card‑like feel with ample internal padding (24–32px all around), large numbers, and short, scannable labels. Overall density stays low and clean, with no cramped clusters of controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mirrors the mockup’s Z‑pattern. After the site’s global header, the eye lands first on the page title and a short explanatory line at the top‑left of the estimator area. From there, it moves down through the stacked configuration controls in the left column, with the horizontal estimate action bar at the bottom acting as a clear next step. In parallel, a strong, card‑like estimate result panel on the right draws the eye across horizontally, providing constant feedback and reinforcing the value of configuring the form. The proposal CTA is anchored within or directly below the summary panel, becoming the final stop in the flow once an estimate is generated.

layout.overall_description: Below the global dark-mode header, the Project Estimator fills the visible viewport as a modern, two-column hero. The background remains a rich charcoal, while the main content sits in a centered, max-width container. On the left, occupying about two-thirds of the width, a clean vertical stack begins with a small all-caps label and a concise page title, followed by a one- or two-line description. Beneath this, large, well-spaced form sections allow the user to pick industry, define engagement scope, choose solution modules, and specify team, timeline, and budget. Each section is separated by generous vertical gaps, with each control spanning most of the column width, creating an uncluttered, editorial feel. At the bottom of this left column, a horizontally oriented action bar stretches edge to edge within the column, visually grounding the configuration area and presenting the primary action to generate or update the estimate. To the right, occupying the remaining third of the width, a tall, card-like estimate result panel sits aligned with the top of the form’s introduction text, using a slightly lighter dark tone and subtle elevation to stand out. It remains in view as the user scrolls the form, showing the current estimated investment and key breakdowns, with a prominent proposal CTA positioned within the card towards its lower portion. The overall effect is tech-forward and consulting-oriented: a clear left-to-right narrative from configuration to insight, set within a spacious, high-contrast dark interface that feels like a high-end strategy tool rather than a simple form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and sits logically at the top of the main estimator content region, just below the global header and above the visible form content. Conceptually, it occupies the full width of the centered max-width container but renders no UI. It initializes data and configuration state before any of the visible estimator components appear, ensuring that when the user first sees the page, the left configuration column and right estimate panel can load in a stable, flicker-free state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, static in the document flow near the top of the estimator section; does not affect layout spacing or alignment of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is also non-visual and is positioned logically alongside or just beneath the config loader within the main estimator section. It spans the full logical width of the content container but has no visible footprint. It keeps the current estimator configuration in sync with the URL, functioning entirely in the background so that the visible layout of the left form column and right summary panel remains undisturbed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, static; integrated near the top of the DOM for the estimator but consumes no visual space and does not alter spacing or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the left column, the industry selector is the first visible form control, placed directly beneath the page introduction (eyebrow label, title, and short description). It sits flush with the left column edge and spans roughly 90–100% of the left column width, leaving consistent inner padding to the container’s sides. Vertically, there is a comfortable gap (about 24–32px) between the description and the top of the selector, and another similar gap below before the next form section begins. The control is presented as a modern select input with a clear label above it, both aligned left, maintaining the tidy, top-down reading order established by the mockup’s hero text stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form element within the vertical flow of the left column, resizing responsively so it remains full-width on mobile when the layout collapses to a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The engagement scope selector appears directly beneath the industry selector in the left column, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 32px to clearly signal a new section. It spans the same effective width as the industry selector, aligning its left edge and label so that all form sections form a clean vertical axis. As a multi-select, it may present a row or grid of pill-style options; these pills flow horizontally within the left column, wrapping onto multiple lines while maintaining at least 16px of spacing between rows. The section title (e.g., "Engagement scope") sits above the option area with 8–12px of spacing, preserving the rhythm of label-then-control seen throughout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s scrollable content, adapting to the column width; on narrower viewports, option pills stack vertically while keeping consistent padding and gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The module selector follows the engagement scope selector in the same left column stack, offset by a similar 32–40px vertical gap to avoid visual crowding. It shares the same horizontal alignment and width as the preceding selectors, creating a consistent left margin and right boundary within the column. As another multi-select, its content area may be slightly taller, so internal spacing is generous: 16–24px between option rows, with the whole section framed by at least 24px of top and bottom padding relative to neighboring sections. This keeps the overall density low while still allowing a rich set of choices.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding vertically as needed while staying constrained to the left column width; scrolls naturally with the rest of the form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-timeline inputs form a clearly defined form section positioned below the module selector, again with a 32–48px vertical gap to mark the transition. Within the left column, this section often uses a two-column internal layout: for example, team size on the left and timeline/duration on the right, each occupying roughly 48–50% of the column width with a 16–24px gap between them. Labels sit above each field, aligned to the top, so the pair reads as a balanced row within the larger vertical stack. On smaller screens, these inputs stack vertically with 16–24px between them, preserving the sense of air and clarity. The section overall spans the full width of the left column but feels visually compartmentalized via clear grouping and consistent spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the left column; its internal fields may reflow between one and two columns depending on viewport width, but the section’s top and bottom edges remain aligned to the 8px grid and to neighboring sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget inputs section sits near the bottom of the left column’s configuration stack, directly below the team-timeline section with 32–48px of vertical spacing. It is treated as a high-importance but still cleanly presented block. The section header and brief helper text (if present) align with the left edge of the column, and the main budget input—whether a range slider, numeric fields, or toggles—spans nearly the full width of the column. If both minimum and maximum budget inputs are needed, they may sit side by side, each taking roughly half the width with consistent 16–24px spacing. This section visually anchors the end of the configuration form, preparing the user for the call-to-action in the action bar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the left column; on smaller screens it collapses to a single-column stack of inputs, maintaining generous vertical spacing between fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimator form acts as the primary structural container for all left-column inputs: industry, engagement scope, modules, team-timeline, budget, and any introductory text above them. It occupies roughly 60–65% of the total content width, aligned to the left within the centered max-width container. Vertically, it starts just below the global header’s bottom edge with a top padding of about 64px, and extends downward to encompass all form sections plus the action bar at its base. The form’s internal layout is a clear top-down flow: intro label and title, short supporting paragraph, then each form section separated by consistent vertical gaps. The right estimate result panel visually begins at approximately the same top baseline as the form’s intro content, so the pair reads as a cohesive two-column hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static primary content column within a responsive 12-column grid. On tablet and mobile, the form reflows to occupy 100% width, with the estimate result panel moving below it; vertical stacking and spacing are preserved to maintain clarity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate action bar is positioned at the bottom of the estimator form’s left column, directly beneath the budget inputs with a moderate 24–32px vertical gap. It extends the full width of the left column, forming a horizontal strip that visually grounds the configuration stack. The bar contains the primary action button (e.g., "Generate estimate" or "Update estimate") aligned to the left, possibly accompanied by secondary textual actions (like reset or help) aligned to the right or beneath, but always with ample breathing room. Vertical padding inside the bar is generous (12–16px), making it feel substantial yet not heavy. The bar adopts a slightly higher contrast than the form background, subtly echoing the mockup’s CTA row in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the left column on larger viewports: when the user scrolls down through longer form content, the action bar remains pinned near the bottom edge of the viewport within the left column, ensuring the primary action is always accessible. On smaller screens, it may become a full-width sticky bar at the bottom of the viewport, above the OS UI chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate result panel forms the entire visible right column and is the primary counterweight to the left form, analogous to the portrait in the original mockup. It is aligned to the top of the estimator form’s intro section, beginning about 64px below the global header, and occupies roughly 35–40% of the main content width. The panel appears as a tall, card-like block with a slightly lighter charcoal background and subtle elevation or border to differentiate it from the page background. Inside, there is substantial padding (24–32px on all sides). At the top, a concise label and summary title (e.g., "Estimated Investment") are followed by a large, prominent numeric value, which takes visual precedence. Secondary details—such as a breakdown by phases, team, or time—are stacked below in minimalist rows with 8–16px spacing. The panel’s content is vertically centered or biased toward the upper half, leaving some breathing space at the bottom for the proposal CTA. The entire card maintains a consistent right margin from the viewport edge, matching the left column’s left margin for a balanced frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within its right-column region on desktop and large tablets: as the user scrolls the left form content, the estimate panel remains visible, pinned near the top of the viewport, only scrolling once the user reaches the bottom of the form. On smaller viewports, it reflows below the form and becomes a standard static block with the same padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proposal CTA is positioned within the lower portion of the estimate result panel, aligned to the left edge of the panel’s internal content area. It sits below the primary estimate number and any key breakdown rows, separated by a comfortable gap of about 24–32px to signal a distinct next step. The button spans a moderate width (roughly 40–60% of the panel’s internal width) rather than edge-to-edge, maintaining the minimalist aesthetic. There is sufficient padding above and below so the button doesn’t feel cramped against the card edges. On smaller screens where the estimate panel moves below the form, the proposal CTA remains in this relative position at the bottom of the summary card, still clearly tied to the estimate context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the estimate result panel’s content flow; its position is anchored near the bottom of the panel’s content stack but moves naturally as the panel reflows on smaller viewports.

name: Blog / Insights
title: Blog / Insights
description: Page to search, filter, sort, and browse blog posts, with the ability to save short posts to a Reading Queue.

core_features: Load and display blog posts based on search, filter, and sort criteria using searchBlogPosts.
Provide a keyword search input for querying blog posts by text fields, mapped to the searchBlogPosts `query` parameter.
Provide date range and topic filters populated dynamically from getBlogFilterOptions and mapped to searchBlogPosts `dateRangeKey` and `topicCategory` parameters.
Provide sort controls populated from getBlogFilterOptions and mapped to the searchBlogPosts `sortBy` parameter.
Optionally filter to short articles (e.g., under a defined number of minutes) via a control mapped to searchBlogPosts `maxReadTimeMinutes`.
Display each blog post with title, summary, publish date, topic category, tags, and estimated read time using BlogPost fields.
Enable navigation from any blog post to content_detail.html with URL parameters `contentType=blog_post`, `contentId`, and `sourcePage=blog`.
Allow users to add eligible blog posts (with estimated read time within a configured threshold) to the Reading Queue via saveBlogPostToReadingQueue.
Show per-post saved state using the `isSavedToReadingQueue` flag from searchBlogPosts results, disabling or changing the save action when already saved.
Provide a link or icon to navigate to saved_items.html with `listType=reading_queue` to view the Reading Queue.
Optionally display a list of recent blog posts using getRecentBlogPosts for additional discovery, independent of the main search results.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing with preset filters from another page
1. User arrives on blog.html with some URL parameters set (e.g., `query`, `dateRangeKey`, `topicCategory`).
2. The blog-page controller parses the URL parameters and initializes internal state for query, date range, topic category, sort option, and optional max read time.
3. The page calls getBlogFilterOptions to retrieve available date range options, topic categories, and sort options.
4. The search/filter component applies the URL-provided values to select the corresponding options in the UI (when they match options returned by getBlogFilterOptions). If a parameter is missing or does not match any option, a default is chosen from the values returned by getBlogFilterOptions (e.g., the first option in each list).
5. The page calls searchBlogPosts with the current state (query, dateRangeKey, topicCategory, sortBy, and optionally maxReadTimeMinutes) to retrieve matching blog posts.
6. The results list renders the returned posts along with the `isSavedToReadingQueue` status for each.
7. User scrolls through the list, and can refine filters or sorting; each change updates the URL and triggers a new call to searchBlogPosts.

Workflow 2: Searching for posts by keyword
1. User focuses the search input and types a keyword or phrase.
2. On submitting the search form (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button), the search component:
   - Updates the `query` parameter in the URL while preserving other active parameters (dateRangeKey, topicCategory, sortBy, maxReadTimeMinutes).
   - Triggers searchBlogPosts with the updated `query` and current filter/sort state.
3. The results list updates to show posts returned in the `results` array.
4. The totalCount from searchBlogPosts is used to show how many posts match the current query (if that feedback is implemented).
5. User can further refine by changing date range, topic, or sort; each refinement updates the URL and re-calls searchBlogPosts.

Workflow 3: Applying filters and sorting
1. User opens the date range dropdown, populated from getBlogFilterOptions.dateRangeOptions, and selects one of the available options.
2. The filter component updates the internal state and sets the `dateRangeKey` parameter in the URL to the selected option key.
3. The page calls searchBlogPosts with the new dateRangeKey and current query/topic/sort settings.
4. Similarly, when the user selects a topic category from options returned by getBlogFilterOptions.topicCategories, the filter component updates the `topicCategory` URL parameter and re-calls searchBlogPosts.
5. When the user changes sort order via the sort options returned by getBlogFilterOptions.sortOptions, the component updates the `sortBy` URL parameter and triggers searchBlogPosts with the new sort value.
6. Optionally, when the user enables a control for shorter posts, the component sets a numeric value (e.g., a threshold in minutes) on the `maxReadTimeMinutes` URL parameter and re-calls searchBlogPosts, which returns only posts with estimatedReadTimeMinutes less than or equal to that value.

Workflow 4: Saving posts to the Reading Queue
1. In the results list, each blog post row/card shows any available estimatedReadTimeMinutes.
2. For posts meeting the configured maximum read time threshold (e.g., at or under a specified number of minutes), an action control such as “Add to Reading Queue” is enabled; for other posts, this control may be hidden or disabled.
3. When the user clicks the save action on an eligible post that is not yet saved (where `isSavedToReadingQueue` is false), the result-item component calls saveBlogPostToReadingQueue with the post’s id and optional notes (if the UI collects notes; otherwise notes can be omitted).
4. On success (success=true), the component updates its local representation of that item’s state to indicate it is now saved (equivalent to `isSavedToReadingQueue = true`).
5. Optionally, the component provides visual feedback (for example, changing the button label to indicate it is in the queue and disabling further saves).
6. If the user reloads or changes filters, a subsequent call to searchBlogPosts returns updated `isSavedToReadingQueue` values, keeping the saved state in sync.

Workflow 5: Navigating to detailed content and the Reading Queue
1. When the user clicks on a blog post title or a “Read more” link in the results list, the component navigates to content_detail.html, passing URL parameters: `contentType=blog_post`, `contentId` equal to the BlogPost.id, and `sourcePage=blog`.
2. When the user clicks the Reading Queue link or icon on this page, navigation goes to saved_items.html with URL parameter `listType=reading_queue`.
3. The browser history keeps the full URL for the Blog / Insights page (including all search/filter parameters), so using the Back button returns the user to the same filtered view.

Workflow 6: Discovering recent posts
1. On initial page load, the controller optionally calls getRecentBlogPosts with a chosen `maxItems` value to obtain a subset of recently published blog posts.
2. The recent-posts component renders this list, for example in a sidebar or below the main results, independent of the current search.
3. The user can click on any recent post item, which navigates to content_detail.html with the same parameters as other post links (`contentType=blog_post`, `contentId`, `sourcePage=blog`).
4. This workflow co-exists with the main search and filtering; recent posts do not affect the current search state unless explicitly designed to do so (e.g., clicking a tag could update filters).
interactions: Typing and editing in the search input: updates local query state on each keystroke; the actual search is executed on form submission, which updates the URL `query` parameter and calls searchBlogPosts.

Changing the date range dropdown: updates the selected date range key, writes it to the `dateRangeKey` URL parameter, clears any conflicting date parameters if used, and calls searchBlogPosts with the new dateRangeKey.
Changing the topic category dropdown: updates the selected topic value, writes it to the `topicCategory` URL parameter, and calls searchBlogPosts.
Changing the sort dropdown: selects one of the sort options returned by getBlogFilterOptions.sortOptions, writes its value to the `sortBy` URL parameter, and calls searchBlogPosts.

Toggling a quick-read filter control (if implemented): when enabled, sets a numeric limit in the `maxReadTimeMinutes` URL parameter (for example, to the threshold that defines short posts); when disabled, removes this parameter. Each change re-calls searchBlogPosts.
Clicking the search form submit button (or pressing Enter): prevents default full-page form submission, synchronizes the URL `query` parameter with the current input, and triggers searchBlogPosts.

Clicking a blog post title or dedicated “Read more” link: triggers client-side navigation to content_detail.html with URL parameters `contentType=blog_post`, `contentId` from the clicked BlogPost, and `sourcePage=blog`.

Clicking the save-to-queue control on a blog result: if the post is eligible and not yet saved, calls saveBlogPostToReadingQueue with that BlogPost.id and optional notes, then updates the UI state according to the response (success or error).

Attempting to save a post already in the Reading Queue: if `isSavedToReadingQueue` is true, the save action is disabled or changes to a non-interactive state (e.g., showing that it is already saved); no additional API call is made.

Clicking the Reading Queue link/icon: navigates to saved_items.html with `listType=reading_queue`, without altering current search state on the Blog / Insights page (state is preserved in the URL should the user navigate back).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Blog / Insights page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for search and filter state. On load, the page controller reads the following parameters (if present): `query`, `dateRangeKey`, `topicCategory`, and any page-specific parameters such as `sortBy` and `maxReadTimeMinutes`. These parameters initialize the UI controls and are passed directly into searchBlogPosts, aligning with its signature (query, dateRangeKey, fromDate, toDate, topicCategory, sortBy, maxReadTimeMinutes). The `fromDate` and `toDate` parameters of searchBlogPosts can remain unset if the UI only uses predefined ranges via `dateRangeKey`; if the UI is extended to support custom ranges, corresponding URL parameters (e.g., `fromDate`, `toDate`) should be introduced, and selecting a custom range would clear `dateRangeKey` in the URL to avoid conflicts. When the user interacts with any search, filter, or sort control, the page updates the appropriate URL parameters using client-side navigation APIs (such as pushState or replaceState) while preserving other parameters, then re-invokes searchBlogPosts with the updated values. Default values are derived from the data returned by getBlogFilterOptions: when a parameter (e.g., `dateRangeKey` or `sortBy`) is missing or invalid, the page selects a default option from the corresponding options arrays and reflects that choice both in UI state and in the URL (by writing the selected option value). The `topicCategory` URL parameter maps to the BlogPost.topicCategory enum values. The `maxReadTimeMinutes` parameter is only present when the quick-read filter is enabled and is omitted otherwise. The Reading Queue state for each post is not persisted in the URL; instead, it is derived from the `isSavedToReadingQueue` flag returned by searchBlogPosts for each result and from the immediate response of saveBlogPostToReadingQueue for user actions. Navigation to other pages carries its own state via URL parameters: clicking a post navigates to content_detail.html using `contentType=blog_post`, `contentId` set to the selected BlogPost.id, and `sourcePage=blog`; clicking the Reading Queue navigates to saved_items.html with `listType=reading_queue`. Because the Blog / Insights view configuration is fully encoded in URL parameters, users can bookmark or share filtered views and can return to the same state using browser navigation.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and state distribution to child components. On page load, it reads URL parameters for query, dateRangeKey, topicCategory, sortBy, and maxReadTimeMinutes, then calls getBlogFilterOptions to obtain available filter and sort options. It computes defaults when URL values are missing or invalid, updates the URL accordingly, and invokes searchBlogPosts with the assembled parameters. It optionally calls getRecentBlogPosts with a configured maxItems to populate the recent-posts panel. It listens for events from search/filter and results components (e.g., search submitted, filters changed, save succeeded) and updates internal state and, when appropriate, the URL.
event_handlers: onLoad
onUrlChange
onSearchCriteriaChange
onSearchResultsLoaded
onSaveToQueueSuccess

components.functionality: Provides a combined control area for keyword search, date range selection, topic filtering, sorting, and optional quick-read filtering. It renders a text input bound to the `query` state; dropdowns for date range and topic populated from getBlogFilterOptions.dateRangeOptions and getBlogFilterOptions.topicCategories; a sort dropdown populated from getBlogFilterOptions.sortOptions; and an optional toggle or checkbox to limit results to posts below a specified read-time threshold, mapping to searchBlogPosts.maxReadTimeMinutes. On any change or submission, it emits an event containing the updated criteria, which the page controller uses to update URL parameters and call searchBlogPosts.
event_handlers: onSubmitSearch
onChangeQuery
onChangeDateRange
onChangeTopicCategory
onChangeSortBy
onToggleMaxReadTimeFilter

components.functionality: Displays the array of blog posts returned from searchBlogPosts.results. It receives each result object, which includes a BlogPost and an isSavedToReadingQueue boolean. It iterates through the list and delegates rendering of each post to the blog-result-item component. It can also display the total result count using searchBlogPosts.totalCount, and conditionally render a no-results state when the array is empty. It listens for events from child result items (e.g., post selected, save requested) and forwards them to the page controller.
event_handlers: onResultsDataChange
onClickPost
onClickSaveToQueue

components.functionality: Represents a single blog post row or card. It renders BlogPost fields (title, summary, publishDate, topicCategory, tags, estimatedReadTimeMinutes) and uses the isSavedToReadingQueue flag to determine the state of the save-to-queue action. If estimatedReadTimeMinutes is defined and at or below the configured maximum threshold for quick reads, the component shows an enabled control for adding the post to the Reading Queue when isSavedToReadingQueue is false. If isSavedToReadingQueue is true, the control is either disabled or visually marked as already saved. Clicking the title or a dedicated read link emits a navigation event. Clicking the save action triggers saveBlogPostToReadingQueue for the associated BlogPost.id and, on success, updates the local item state to reflect that the post is saved.
event_handlers: onClickTitle
onClickReadMore
onClickSaveToQueue
onSaveToQueueSuccess
onSaveToQueueError

components.functionality: Provides direct navigation from the Blog / Insights page to the Reading Queue view. It renders as an icon or text link labeled for the Reading Queue or Saved Articles. When activated, it navigates to saved_items.html with the URL parameter `listType=reading_queue`. It does not modify the current search/filter state in the Blog / Insights URL.
event_handlers: onClick

components.functionality: Uses getRecentBlogPosts to retrieve a limited set of the most recently published blog posts and displays them as a compact list for quick access. Each item shows key BlogPost fields such as title and publishDate and may also indicate estimatedReadTimeMinutes. Clicking an item navigates to content_detail.html with `contentType=blog_post`, `contentId` set to the selected post’s id, and `sourcePage=blog`. This component is read-only and does not provide filtering or save-to-queue actions (those belong to the main results list).
event_handlers: onRecentPostsLoaded
onClickRecentPost

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page adopts the hero-like two-column pattern from the mockup. The main blog controller (page title, context label, short intro) forms a text-heavy block at the top-left. Directly beneath it, a single horizontal search-and-filter bar spans the full width of the left column, with search on the left, filters in the middle, and the Reading Queue link aligned to the far right, echoing the header's utility/social pattern. Below that, blog results are stacked vertically as a clean list of cards. On the right, mirroring the hero portrait, a tall Recent Posts panel occupies a narrower column, starting roughly aligned with the search bar and extending down alongside the results list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page adopts the hero-like two-column pattern from the mockup. The main blog controller (page title, context label, short intro) forms a text-heavy block at the top-left. Directly beneath it, a single horizontal search-and-filter bar spans the full width of the left column, with search on the left, filters in the middle, and the Reading Queue link aligned to the far right, echoing the header's utility/social pattern. Below that, blog results are stacked vertically as a clean list of cards. On the right, mirroring the hero portrait, a tall Recent Posts panel occupies a narrower column, starting roughly aligned with the search bar and extending down alongside the results list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Within a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), the left content column takes about 60–65% of the width and the right column about 35–40%. Vertically, the top 'controller' region (page controller + search/filters + reading queue link + the top of the recent posts panel) visually acts like a hero band occupying roughly the top third of the viewport. The remaining vertical space is dedicated to the scrollable list of blog results on the left and the continuing right-hand recent posts panel. Generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separates the page controller from the search bar, and the search bar from the results list.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is arranged with low density, large type and clear grouping. The blog-page-controller has ample whitespace above and below, with clear separation between the context label, H1, and supporting copy (16–24px gaps). The search-and-filter bar is a single clean row with 16–24px padding inside and 24–32px gaps between search input, filters, and the reading queue link. The blog results list uses card-like items with 24–32px internal padding and 24–32px gaps between items. The recent posts panel is visually simple and compact in typography but still padded generously and not overloaded with entries.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the large left-aligned blog page title and its small all-caps context label above it, echoing the hero pattern. From there, it moves downward to the supporting description, then flows into the search-and-filter bar directly below. Within that bar, the gaze travels horizontally from the search input on the left, across filter controls, to the Reading Queue link at the far right, mimicking the header's left-to-right utility pattern. After that, the eye drops down into the vertical stream of blog result cards. In parallel, the Recent Posts panel on the right acts as a strong vertical counterweight—similar to the portrait in the hero—drawing the eye across from the left-column content and offering a secondary entry point into content discovery.

layout.overall_description: The Blog / Insights page sits within a centered, dark-theme container that inherits the site's modern, tech-forward aesthetic. Under the global navigation, the top of the page opens with a spacious, text-led header block on the left: a small all-caps label like “INSIGHTS” sits above a bold, multi-line page title and a concise descriptive paragraph, all arranged in a clear vertical stack with generous breathing room. This left block occupies roughly two-thirds of the width. To its right, instead of a portrait, a tall Recent Posts panel anchors the column, creating a solid vertical presence that mirrors the hero's right-side emphasis. Just below the page title, spanning the width of the left column, a single horizontal search-and-filter band provides search input on the left, filter controls in the center, and a Reading Queue link aligned to the far right, echoing the header's utility and social alignment. Below this band, blog posts appear as a column of clean, card-like items, each with ample padding and clear typographic hierarchy, scrolling vertically while the right-hand Recent Posts panel remains visually aligned and optionally sticky. The overall layout preserves the mockup’s calm, editorial feel: large type, dark charcoal background, high-contrast text, generous margins, and clear left-to-right then top-to-bottom reading paths that guide visitors from discovery (page title) to exploration (search, filters, recent posts) and into deeper reading (results list and reading queue).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-page-controller sits directly beneath the global site header, aligned to the left within the main container. It spans roughly 60–65% of the total content width (left column) and is centered vertically in the top third of the viewport with 64px padding above and about 32–48px below before the search bar. Inside this block, content is stacked vertically in a linear sequence: at the top, a small all-caps context label (e.g., “INSIGHTS” or “BLOG”) is left-aligned and spaced about 8–12px above the main headline. Below it, a large multi-line H1 headline occupies most of the block's vertical height, with line length roughly 8–10 words per line and 16–24px of space below. A supporting paragraph follows, constrained to the same column width, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between it and the headline, and around 24–32px below before reaching the search-and-filter-bar. The entire blog-page-controller area uses generous left/right padding (24–32px inside the main container) and aligns to the same vertical baseline as the top of the Recent Posts panel on the right when viewed together at desktop widths.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal flow; its height adapts to the length of title and description, but it always stays at the top of the main scrollable content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-and-filter-bar is positioned immediately beneath the blog-page-controller, still within the left main column.
