It spans the full width of that left column (approximately 60–65% of the container), with 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the content above and 32–40px of space below before the first blog result item. Visually, it appears as a single horizontal band with a slightly elevated feel: a subtle contrasting dark surface against the hero background, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding. Inside this band, elements are arranged in a left-to-right row: the search input occupies the left half (roughly 40–50% of the bar width), the filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, chips, sort toggles) sit centered in the middle (20–30% of the width), and the Reading Queue link is aligned to the far right edge of the bar, visually similar in prominence to a secondary CTA. Spacing between these groups is generous (16–24px gaps), ensuring low density and clear separation. On smaller screens, this row can collapse into a vertical stack with the search input on top, filters below, and the Reading Queue link last, all still padded and aligned to the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow, but visually treated as a control bar. On larger screens it may use a subtle sticky-on-scroll behavior for usability (sticking to the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past the page controller), while still spanning only the left column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-results-list occupies the remainder of the left column directly below the search-and-filter-bar. It is a vertical stack that stretches from just under the bar down to the lower edge of the viewport and beyond, becoming the main scrollable body of the page. It retains the same horizontal alignment as the page controller and search bar, with consistent left/right padding (24–32px from the container edges). Each blog-result-item within the list is separated by 24–32px of vertical space, giving a relaxed, editorial feel. The list visually aligns with the top and lower edges of the Recent Posts panel on the right, creating a balanced two-column layout: as the user scrolls, the list flows downwards while the right-hand column continues alongside it, acting as a secondary vertical reference.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The list is fully scrollable content within the main page flow. It does not fix or float; it grows vertically as more results load, and on smaller screens it becomes a full-width single column beneath the search-and-filter-bar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each blog-result-item is rendered as a clean, card-like block within the blog-results-list, spanning nearly the full width of the left column (minus standard inner padding). The card uses a dark surface slightly elevated from the background, with 24–32px padding on all sides to respect the 8px grid. Inside the card, content is arranged in a clear vertical sequence: a small all-caps category or tag at the top, the post title in a bold, larger typeface directly beneath it, followed by a short summary paragraph and a compact metadata row (author, date, read time) near the bottom. Vertical spacing between these elements follows the base scale (8–16px between label and title, 8–12px between title and summary, 12–16px between summary and metadata). On some items, a small thumbnail or accent graphic can sit to the right of the text, but never more than ~30% of the card width, preserving the text-first feel. Each card maintains at least 24px of vertical separation from the next, and the cards collectively echo the headline/paragraph/buttons grouping of the hero by keeping clusters of text distinct and airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical list. Cards expand vertically based on content length but maintain consistent padding and spacing. On hover/interaction, subtle elevation or border glow reinforces clickability without changing layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-queue-link is visually integrated into the right side of the search-and-filter-bar, acting as a navigation-style pill that mirrors the placement of social icons and utility links in the global header. Horizontally, it is anchored to the far right edge of the search bar content area, with 16–24px of internal horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, giving it a compact but noticeable presence. It sits on the same baseline as the search input and filter controls, forming a single unified row. The link may include a small icon (e.g., bookmark or queue list) to its left and a short label like “Reading Queue”, with the label in medium-weight type. It does not dominate the search input visually but stands out as a clear interactive element, using a slightly higher-contrast outline or subtle filled style compared to text-only elements. On smaller screens, when the search-and-filter-bar collapses into a column, this link moves below the filters, left-aligned with a bit of extra top margin (16–24px) to keep it distinct as a navigation action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It moves with the search-and-filter-bar: if the bar becomes sticky at the top of the viewport, the reading-queue-link remains anchored to the bar’s right edge. Otherwise, it is static within the bar’s layout, not independently fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-posts-panel occupies the right column, acting as the visual counterpart to the hero portrait in the original mockup. It begins near the top of the viewport, horizontally aligned with the top of the blog-page-controller’s main content but slightly offset downward to feel subordinate to the main page title (e.g., 24px lower than the top of the title). The panel width is approximately 35–40% of the container, with 24–32px internal padding and 24–32px separation from the right edge of the container. Vertically, it runs alongside the search-and-filter-bar and continues down next to the blog-results-list. Inside, the content is arranged in a vertical stack: a small section label at the top (e.g., “Recent Posts” in all caps or small caps), a bit of vertical spacing (8–12px), then a list of 4–6 compact recent post links. Each recent post item contains a short title and optional metadata (e.g., date), with 12–16px vertical gaps between items, maintaining the low-density feel. The height of the panel is sufficient to provide a strong vertical presence (roughly 60–75% of the viewport height at desktop), mimicking the portrait’s visual weight without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the panel can use a sticky behavior: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains fixed within the right column, pinned a small distance (24–32px) from the top of the viewport, while the blog-results-list continues to scroll on the left. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, it drops below the main results list and becomes a full-width section, losing the sticky behavior in favor of a straightforward vertical stack.

name: Training Programs
title: Training Programs
description: Browse and filter AI training programs by delivery format, region, capacity, and budget, compare options by price, and open detailed program pages.

core_features: Initialize available filter options (delivery formats, regions, capacity bands, budget ranges, live session counts) by calling getTrainingProgramFilterOptions.
Read optional incoming URL parameters (deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxPrice) to preset filters when the page loads.

Allow users to adjust filters (delivery format, region, min/max participants, budget max, currency, minimum live sessions) and sorting, and execute searchTrainingPrograms with the current filter state.
Display a list of training programs returned by searchTrainingPrograms, including price, currency, deliveryFormat, region, capacity range, and liveSessionsCount where available.
Allow users to sort the list, including sorting by price to identify the most affordable program that meets the current criteria via the sortBy parameter of searchTrainingPrograms.
Highlight the lowest-priced training program within the current search results that matches the applied filters.
Allow users to select multiple programs from the result list for side-by-side comparison of key attributes using only the data returned by searchTrainingPrograms.
Navigate to training_detail.html with the selected training program ID (training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=...) when a user selects a program card or clicks a "View Details" interaction.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load page with pre-applied filters from another page
1. User clicks a link on index.html that includes some or all of: deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxPrice in the URL to training.html.
2. Training Programs page reads URL parameters and stores them in its internal filter state.
3. Page calls getTrainingProgramFilterOptions.
4. When filter options are loaded, the filter controls are initialized using a combination of URL parameter values (if present) and defaults from getTrainingProgramFilterOptions.
5. Page calls searchTrainingPrograms with the current filter state, including any URL-provided deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxPrice, and derived currency, maxParticipants, minLiveSessions, and sortBy.
6. Results are rendered in the training program list, and the cheapest program in the current result set is identified and highlighted.
7. User can click any program card or "View Details" to navigate to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id>.

Workflow: Refine filters to find training within a budget and capacity
1. User lands on training.html without preset filters or after initial load.
2. User opens the filter panel and selects a delivery format and region from dropdowns populated via getTrainingProgramFilterOptions.
3. User sets a minimum participant capacity using either a capacity band option (mapped to minParticipants/maxParticipants) or a numeric input; user optionally sets a maximum budget or selects a budget range (mapped to maxPrice and currency).
4. User optionally sets a minimum live session count using available liveSessionCounts.
5. User submits the filter form or changes a filter control.
6. The filter component updates the URL parameters to reflect the new filter state (e.g., deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, maxPrice, currency, minLiveSessions) and triggers a new call to searchTrainingPrograms with these parameters and the current sortBy.
7. The results list updates with filtered programs; the cheapest program under the specified maxPrice is re-identified and highlighted.

Workflow: Identify the most affordable suitable program and compare with others
1. After applying filters, user selects a sort order (e.g., sort by price ascending) via the sort control.
2. The sort control updates the URL parameter sortBy and triggers searchTrainingPrograms with the same filters but new sortBy.
3. Results list re-renders in the new order. Independently, the cheapest program in the current result set is computed from the returned prices and marked as the "lowest price" within the result set.
4. User selects one or more checkbox controls on different program cards to add them to a comparison selection list.
5. The comparison component reads selected programs from the current results set (by IDs carried in state and URL, such as compareIds) and displays their key attributes (price, currency, deliveryFormat, region, min/max participants, liveSessionsCount, durationDays where available) side-by-side.
6. User can remove programs from comparison (which updates the URL compareIds parameter and component state) or navigate to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id> from within the comparison view.

Workflow: Open program details for a chosen option
1. User browses the results list or comparison view.
2. User clicks on a program’s card area or a dedicated "View Details" action.
3. Page performs navigation to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<selectedProgramId> (without additional state persistence besides URL).
4. User can return to training.html via browser back; filters, sort, and comparison selection restore from URL parameters.
interactions: On initial page load, the controller component reads URL parameters (deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxPrice, currency, minLiveSessions, maxParticipants, sortBy, compareIds) and initializes in-memory filter and selection state.

On initial page load after state initialization, the controller calls getTrainingProgramFilterOptions to populate filter controls, then calls searchTrainingPrograms using the current filter and sort state reflected in URL parameters.

On changing a filter control (delivery format, region, capacity band, numeric minParticipants, numeric maxParticipants, budget range, numeric maxPrice, currency, minimum live sessions), the filter panel updates the URL parameters and signals the controller to refresh results via searchTrainingPrograms.

On clicking a "Clear filters" or equivalent action, the filter panel removes relevant URL parameters (deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, maxPrice, currency, minLiveSessions, sortBy) and triggers a fresh call to searchTrainingPrograms with default parameters.

On selecting a sort option (e.g., by price ascending, price descending, or other supported fields), the sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new call to searchTrainingPrograms.

On receiving new search results from searchTrainingPrograms, the results list component binds the returned TrainingProgram array to program cards and triggers the cheapest-program-highlighter to recompute which result is lowest-priced based on current currency and filter constraints.

On ticking or unticking a comparison checkbox on a program card, the comparison selector updates the list of selected trainingProgramIds in its internal state and in the URL (e.g., compareIds), and the comparison view updates accordingly using only the already-fetched result data.
On clicking a program card or "View Details" link, the page navigates to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id> using the clicked program’s id field; no additional API is called here.

On browser back/forward navigation, the controller re-reads the URL parameters, restores the filter/sort/selection state, and re-runs searchTrainingPrograms so the list and comparison view match the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s state is fully driven by URL parameters and API responses; no localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. On load and on each URL change, the controller parses URL parameters and maps them to the searchTrainingPrograms request:
- deliveryFormat (string): If present, passed directly to searchTrainingPrograms.deliveryFormat and used to preselect the delivery format filter control.
- region (string): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.region and used to preselect the region filter control.
- minParticipants (number): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.minParticipants and used as the lower bound for participant capacity filter elements.
- maxParticipants (number): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.maxParticipants and used as the upper bound for capacity filtering.
- maxPrice (number): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.maxPrice and used as a budget constraint in the UI (e.g., numeric budget input or derived from a budget range selection).
- currency (string): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.currency and used to set the currency filter control. If absent, a default may be chosen based on filter options or server-side defaults, and once the user selects a currency, the choice is reflected back into the URL.
- minLiveSessions (number): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.minLiveSessions and bound to the minimum live sessions filter.
- sortBy (string): If present, passed to searchTrainingPrograms.sortBy and used to preset the sort control. When user changes sorting, the new value is written into the URL and used in subsequent API calls.
- compareIds (string/CSV or repeated parameter): If present, defines which trainingProgramIds are in the comparison selection. After each searchTrainingPrograms call, the comparison component looks up the current results for these IDs and displays them; IDs not in the current result set are ignored in the comparison view.
Internally, the controller maintains a single 'currentFilters' state object mirroring the above URL parameters. Any user action that changes filters, sorting, or comparison selection must update the URL to remain the single source of truth, then trigger searchTrainingPrograms using the updated 'currentFilters' state. getTrainingProgramFilterOptions is called once on initial page load (and optionally on soft reload) and its output remains in memory to drive filter option lists.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL-state synchronization, and interaction between filter controls, results list, comparison selector, and cheapest-program highlighter.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters (deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, maxPrice, currency, minLiveSessions, sortBy, compareIds) and initialize internal state; call getTrainingProgramFilterOptions; once filter options are available, call searchTrainingPrograms with the current filter/sort state.
onUrlChange: When the URL query string changes (e.g., via pushState or browser navigation), re-parse parameters, update internal state, and call searchTrainingPrograms with the new state.
onFiltersUpdated(filters): Receive updated filters from training-filters-panel, update URL parameters accordingly, and call searchTrainingPrograms with merged state.
onSortUpdated(sortBy): Update sortBy in URL, update internal state, and call searchTrainingPrograms.

onResultsLoaded(results, totalCount): Store latest TrainingProgram results and totalCount, notify training-results-list, cheapest-program-highlighter, and training-comparison-selector to re-render using the new data.
onCompareSelectionUpdated(selectedIds): Update compareIds in URL, store selectedIds in state, and notify training-comparison-selector to re-render.

components.functionality: Displays and manages filter controls (delivery format, region, capacity, budget, live sessions, currency) based on options from getTrainingProgramFilterOptions and URL state; emits updated filter objects to the controller.
event_handlers: onInit: Request filter options by invoking getTrainingProgramFilterOptions via the controller; once received, populate dropdowns for deliveryFormats, regions, capacityBands, budgetRanges, and liveSessionCounts.

onPrefillFromUrl(urlParams): When initialized, preselect filter controls using URL parameters (deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, maxPrice, currency, minLiveSessions) if present.
onDeliveryFormatChange(value): Update internal filter state and emit updated filters to training-search-controller.onFiltersUpdated.
onRegionChange(value): Update internal filter state and emit updated filters.
onCapacityBandSelect(band): Map selected capacity band to minParticipants and maxParticipants, update internal state, and emit updated filters.
onMinParticipantsInputChange(value): Update minParticipants, emit updated filters.
onMaxParticipantsInputChange(value): Update maxParticipants, emit updated filters.
onBudgetRangeSelect(range): Map to maxPrice and currency, update state, emit updated filters.
onMaxPriceInputChange(value): Update maxPrice, emit updated filters.
onCurrencyChange(value): Update currency, emit updated filters.
onMinLiveSessionsChange(value): Update minLiveSessions, emit updated filters.
onClearFiltersClick: Reset all filter controls to their default values, remove related URL parameters through the controller, and emit filters without constraints to trigger a new search.

components.functionality: Provides sort options for the training program list (including price-based sorting) and synchronizes the chosen sort order with the URL and searchTrainingPrograms.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl(urlParams): Read sortBy from URL and preselect the appropriate sort option.

onSortOptionChange(sortBy): When user selects a sort option (for example, price ascending, price descending, or other supported criteria), emit sortBy to training-search-controller.onSortUpdated to update URL and trigger a new search.

components.functionality: Renders the list of training programs returned by searchTrainingPrograms, showing key attributes and exposing actions to view details or add to comparison selection.
event_handlers: onResultsDataUpdate(results): Receive updated TrainingProgram array from the controller and render a list of program items/cards with fields such as name, description snippet, price, currency, deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, liveSessionsCount, and durationDays where available.
onProgramCardClick(trainingProgramId): Navigate to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id>.
onViewDetailsClick(trainingProgramId): Navigate to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id>.
onCompareCheckboxToggle(trainingProgramId, isSelected): Notify training-search-controller.onCompareSelectionUpdated with the new list of selected IDs (old selection plus/minus this ID).
onHighlightCheapestUpdate(cheapestProgramId): When notified by cheapest-program-highlighter, visually mark the cheapest program item as the lowest-priced within current results.

components.functionality: Determines which training program in the current result set is the cheapest based on price (and optionally constrained by currency or budget filters) and informs the results list for highlighting.
event_handlers: onResultsDataUpdate(results, currentFilters): Compute the cheapest program among the provided results by comparing the price field, taking into account the current currency filter from currentFilters. Once computed, emit the cheapest program’s id to training-results-list.onHighlightCheapestUpdate for visual indication.

onFiltersOrCurrencyChange(currentFilters): When currency or budget filters change, recompute the cheapest program using the latest results that match the new constraints and re-emit the updated cheapestProgramId.

components.functionality: Manages and displays a comparison view of user-selected training programs using only the current page’s search results and URL-based selection state.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl(compareIds, results): When the page loads or the results update, read compareIds from the URL, find matching programs in the latest results, and display them in a comparison panel with columns for key attributes (price, currency, deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, liveSessionsCount, durationDays).
onCompareSelectionUpdated(selectedIds, results): When the controller notifies of an updated selection, filter the results by selectedIds and update the comparison table accordingly.

onRemoveFromComparison(trainingProgramId): Remove the program from the local selection, notify training-search-controller.onCompareSelectionUpdated with the new selectedIds list so the URL compareIds is updated.
onCompareProgramDetailsClick(trainingProgramId): Navigate to training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=<id> from within the comparison panel.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s hero logic but adapted for a search/browse layout. At the top of the content area (below the global header/nav), a single, horizontally centered search controller acts as the page’s "hero" control. Directly beneath it, the main area splits into a two-column layout: the left column (about 60–65% width) is the primary content stack (sort control + results list), and the right column (about 35–40% width) becomes a structured, vertical stack of supporting tools (filters, cheapest indicator, comparison selector). Within each column, content flows in a clear top-to-bottom sequence with generous spacing between clusters.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s hero logic but adapted for a search/browse layout. At the top of the content area (below the global header/nav), a single, horizontally centered search controller acts as the page’s "hero" control. Directly beneath it, the main area splits into a two-column layout: the left column (about 60–65% width) is the primary content stack (sort control + results list), and the right column (about 35–40% width) becomes a structured, vertical stack of supporting tools (filters, cheapest indicator, comparison selector). Within each column, content flows in a clear top-to-bottom sequence with generous spacing between clusters.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main container is centered with a max-width of roughly 1100–1200px and 24–32px horizontal padding. The search controller spans about 70–80% of the container width and sits alone with ample vertical breathing room. Below, the left column occupies ~65% of the width for the results area; the right column occupies ~35% for filters and indicators. Vertically, the search band plus the upper portion of the results area visually approximate a hero-like block, with 64–96px top padding from the global header and similar bottom padding before subsequent site sections (if any).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and editorial: each functional cluster (search, sort, filters, results, indicators, comparison) is separated by 24–32px gaps, with 16–24px internal padding in each component. Lists (results, filters) do not feel cramped; cards use plenty of negative space and clear hierarchy. The dark background with high-contrast type reinforces a clean, minimal feel. No element spans edge-to-edge; everything respects the 8px baseline and margin system.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the large, centered search controller at the top. From there, the gaze moves down into the left column to scan the sort control and then the vertical stack of training cards. As users scroll, their peripheral vision catches the right column where the filters and cheapest-program indicator live, echoing the mockup’s portrait-as-counterbalance behavior. The comparison selector echoes the chat bubble behavior: it anchors to the bottom-right and becomes a persistent, subtle call-to-action that the eye naturally reaches after scanning across the results. Overall, the pattern is a Z-shaped flow: top-left (search label/input) across to top-right (auxiliary search actions if any), down left through results, then across to the right column’s tools and finally to the bottom-right comparison selector.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global dark-mode header/nav, the Training Programs page opens with a spacious, centered search band on a charcoal background. This band feels like a mini-hero: a prominent search field, perhaps a compact label or context line above it, and any secondary search actions aligned horizontally beneath or beside it. The search area spans roughly three-quarters of the main container, with generous 48–64px vertical padding so it reads as the primary control. Immediately below, the content splits into a two-column layout within the same centered container. The left column, slightly wider, hosts the sortable list of training programs: a slim sort control row at the top, followed by vertically stacked program cards, each breathing with ample whitespace. The right column acts as a structured sidebar: a small, high-impact cheapest-program indicator sits at the top, followed by a tall filter panel card with grouped filter sections. Both columns share a common top alignment, reinforcing the clean grid. As users scroll, the filter panel tends to remain visible via sticky behavior on desktop, mimicking the mockup’s stable right-side portrait. At the bottom-right of the viewport, a compact, floating comparison selector echoes a chat bubble—hovering just above the page edge, it stays available as users scroll through results, visually tying together the browsing and decision-making experience in a modern, minimalist, and professional way.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training search controller sits at the very top of the page’s main content area, directly below the global header, centered within the max-width container. It spans approximately 70–80% of the container width on desktop, leaving clear margins on both sides to maintain the spacious, editorial feel. Vertically, it is given generous padding—around 32px top and bottom within the component—and is isolated from the two-column layout below by a 32–48px gap, so it reads as a distinct, hero-like band. The search input itself is the dominant element, horizontally centered or left-aligned within the controller, with any labels or helper text above it and secondary actions (e.g., advanced options) aligned to the right or just below, forming a simple top-to-bottom hierarchy. On smaller screens, this component expands to nearly full width with preserved vertical spacing, stacking internal elements vertically to remain legible and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, but visually treated as a hero control with generous top margin from the global header (~64px) and a clear separation from the subsequent two-column content. On mobile, it sits full-width above all other components, with consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training filters panel occupies the upper-middle portion of the right column in the main two-column layout. The right column is about 35–40% of the container width on desktop, starting directly to the right of the results column, with a 32px horizontal gap between columns. The filters panel is displayed as a tall, dark card with slightly lighter background than the page, full width within the right column, with 24px internal padding and 24–32px spacing between filter groups. It begins just below or immediately after the cheapest-program-highlighter (which sits above it in the right column), creating a tidy vertical stack: indicator on top, filter card beneath. The top of the filter panel is visually aligned with the top of the results list in the left column, so both columns start at the same vertical level, echoing the mockup’s balanced hero layout. On mobile, the filter panel collapses into a horizontally full-width, collapsible drawer or accordion placed above the results list, keeping it accessible but not overwhelming the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the filter panel behaves as a sticky sidebar: its top edge is offset slightly below the global header and search controller (e.g., 16–24px) and remains visible as the user scrolls the results column, up until the bottom of the page. This mimics the persistent right-side visual in the mockup. On mobile, sticky behavior is removed; the panel becomes a collapsible section near the top of the content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training sort control sits at the top of the left column, directly beneath the search controller and aligned to the same left edge within the container. It spans the full width of the left column (~60–65% of the container). The control is relatively compact vertically—about 40–56px tall—with 16px vertical padding, and sits on a subtle card or lightly elevated row to distinguish it from the results list below. Within this row, the sort label is left-aligned, while the sort dropdown or buttons are right-aligned, creating a horizontal tension similar to the header’s nav vs. social icons in the mockup. A small text element, such as the number of results, may be positioned close to the sort label or directly below it in smaller type, preserving the clear hierarchy. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom of this sort control and the top of the first results card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column’s flow. On long scrolls, it scrolls away with the results, but due to the hero-like prominence of the search controller above, the sort remains a local, contextual control rather than a global sticky bar. On narrower screens, its internal layout stacks vertically: result count on top, sort input below, both left-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training results list occupies the bulk of the left column beneath the sort control. The left column takes up roughly 60–65% of the container width and runs from just below the sort control down toward the fold and beyond. Each training program is presented as an individual card with generous 24px internal padding and 24–32px vertical spacing between cards to maintain the low-density, editorial aesthetic. Cards are full-width within the left column, aligning their left edges to the sort control and search controller above, creating a strong vertical rhythm. Program titles and key details use high-contrast white text, with secondary metadata in muted tones, mirroring the typography hierarchy from the hero. Horizontally, the right column (filters and indicators) sits to the immediate right of these cards, separated by a consistent 32px gutter, allowing the eye to move laterally from a specific result to associated filtering tools. On smaller viewports, the results list becomes a single-column, full-width stack below the search and filters, preserving the card spacing and hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content: it forms the main scroll area of the page. The results list is not sticky; instead, the right-column filters remain more stationary (via sticky behavior) to echo the mockup’s stable right-side visual while the primary content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cheapest-program highlighter is a compact, high-impact strip positioned at the very top of the right column, just above the training filters panel. It spans the full width of the right column (~35–40% of the container) and is visually designed as a small, slightly brighter or accented card that stands out against the dark background—similar in weight to a badge or highlighted statistic. Internally, it contains a concise label (e.g., “Cheapest available program”) and a key figure (price) in larger type, possibly with an icon or subtle accent color aligned to the right. There is a 16–24px gap below this highlighter before the beginning of the filters panel. Its top aligns vertically with the top of the sort control and results list, so both the left and right columns share a unified starting baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column’s stack but visually coupled with the sticky filter panel. When the filters panel becomes sticky on desktop, the highlighter is effectively part of that sticky block, staying visible near the top of the right column as the user scrolls results. On mobile, this highlighter appears above the collapsible filter section, full-width, and scrolls away with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training comparison selector is positioned as a floating element at the bottom-right of the viewport, echoing the chat bubble behavior from the mockup. It sits above the right column and results content, offset about 24–32px from the right edge of the container (or viewport on smaller screens) and 24–32px from the bottom edge. The component is compact—roughly the size of a pill or small card—containing an icon or label (e.g., “Compare (3)”) with a subtle glow or border to differentiate it from the background without being intrusive. As the user scrolls down the results list, the selector remains in place, always within thumb reach and visually aligned with the right column’s tools. When expanded, it can rise upward as a small overlay panel aligned with the same bottom-right anchor, listing selected programs in a tidy mini-list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s bottom-right corner on desktop and tablet, maintaining a consistent offset relative to the container’s right edge to feel anchored to the layout rather than the browser edge. On mobile, it can shift slightly inward or upward to avoid overlapping primary navigation or system UI, but remains fixed as a floating action element. It does not participate in the normal scroll flow, staying constantly visible as a persistent utility.

name: Training Detail & Booking
title: Training Program Detail & Booking
description: Displays a specific training program’s details and allows users to submit a booking/request form for that program.

core_features: Load and display full details of a specific training program based on the trainingProgramId URL parameter using getTrainingProgramDetail.
Show structured training information, including description, agenda, learning objectives, delivery format, region, participant capacity, number of live sessions, duration, and pricing.
Provide a primary call-to-action button that moves the user focus to the booking form for the currently viewed training program.
Display a booking form bound to the current training program, collecting contact name, email, company name, participants count, desired start date, region preference, and notes/objectives.
Validate booking form input before submission (required fields, basic format checks).
Submit the booking/request data via submitTrainingBookingRequest with the current trainingProgramId and form values.
Handle and display the response from submitTrainingBookingRequest, including success/failure messaging and booking request details where appropriate.
Allow users to navigate back to the training catalog (training.html) via a dedicated link.
user_workflows:
View training details and submit booking request:
1. User navigates to training_detail.html with a trainingProgramId URL parameter.
2. On page initialization, the page reads trainingProgramId from the URL.
3. The page calls getTrainingProgramDetail(trainingProgramId).
4. While loading, the user sees a loading state for the training details section.
5. When the TrainingProgram is returned, the page populates the training detail sections (summary, agenda, learning objectives, delivery format, region, participant limits, live sessions count, duration, pricing).
6. User reviews the information and clicks the primary booking button.
7. The page scrolls or focuses to the booking form, which is pre-associated with the current trainingProgramId.
8. User fills in contact name, contact email, company name (optional), participants count (optional), desired start date, region preference (optional), and notes/training objectives (optional).
9. User submits the form.
10. The page validates required fields (e.g., contactName, contactEmail) and basic formats (e.g., email, date) on the client side.
11. If validation passes, the page calls submitTrainingBookingRequest with trainingProgramId and all form inputs.
12. While the request is being processed, the form is disabled or shows a submitting state.
13. When the API response returns, the page checks the success flag.
14. If success is true, the page displays a confirmation message and may show key data from bookingRequest (e.g., derived status or requested start date), and can optionally keep the submitted values visible in a read-only view.
15. If success is false or an error occurs, the page displays an error message and allows the user to adjust the form and resubmit.

Navigate back to training catalog:
1. User clicks the "Back to Training Catalog" link.
2. The browser navigates to training.html without additional parameters (or with catalog-level parameters if defined elsewhere).

Handle missing or invalid trainingProgramId:
1. User opens training_detail.html without a trainingProgramId or with a value that leads to an error from getTrainingProgramDetail.
2. On initialization, the page attempts to read trainingProgramId from the URL.
3. If trainingProgramId is missing, the page shows an error state indicating the training program could not be identified and offers a link to training.html.
4. If trainingProgramId is present but getTrainingProgramDetail returns an error or an inactive program, the page shows an appropriate error or unavailable state and provides a link back to training.html.
interactions: On page load (or when the URL parameter changes), the page reads trainingProgramId from the URL query string and triggers getTrainingProgramDetail.
If trainingProgramId is present, clicking reload/refresh re-triggers getTrainingProgramDetail for that same ID.
Clicking the primary "Book Training" / "Request This Program" button scrolls or focuses the viewport on the booking form component associated with the current TrainingProgram.

Typing into booking form inputs (contact name, email, company name, participants count, desired start date, notes/objectives, region preference) updates the internal form state but does not yet call any API.

Changing the desired start date input updates a date value that will be mapped to desiredStartDate when submitting submitTrainingBookingRequest (e.g., an internal transformation from month/year selection to a concrete datetime value).
Submitting the booking form triggers client-side validation and, if valid, calls submitTrainingBookingRequest with trainingProgramId and the collected form values.
While submitTrainingBookingRequest is in progress, form inputs and submit button are disabled or marked as loading to prevent duplicate submissions.
On successful submission, the page shows a confirmation state, potentially disabling the form or showing a clear success banner with data from bookingRequest and message.
On failed submission, the page displays an error state with the response message if available, keeps the current form values, and re-enables the submit button for retry.
Clicking the "Back to Training Catalog" navigation link causes the browser to navigate to training.html, clearing the current training-specific context.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the trainingProgramId URL query parameter. When trainingProgramId is present, the page invokes getTrainingProgramDetail(trainingProgramId) to populate the TrainingProgram state. If trainingProgramId changes (e.g., via navigation to a different training_detail.html?trainingProgramId=...), the page re-runs getTrainingProgramDetail and replaces the existing TrainingProgram state. Page-level states include: (1) loading: when getTrainingProgramDetail is in progress; (2) loadError: when the TrainingProgram cannot be loaded (missing or invalid trainingProgramId or API error); (3) loaded: when a valid TrainingProgram object is available. The booking form maintains its own local form state (field values and validation errors) and a submission state. Submission state transitions: idle -> submitting -> success or error. On submit, submitTrainingBookingRequest is called with trainingProgramId and the current form state. The response object (containing success, bookingRequest, and message) is stored in a transient page state to control display of confirmation or error messaging. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client storage mechanisms; all persistent identifiers are managed via the URL parameter (trainingProgramId) and real-time API calls.

components.functionality: Reads the trainingProgramId from the URL, calls getTrainingProgramDetail, and manages loading, success, and error states for the TrainingProgram. Exposes the loaded TrainingProgram object (or error) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read trainingProgramId from the URL query string and, if present, invoke getTrainingProgramDetail(trainingProgramId).
onURLParamChange: When trainingProgramId changes in the URL, re-invoke getTrainingProgramDetail with the new ID and reset loading/error states.
onLoadSuccess: Store the returned TrainingProgram in component state and set loading=false, error=null.
onLoadError: Set an error state and make TrainingProgram data unavailable, to be handled by a downstream error display component.

components.functionality: Displays core information from the current TrainingProgram, such as its name, description, duration, delivery format, region, and high-level capacity information. Provides quick context before the user views the full agenda and objectives.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the TrainingProgram from training-detail-loader updates, re-render the header details.
onMissingData: If TrainingProgram is unavailable due to error, show a concise message that the program could not be loaded (actual messaging text is configurable).

components.functionality: Displays structured details of the training program such as agenda, learning objectives, number of live sessions, duration in days, and any participant limits using the TrainingProgram data.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Update rendered agenda and learning objectives whenever the TrainingProgram object changes.
onToggleSection: Allow sections (e.g., agenda or objectives) to be expanded/collapsed; this is purely client-side and does not call any interface.

components.functionality: Shows logistical attributes of the TrainingProgram such as deliveryFormat, region, minParticipants, maxParticipants, isRemoteFriendly, and pricing (price and currency). Provides a functional summary of how and where the training is delivered and for how many participants at what price.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Refresh logistics details when TrainingProgram changes.

components.functionality: Provides the primary call-to-action to initiate booking for the currently loaded TrainingProgram. When clicked, moves the user focus/scroll to the booking form component.
event_handlers: onClick: Scroll or jump to the training-booking-form component and set focus on its first input field if possible.
onDataUnavailable: Disable or hide the CTA if TrainingProgram failed to load or is unavailable.

components.functionality: Collects booking/request details for the current training program and submits them via submitTrainingBookingRequest. Fields are bound to form state and mapped directly to the parameters of submitTrainingBookingRequest.
event_handlers: onInit: Initialize empty form state for contactName, contactEmail, companyName, participantsCount, desiredStartDate (in a UI-appropriate format such as date or month/year), notes, and regionPreference, and associate the form with the current TrainingProgram.trainingProgramId.
onFieldChange: Update local form state as the user types or changes values in any field.

onValidate: Before submission, validate required fields (contactName, contactEmail), ensure email format is syntactically valid, ensure participantsCount is a non-negative number when provided, and verify that a desiredStartDate selection conforms to the allowed format.

onTransformDesiredStartDate: Convert the user’s selected desired start date (which might be a month/year or a full date) into a datetime value acceptable for the desiredStartDate parameter when calling submitTrainingBookingRequest.

onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitTrainingBookingRequest with trainingProgramId (from TrainingProgram.id or the URL parameter), contactName, contactEmail, companyName, participantsCount, transformed desiredStartDate, notes, and regionPreference. Set internal state to submitting=true while awaiting the response.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitTrainingBookingRequest returns with success=true, store bookingRequest and message in component state, set submitting=false, and trigger display of the booking-confirmation-banner component. Optionally prevent further edits or disable the form, depending on configuration.

onSubmitError: When submitTrainingBookingRequest fails or returns success=false, capture the message, set submitting=false, and display an inline error while keeping current form values for correction and resubmission.

components.functionality: Displays confirmation or error messages after a booking submission attempt, based on the response from submitTrainingBookingRequest. Can show key fields from bookingRequest, such as the requested start date or status, if appropriate.
event_handlers: onBookingResponse: Listen to submission results from training-booking-form and update the displayed status (success or error) and associated message.
onDismiss: Allow the user to dismiss the confirmation or error banner; this only affects UI state and does not modify any stored data.
onResubmitRequested: If the user triggers a reattempt (e.g., from an error state), focus the training-booking-form component for correction and re-submission.

components.functionality: Handles and displays errors related to loading the TrainingProgram, such as missing trainingProgramId or failed getTrainingProgramDetail calls. Provides a functional navigation link back to the training catalog.
event_handlers: onLoadError: When training-detail-loader reports an error, display a non-technical message that the training program could not be loaded and show a link to the catalog.
onBackToCatalogClick: Navigate to training.html without relying on additional APIs.

components.functionality: Provides a consistent navigational control for users to return to the main training catalog page (training.html).
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to training.html using standard link behavior. No additional parameters are required by this page’s functionality.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses the hero pattern from the mockup: a prominent training-detail header at the top functioning as a hero band, with text-first emphasis on the left (program name, short description, key value prop, primary booking CTA) and supporting meta details on the right. Beneath this hero, the body splits into a two-column layout: the left column stacks the training-structure and logistics sections in a single vertical reading flow, while the right column holds the booking form as a persistent, card-like module. The back-to-training link, confirmation banner, loader, and error panel all live in the same main content column and appear above or instead of the main content as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses the hero pattern from the mockup: a prominent training-detail header at the top functioning as a hero band, with text-first emphasis on the left (program name, short description, key value prop, primary booking CTA) and supporting meta details on the right. Beneath this hero, the body splits into a two-column layout: the left column stacks the training-structure and logistics sections in a single vertical reading flow, while the right column holds the booking form as a persistent, card-like module. The back-to-training link, confirmation banner, loader, and error panel all live in the same main content column and appear above or instead of the main content as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) sits below the global site header. The training-detail header spans the full container width but internally uses about 60–65% for the left text column and 35–40% for the right meta/summary column, mirroring the hero split from the mockup. In the main content band below, the grid again allocates ~60–65% of the horizontal space to the left detail sections and ~35–40% to the right booking form. Vertical spacing between major sections (header → details → booking area bottom) follows large 64–96px gaps, with 24–32px gaps between related elements inside each section.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately sparse and editorial: generous padding around the header, large type for the training title, and clear separation between clusters (title, meta info, description, CTA, sections). Each section (structure, logistics, form) is treated as a clean block with comfortable margins and limited on-screen elements at once. The booking form is visually compact but not crowded, with clear grouping of fields and ample breathing room inside a card. No dense tables; information is broken into short paragraphs and bullet lists with consistent 16–24px vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye lands first on the large training title in the header’s left column, then slightly above to register the small all-caps context or category. From there, it moves down to the short description and nearby primary booking CTA. The right side of the header provides a quick scan of essentials (duration, format, price), echoing the hero’s counterbalance portrait. Scrolling down, the gaze follows the left column from training structure into logistics. In parallel, the right column booking form forms a persistent focal point—particularly due to its card styling and slight elevation—capturing attention whenever the user looks to the right. Status elements (confirmation banner, error panel) appear high in the visual hierarchy, just under the global nav, so they’re seen before the main content on load or after submitting the form.

layout.overall_description: Below the global site header and navigation, the page content sits in a centered, max-width container against a dark, modern backdrop that echoes the hero aesthetic of the mockup. At the very top of this container, a subtle back-to-training link aligns to the left, giving a lightweight breadcrumb feel without competing visually. Just below, a wide hero-like training-detail header dominates the upper viewport: on the left, a slim all-caps label introduces the training category, followed by a large, multi-line training title, a concise supporting paragraph, and the primary booking CTA button, all stacked with generous vertical spacing. On the right, a tall, clean meta panel mirrors the portrait’s role from the original mockup, summarizing key facts such as format, duration, and audience within a compact card area. As the user scrolls, the page transitions into a two-column content band: the left column flows seamlessly into the training structure and then the logistics section, each clearly separated with ample breathing room; the right column holds the booking form inside a lightly elevated card that stays visually anchored as a conversion focal point. When a booking is submitted, a full-width confirmation banner appears just under the site nav and above the hero, spanning the content width and pushing everything down, ensuring the success state is immediately noticed. In error or loading states, the loader or error panel temporarily replaces this central content region, remaining centered and uncluttered. Overall, the layout feels spacious, strategic, and focused on guiding users from understanding the training to confidently completing a booking request.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-detail-loader appears as an overlay centered within the main content region below the global site header. It covers the area where the back link, header, sections, and booking form would normally render, dimming the background with a subtle dark tint while keeping the fixed site header visible above. The loader’s visual element (spinner or skeleton) is vertically centered in the viewport and horizontally centered within the max-width container, occupying roughly the middle third of the screen. Padding around the loader content follows the spacious style, with at least 64px of clear space on all sides to avoid visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-content overlay for the main page area: positioned relative to the viewport below the global header, blocking interaction with underlying elements while active. It scrolls with the page only if the viewport is smaller than the content; otherwise it remains effectively locked in the visible center until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-detail-header functions as a hero band located near the top of the page, directly beneath any confirmation banner (if present) and the back-to-training link. It spans the full width of the central container, with 64–96px of top padding and 64px of bottom padding, creating a dominant block that fills most of the initial viewport on larger screens. Inside, it is divided into two columns: the left column takes about 60–65% of the width and hosts a vertical stack of content—first a small, all-caps context label aligned to the left, then a large multi-line H1 training title, then a short 2–3 line descriptive paragraph. Below this, with a 24–32px gap, there is a horizontal row reserved for the primary booking CTA (training-booking-cta) aligned to the left. The right column occupies the remaining 35–40% and is aligned to the same top baseline as the context label. It contains a vertically centered meta card or panel, with 24–32px horizontal padding inside, summarizing at-a-glance details like duration, format, price, and level in a stacked layout. The right column’s height roughly matches the height of the left text stack, creating a balanced, rectangular hero composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal document flow, immediately following any status banner and back link. On scroll, it moves off-screen naturally; there is no sticky behavior, but its generous height ensures that on most desktop viewports the title, description, and CTA are fully visible above the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-structure-section is the first major body section beneath the hero header, aligned within the same central container. There is a generous vertical gap (64–80px) between the bottom of the header and the top of this section. On desktop, it is anchored to the left column of a two-column layout: it occupies approximately 60–65% of the container width, using the same left alignment as the hero’s text. Within this left column, the section starts with a medium-sized section title (e.g., “Training Structure” or similar) aligned to the left, followed by 16–24px vertical spacing, then paragraphs and/or bullet lists detailing the curriculum or module breakdown. Content is arranged in a clear top-to-bottom flow with consistent 16–24px gaps between paragraphs or bullet groups. The right side of the page, directly opposite the top of this section, is visually occupied by the booking form card, creating a strong horizontal alignment between the start of the structure section and the top of the form. On tablet and mobile, the structure section becomes full-width and appears above the booking form, maintaining its generous top and bottom spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as part of the main left content column. On wide screens, it shares a row with the booking form in the right column; on narrower breakpoints it stacks above the form, always maintaining generous vertical spacing from adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-logistics-section is positioned directly beneath the training-structure-section in the same left content column, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of 48–64px. It aligns to the same left edge and shares the same width (around 60–65% of the container on desktop). At the top of this section, a clear heading (e.g., “Logistics” or “Practical Details”) appears left-aligned, with 16–24px of space below before the supporting text. Content inside is grouped logically: a short introductory paragraph, followed by small subheadings or bullet lists for items like location, delivery format, group size, and prerequisites, each separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. On desktop, this section continues to sit opposite the booking form column, but as the user scrolls, the form’s sticky behavior keeps it visible while the logistics content moves up and down in the left column. On mobile, the logistics section appears above the booking form, directly below the structure section, each separated by generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Regular flowing section within the left column. It scrolls normally beneath the hero and structure section and does not have sticky or fixed positioning, ensuring a smooth editorial reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-booking-cta is placed within the lower portion of the training-detail-header’s left column, directly beneath the descriptive paragraph. It appears as a prominent, horizontally oriented primary button aligned to the left edge of the text column, with 24–32px of vertical space above it and at least 16–24px of space below before any secondary text or links. The button width is content-based but visually substantial, typically occupying 25–40% of the header’s width on desktop without stretching edge-to-edge, reinforcing the minimalist, high-impact style. It may be accompanied by optional, smaller secondary actions to its right or below, but it remains the clear focal point in that row. When clicked, it is visually associated with scrolling or jumping the user down to the booking form section in the right column of the body content, leveraging the hero’s CTA row as the main conversion gateway from the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the header’s left column, participating in the normal document flow. It does not stick to the viewport; its prominence is achieved by positioning, size, and contrast. On smaller screens it remains left-aligned within the stacked header content and is typically visible without scrolling on most mobile viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-booking-form is housed in the right column of the main content region, starting at roughly the same vertical level as the top of the training-structure-section. On desktop, it occupies about 35–40% of the container width and is encapsulated in a card-like block with 24–32px internal padding and a subtle elevation or border to distinguish it from the dark background, echoing the clean UI treatment from the mockup’s portrait/chat area. The form card aligns to the right edge of the grid and leaves a balanced gap between itself and the left-column content (typically 32–48px). Inside the card, fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, grouped logically (contact info, organization, training preferences), and capped with the form’s internal submit control at the bottom. The card’s height may exceed the viewport; however, its top is aligned just below the hero, so the top of the form is visible as soon as the user scrolls down to the content section. On tablet and mobile, the form breaks out of the two-column layout and becomes a full-width section placed beneath the training-structure and training-logistics sections, maintaining generous 48–64px top padding to clearly separate it from the preceding content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the form behaves as a sticky side card: its container is positioned so that the top of the card sticks a short distance below the global header (e.g., with a 24–32px top offset) while the user scrolls through the left column details, until the bottom of the page content is reached. This keeps the booking option persistently visible. On smaller breakpoints where vertical space is limited, the form loses the sticky behavior and scrolls normally as a full-width section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-banner appears as a horizontally spanning band located at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global site header/navigation and above the back-to-training link and hero header. It stretches the full width of the main container and visually reads as a slim but noticeable strip, with 16–24px vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding inside. The text is centered or left-aligned depending on the broader site style, but in either case it’s easy to scan at a glance. There is a comfortable 24–32px gap between the bottom of this banner and the back-to-training-catalog-link below, ensuring it feels distinct and not crowded. The banner effectively pushes the rest of the content down, so the confirmation message is the first thing in-view after a successful submission, leveraging the high-contrast hero styling to stand out.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered conditionally in the normal document flow. When present, it occupies a fixed vertical space at the top of the content stack but scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down. It does not cover or overlap content; instead, it reflows the page by pushing the hero and subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-load-error-panel replaces the usual content when a training fails to load. It is centered within the same max-width container, starting just below the global header. The panel itself is a card-like block occupying roughly 50–60% of the container width on desktop, centered horizontally and positioned so that its center aligns near the vertical midpoint of the viewport. It features generous internal padding (24–32px) with a clear error icon or heading at the top, a concise explanation underneath, and the back-to-training-catalog-link either integrated within the panel’s content or placed directly below it with a 16–24px gap. The surrounding area is intentionally empty, leaving large margins above and below (at least 64px) to maintain the minimalist, uncluttered feel even in error states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in place of the normal header, sections, and form—when active, those components are not rendered. It scrolls normally if the viewport is shorter than the panel, but on typical screens it effectively appears as a centered, modal-like message within the standard page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-training-catalog-link is a small, understated navigation link positioned at the top-left of the main content container, acting like a breadcrumb. It sits directly below any confirmation banner (if present) and 24–32px beneath the bottom of the global header, aligning with the left edge of the grid used by the hero’s text. The link may include a left-pointing arrow icon followed by the text label (e.g., “Back to trainings”), and it occupies only the minimum width needed for its content. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between this link and the top of the training-detail-header, so it feels visually separated yet clearly associated with the page title area. On mobile, it remains at the very top of the content stack, stretching no more than its text width but still aligned to the left margin for consistency.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A simple inline element in the normal flow at the top of the page content. It scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down and does not have sticky or floating behavior, maintaining its role as a light, non-dominant navigation affordance.

name: About
title: About
description: About page that dynamically loads and displays the consulting firm’s mission, values, team overview, industries served, and credentials using getAboutPageContent, and provides navigation to related pages.

core_features: Load structured About content (mission, values, team summary, industries served, certifications, partnerships, achievements) via getAboutPageContent when the page initializes.

Display separate, addressable sections (mission, values, team, industries served, certifications and partnerships, notable achievements) that can be directly targeted and focused using URL parameters.
Provide in-page navigation that updates the URL (e.g., ?section=team) and scrolls/focuses the corresponding section without reloading the page.
Expose clear CTAs to Book a Consultation, View Client Case Studies, and Contact Us, with navigation that forwards context parameters in the URL (e.g., source=about&section=team).
Handle loading and error states for getAboutPageContent and update the visible sections accordingly (e.g., show/hide sections based on API response completeness).
user_workflows:
Workflow: First-time visitor reads About content
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with parameters like about.html?section=mission).
2. The page controller reads URL parameters (e.g., section, ref).
3. The about-content-loader component calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
4. While waiting, a loading state is shown instead of content sections.
5. On success, the mission, values, teamSummary, industriesServed, certificationsAndPartnerships, and notableAchievements from the response are passed to the respective section components.
6. The section-manager checks the section URL parameter (if present) and scrolls/focuses the corresponding section.
7. The user scrolls or uses in-page navigation links to view different sections; the visible/active section is reflected in the section URL parameter.

Workflow: User navigates to Book Consultation from About
1. User is on about.html, having loaded content via getAboutPageContent.
2. The user clicks the 'Book a Consultation' CTA inside the about-cta-panel.
3. The CTA click handler reads the current URL parameters (e.g., section, ref) and constructs a navigation URL to book_consultation.html.
4. The handler appends context parameters such as source=about and section=<current section value if any> to the target URL (e.g., book_consultation.html?source=about&section=team).
5. Browser navigates to the constructed book_consultation.html URL; the About page has no further responsibility for the booking logic.

Workflow: User navigates to Case Studies from About
1. User scrolls to or focuses a section where the 'View Client Case Studies' CTA is visible in the about-cta-panel.
2. On click, the event handler determines the current section parameter from the URL.
3. The handler builds a navigation URL for case_studies.html with source=about and optionally section=<current section> (e.g., case_studies.html?source=about&section=industries).
4. Browser navigates to case_studies.html with the context parameters preserved.

Workflow: User navigates to Contact from About
1. User is on about.html and clicks 'Contact Us' CTA.
2. The click handler reads current URL parameters (ref, section, or others if present).
3. The handler constructs contact.html?source=about&section=<current section if any>.
4. Browser navigates to contact.html; no form submission occurs on the About page.

Workflow: User deep-links into a specific About section
1. User opens a link such as about.html?section=industries&ref=assessment.
2. The page controller parses URL parameters on load.
3. getAboutPageContent is called and content is loaded.
4. Once data is available, the section-manager locates the industries section component and sets it as active, scrolling into view and updating any in-page navigation highlight.
5. If the user clicks a different section from the in-page navigation, the section parameter is updated in the URL (e.g., ?section=team&ref=assessment) using history API without a full reload.

Workflow: Handling getAboutPageContent loading or error
1. On about.html load, about-content-loader triggers getAboutPageContent.
2. The loader sets an internal state to 'loading', which can be used by sections to show loading placeholders instead of empty content.
3. If the call succeeds, state is set to 'loaded' and the response object is distributed to child components.
4. If the call fails, state is set to 'error'; the page may show a generic error message and hide content sections that rely on the data (no static fallback content is injected).
interactions: On page load (about.html with optional URL parameters): parse section and ref parameters; call getAboutPageContent; on success, render all available sections and auto-focus the section indicated by the section parameter if present.

On URL change via browser back/forward (popstate): re-parse the section parameter and update which section is active/visible/focused without re-calling getAboutPageContent (content remains cached in the page state).

On click of an in-page navigation item (e.g., Mission, Values, Team, Industries, Credentials): update the section parameter in the URL using history.pushState, then scroll to the corresponding section component and set it as active.
On click of 'Book a Consultation' CTA: read current section and ref from the URL and navigate to book_consultation.html with those parameters plus source=about.
On click of 'View Client Case Studies' CTA: read current section and ref from the URL and navigate to case_studies.html with those parameters plus source=about.
On click of 'Contact Us' CTA: read current section and ref from the URL and navigate to contact.html with those parameters plus source=about.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is controlled using URL parameters and in-memory page state. The primary URL parameter is section, which indicates which content section should be treated as active (e.g., section=mission, section=values, section=team, section=industries, section=credentials). On initial load, the page reads section from location.search; after getAboutPageContent returns, section-manager scrolls to and highlights the matching section if it exists. If section is absent or does not correspond to a known section, a default section is used without modifying the URL. A secondary URL parameter ref is treated as a context/source indicator (e.g., ref=nav, ref=footer, or values added by other pages); the About page reads but does not modify ref, and simply forwards it when navigating to other pages. All outbound navigation CTAs (to book_consultation.html, case_studies.html, and contact.html) append source=about and propagate existing URL parameters (such as section and ref) to maintain context. History API is used when clicking in-page navigation to update the section parameter without a full reload, so users can use browser back/forward to navigate between sections. The data from getAboutPageContent is not encoded in the URL; it lives only in in-memory state managed by the page controller.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page, parses URL parameters, triggers getAboutPageContent, manages global loading/error state, and coordinates which section is active based on the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit (parse URL parameters, call getAboutPageContent)
onGetAboutPageContentSuccess (store mission, values, teamSummary, industriesServed, certificationsAndPartnerships, notableAchievements in page state and notify child components)
onGetAboutPageContentError (set error state for child components)
onUrlChange (e.g., popstate: re-read section parameter and notify section-manager)

components.functionality: Encapsulates the call to getAboutPageContent and exposes loading, loaded, and error states as well as the returned content object to child components.
event_handlers: onInit (invoke getAboutPageContent)
onRetryClick (optional handler to retry getAboutPageContent if an error occurred)

components.functionality: Provides a set of internal navigation links to jump between sections (e.g., mission, values, team, industries, credentials). Keeps the active link in sync with the current section URL parameter and triggers URL updates when users select a different section.
event_handlers: onClickNavItem (update section parameter in URL via history.pushState and request scroll to target section)
onSectionChange (receive active section changes from about-page-controller and update visual active state)

components.functionality: Displays the mission text returned by getAboutPageContent and registers itself with the section-manager so it can be targeted by the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate (re-render when mission value changes in page state)
onSectionFocus (scroll into view or highlight when section=mission is active)

components.functionality: Displays the list of values returned in getAboutPageContent.values and allows them to be read as discrete items (e.g., as a list). Registers under section=values for navigation.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate (re-render when values array changes in page state)
onSectionFocus (scroll into view or highlight when section=values is active)

components.functionality: Shows the overall teamSummary from getAboutPageContent, giving an overview of the team’s experience and typical client profiles. Registers under section=team for navigation.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate (re-render when teamSummary changes in page state)
onSectionFocus (scroll into view or highlight when section=team is active)

components.functionality: Displays the industriesServed array from getAboutPageContent as a list of industries in which the firm operates. Registers under section=industries for navigation.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate (re-render when industriesServed changes in page state)
onSectionFocus (scroll into view or highlight when section=industries is active)

components.functionality: Displays credentials-related information using certificationsAndPartnerships and notableAchievements from getAboutPageContent. Registers under section=credentials for navigation.
event_handlers: onContentUpdate (re-render when certificationsAndPartnerships or notableAchievements change in page state)
onSectionFocus (scroll into view or highlight when section=credentials is active)

components.functionality: Maintains a registry of section components (mission, values, team, industries, credentials), interprets the section URL parameter, and coordinates which section is active and should be scrolled into view.
event_handlers: onInit (register sections as they mount, read initial section parameter)
onUrlSectionChange (when section parameter changes, determine matching section and emit onSectionFocus to the appropriate component)
onScroll (optional: detect which section is currently in view and request about-page-controller to update the section parameter accordingly)

components.functionality: Renders functional CTAs for 'Book a Consultation', 'View Client Case Studies', and 'Contact Us', and handles navigation to the corresponding pages while preserving and/or augmenting URL parameters for context.
event_handlers: onClickBookConsultation (read current section and ref parameters; navigate to book_consultation.html?source=about&section=<currentSection>&ref=<refIfPresent>)
onClickViewCaseStudies (read current section and ref parameters; navigate to case_studies.html?source=about&section=<currentSection>&ref=<refIfPresent>)
onClickContactUs (read current section and ref parameters; navigate to contact.html?source=about&section=<currentSection>&ref=<refIfPresent>)

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page uses a hero-like first section for the mission that mirrors the homepage hero: a text-dominant left column and a supporting right column, all on a dark, high-contrast background. Directly beneath the global site header, a horizontal in-page navigation bar lets users jump between Mission, Values, Team, Industries, Credentials, and the closing CTA. Below the hero-style mission area, the remaining sections are stacked vertically in a clean, editorial flow. Each major section follows a two-column pattern on desktop (text lead on the left, supporting lists/cards/visuals on the right), with sections clearly segmented and titled.
