On smaller screens, these columns collapse into a single vertical stack with the narrative text coming first.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page uses a hero-like first section for the mission that mirrors the homepage hero: a text-dominant left column and a supporting right column, all on a dark, high-contrast background. Directly beneath the global site header, a horizontal in-page navigation bar lets users jump between Mission, Values, Team, Industries, Credentials, and the closing CTA. Below the hero-style mission area, the remaining sections are stacked vertically in a clean, editorial flow. Each major section follows a two-column pattern on desktop (text lead on the left, supporting lists/cards/visuals on the right), with sections clearly segmented and titled. On smaller screens, these columns collapse into a single vertical stack with the narrative text coming first.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page uses a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) on a 12-column grid with generous horizontal padding (24–32px on each side). On desktop, primary text columns generally occupy about 60–65% of the width (7–8 columns), while secondary content (lists, cards, stats, or visuals) occupies 35–40% (4–5 columns). The hero-style mission section consumes most of the initial viewport height with 80–96px top and bottom padding, while subsequent sections use 64–96px vertical spacing between them. Full-width strips (in-page nav and final CTA panel) run edge-to-edge of the viewport, with their internal content still constrained to the central max-width container.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and highly scannable. Each section has a clearly separated title, short introduction, and then supporting elements (cards, lists, summaries) with generous spacing (24–32px) between clusters. No dense tables are used; instead, industries and credentials are displayed as structured lists or cards with ample breathing room. The dark background, large typography, and consistent use of white space echo the modern, minimalist feel of the mockup, making the page feel editorial rather than document-like.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top with the brand header (outside this scope), then immediately into the in-page navigation and mission hero. Users' eyes are drawn first to the large mission headline on the left, then down through the mission copy and into the next sections via the sticky in-page navigation that remains visible as they scroll. Each section continues the left-to-right reading pattern: left column establishes context (section label, headline, brief explanation), then the eye moves right to explore supporting content (values cards, team highlights, industry list, credentials grid). The final CTA band at the bottom provides a strong, high-contrast endpoint, pulling attention back to a focused action after the more informative content above.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits on a dark, modern canvas that echoes the homepage hero: a charcoal backdrop, bright white type, and restrained accent colors. Just below the global header, a slim horizontal in-page navigation strip spans the width of the screen, anchored to the top as you scroll to keep orientation and quick jumping between sections. The first visible content block is a hero-like Mission section that fills most of the initial viewport, with a large, confident mission statement on the left and a slim right column for supporting stats or a subtle visual—mirroring the two-column hero pattern of the mockup. As you scroll, sections stack in a calm vertical rhythm: Values, Team, Industries, and Credentials, each occupying a distinct horizontal band with ample top and bottom breathing room and a consistent two-column grid. Titles and key messages always anchor on the left, while the right column carries cards, bullet groups, or highlight blocks that balance the layout visually without feeling crowded. At the very bottom, a full-width CTA panel with a slightly brighter or accented background creates a strong closing moment, inviting visitors to initiate contact or schedule a consultation, completing a journey that flows naturally from high-level mission through proof and credibility to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-controller acts as the structural canvas for the entire About page, sitting directly beneath the global site header and spanning the full viewport width and height. It establishes the dark-mode foundation with a charcoal-tinted background at the top that can gradually transition to slightly lighter neutrals as the user scrolls down through sections. Inside, it centers all main content within a max-width container of roughly 1100–1200px, aligned to a 12-column grid, with 24–32px padding on the left and right to protect content on large screens. Vertically, it ensures that the first visible mission section can expand to fill approximately 70–80% of the viewport height, maintaining a dramatic hero-like presence, while subsequent sections occupy stacked horizontal bands with consistent 64–96px spacing between them. All other components are rendered within this controller, which orchestrates their order and ensures a clear, linear top-to-bottom reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a root scrolling container for the page content with standard document flow; it is non-sticky and does not overlay other elements but defines the overall background and max-width constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is an invisible technical layer positioned at the very top of the main content area inside the about-page-controller, just beneath the global header and above the in-page navigation. It occupies effectively zero visual space and does not interrupt the visual flow, but conceptually aligns to the top-left corner of the content grid, ensuring that mission, values, team, industries, and credentials data are fetched before their respective sections are populated. The layout of visible elements is unaffected by this component; sections will either render with loading states within their own areas or appear once content is ready, all within the established centered container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-positioned; participates in the DOM for data lifecycle only and does not directly consume or alter spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-navigation is a horizontally oriented in-page navigation bar that sits immediately below the global site header and just above the mission hero section. On desktop, it spans the full width of the viewport as a slim band (about 56–64px tall), with its content constrained inside the central max-width container. Within that container, section labels such as Mission, Values, Team, Industries, Credentials, and Contact/CTA are arranged left-to-right in a single row, aligned to the left edge of the content grid, with 24–32px horizontal spacing between each item. The active section is indicated by a high-contrast underline or pill background that mirrors the primary CTA styling from the hero. The bar itself sits on a slightly darker or subtly separated charcoal strip to distinguish it from the main background, reinforcing its utility role. On scroll, this navigation becomes sticky: it pins to the top of the viewport just beneath the global header, maintaining its full-width presence and preserving 8–16px vertical breathing space above the mission section content that scrolls underneath. On tablet and mobile, the nav remains full-width but may allow horizontal scrolling for the items, with labels rendered as compact pills that still follow the same alignment and spacing logic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky positioning: initially sits in normal flow between the header and mission section, then affixes to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls, always remaining just beneath the main site header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-mission-section is the hero-style band of the About page and appears directly beneath the in-page navigation bar. It spans the full viewport width, with a rich charcoal background that matches the site’s dark hero style, and occupies roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Inside the centered max-width container, the content is organized into a two-column layout aligned to the 12-column grid: the left content column takes about 7–8 columns (around 60–65% of the container width), and the right supporting column occupies the remaining 4–5 columns. The left column forms a clear vertical stack: a small all-caps label like “ABOUT” or “OUR MISSION” at the top, followed by a large, multi-line H1 mission statement that visually dominates the section, then a short 2–3 line supporting paragraph elaborating on the consulting firm’s purpose and AI/business strategy focus. Each of these blocks is separated by generous vertical spacing (24–32px), creating a calm, linear reading path. The right column is vertically centered relative to the main headline block and contains supporting elements such as a compact set of metrics (e.g., years of experience, industries served) or a subtle abstract visual related to AI and strategy; these elements form a tight vertical stack with moderate spacing, ensuring they complement rather than compete with the mission text. Top and bottom padding around the content is generous (80–96px), so the hero feels spacious and balanced, with the text-heavy left side naturally drawing the eye first, then flowing across to the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal page flow, expanding to accommodate its content and maintaining its relative height dominance at the top of the page; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-values-section is the first major content band following the mission hero and is separated from it by a substantial vertical gap of roughly 64–80px, keeping the editorial, low-density feel. It spans the full width of the viewport but may subtly shift to a slightly lighter dark background tone to differentiate it from the mission area while preserving the overall dark-mode aesthetic. Inside the central container, the layout again uses a two-column structure but with the visual weight slightly more balanced. On desktop, the left column occupies about 5–6 of 12 columns (~40–50% width) and holds the section label (“VALUES”), a medium-sized headline, and a concise explanatory paragraph; these are vertically stacked with 16–24px spacing, aligned left, and sit near the top of the section to anchor the user’s understanding. The right column spans the remaining 6–7 columns and presents the firm’s core values as a grid of cards or articulated points: typically 2 columns of 2–3 cards each, with 24–32px vertical and horizontal gaps. Each value card has a short title and 1–2 lines of supporting text, with sufficient padding (12–16px internal) and breathing space around them. The section has 64–80px padding at the top and bottom, so the card grid feels airy and organized, encouraging the eye to move from the left explanatory text to the right grid and then down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked below the mission section, expanding vertically as needed to accommodate the number of value cards while preserving generous top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-team-summary-section appears directly below the values section, separated by approximately 64–72px of vertical space to maintain the rhythm of distinct content bands. It may share a similar background tone with the values section or shift slightly to create a subtle alternation while still staying within the dark, muted palette. Inside the centered container, it follows a text-led two-column layout. The left column spans roughly 7 of 12 columns (around 60% width) and contains a section label (e.g., “TEAM”), a bold but smaller-than-hero headline that frames the team narrative, and a 2–3 paragraph overview describing the composition, expertise, and approach of the consulting team. These text elements are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing, creating a narrative block that reads smoothly top-to-bottom. The right column occupies about 5 columns and serves as a visual summary: this might be a vertical stack of 2–3 key roles with names and short descriptions, small circular portrait thumbnails, or a compact stat cluster (e.g., number of consultants, average years of experience). These elements are grouped with 16–24px spacing and aligned to the top of the right column, creating a counterbalance to the text-heavy left side without appearing cluttered. The overall section height remains generous, with about 64–80px padding above and below the content, so the team overview feels substantial but still airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participating in normal vertical stacking, taking enough height to comfortably fit text and any team highlight elements without crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-industries-served-section sits beneath the team summary, separated by a consistent vertical gap of around 64px, reinforcing the sense of modular content bands. The background can either continue the current tone or adjust slightly (for example, a very dark slate) to give this section its own identity while keeping contrast high for text. Within the central container, it uses a layout where the supporting content on the right is slightly emphasized. The left column takes approximately 4–5 of 12 columns (~35–40% width) and holds the section label (“INDUSTRIES”), a focused headline, and a compact explanatory paragraph outlining the breadth of industries served. The right column spans 7–8 columns and is dedicated to a structured display of industries—most likely a two-column list or grid of industry names rendered as simple text rows or subtle chips. Items are grouped with 12–16px vertical spacing and 24px column separation, arranged to form a light, easily scannable block rather than a dense table. The list is aligned to the top of the right column, so the user naturally transitions from the left explanation into the right-hand list and down through the industries. The section’s top and bottom padding (64–80px) ensures the list has enough breathing room even if many industries are displayed, preserving the minimalist, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow, expanding vertically as required to accommodate the industries list while maintaining consistent padding and spacing within the section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-credentials-section functions as a grouped evidence band near the lower portion of the page, following the industries section with a 64–80px vertical gap. To subtly elevate its importance and differentiate it from the sections above, the background may shift to a slightly lighter dark neutral or a desaturated accent-tinted dark, while still maintaining a cohesive dark-mode aesthetic. Inside the max-width container, the section opens with a left-aligned heading block spanning roughly 6–7 of 12 columns (~50–60% width): a label such as “CREDENTIALS,” a strong but compact headline, and a short paragraph framing the types of credentials (certifications, partnerships, speaking, publications). Immediately below this heading block, the section expands into a multi-row grid of credential cards that stretches across the full content width. On desktop, each card typically occupies 3–4 columns (so 3 or 4 cards per row depending on content), with 24px horizontal and vertical gaps defining clear separation. Each credential card has a concise title (e.g., “Certified AI Practitioner,” “Fortune 500 Clients,” “Keynote Speaker at X”), possibly an icon, and a short one- or two-line description. Cards use 12–16px internal padding and align to the same 8px baseline grid, maintaining visual clarity. The section’s overall vertical padding is generous (80–96px top and bottom), allowing several rows of cards to appear without feeling crowded, so the user can scan rightward and downward through credentials after absorbing the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, with height determined by the number of credential cards; remains a clearly separated band in the vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-manager is an invisible orchestrator for the About page sections, conceptually spanning the vertical range from the mission section through the credentials and CTA. It does not present any visual UI of its own, but it observes the scroll position of each major section (Mission, Values, Team, Industries, Credentials, CTA) and coordinates with the about-section-navigation to update the active state. Spatially, it exists alongside other components within the about-page-controller but occupies no additional height or width; it simply references the positional data of the visible sections. Its presence allows smooth scrolling to sections when nav items are clicked and ensures that the correct nav item is highlighted as the user scrolls through the content bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that does not influence physical layout; interacts with scroll behavior and section anchors without occupying space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-cta-panel is the final visual band on the About page, appearing directly beneath the credentials section with a slightly larger vertical separation of about 80–96px to emphasize its role as a closing moment. It spans the full width of the viewport and uses a high-contrast background—either a brighter charcoal or a restrained accent color that still feels corporate and modern—setting it apart from the preceding sections. Inside the central max-width container, the CTA content follows the established text-left, support-right pattern. On desktop, the left column covers about 7 of 12 columns (~60% width) and includes a compact all-caps label (e.g., “NEXT STEP”), a bold, concise headline inviting users to engage (such as booking a consultation or contacting the team), and a brief supporting sentence or two explaining the value of reaching out. These elements are stacked with 16–24px vertical spacing. The right column occupies roughly 5 columns and centers one or two prominent call-to-action elements: a primary button styled consistently with the site’s main CTAs and optionally a secondary text link (e.g., “View Services”) placed below with 8–12px spacing. The section’s vertical padding (72–96px top and bottom) ensures the CTA feels substantial and focused without being overwhelming, and it aligns visually with the end of the page’s Z-pattern flow—after scanning credentials, the eye naturally lands on this final, action-oriented band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the bottom of the About page content stack; it does not stick or float but serves as the natural end-point of the scroll.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for submitting general inquiries and accessing alternative contact options or specialized request forms.

core_features: Display a general contact form that collects name, email, company, topic, and a free-text message.
Automatically initialize the contact topic based on the optional URL parameter `topic` when present.
Submit the contact form via the `submitContactForm` interface and handle success or error responses.
Provide links to specialized pages for structured requests, including consultation booking, project estimation, and proposal requests.
Show alternative contact information for users who prefer direct contact methods instead of the form.
user_workflows: General inquiry via direct navigation: 1) User opens contact.html without URL parameters. 2) Page loads with an empty contact form and neutral default topic state. 3) User fills in name, email, company (optional), selects or leaves topic, and enters their message. 4) User submits the form. 5) The page calls `submitContactForm` with the form values. 6) If the call succeeds, the page displays a confirmation including the returned ticket identifier and keeps the entered data read-only or cleared according to configuration. 7) If the call fails, an error status is shown and the user can edit fields and resubmit.

Topic-prefilled inquiry from other pages: 1) User clicks a generic contact link on index.html or about.html that navigates to contact.html with a `topic` URL parameter. 2) On load, the Contact page reads the `topic` parameter. 3) The topic selector in the contact form is initialized to match the URL topic value. 4) User reviews or adjusts the pre-selected topic, completes remaining fields, and submits. 5) The page sends the selected topic (which may differ from the initial parameter) to `submitContactForm`. 6) Upon success, a confirmation message referencing the chosen topic and ticket identifier is displayed.

Redirect to specialized forms when needed: 1) User arrives on contact.html (with or without `topic`). 2) While considering what to submit, the user reads brief guidance near the specialized-form links indicating when to use each. 3) If the user decides a structured consultation, estimation, or proposal is more appropriate, they click the corresponding link. 4) The page navigates to the selected target (book_consultation.html, estimator.html, or proposal_request.html) without submitting the general contact form.

Using alternative contact methods: 1) User arrives on contact.html and prefers not to complete the form. 2) User views the alternative contact information component, which exposes configured channels (e.g., email addresses, phone, or office locations) without hardcoded values in the page. 3) User uses those channels outside the website or copies details as needed. 4) No `submitContactForm` call is made in this workflow.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the `topic` URL parameter, if present, and emits an initialization event that updates the contact form topic field.

When the user changes any form field (name, email, company, topic, message), the contact form component updates its internal state accordingly and clears any previous error messages specific to that field.

When the user clicks the form submit button, the contact form component validates required fields on the client side; if validation passes, it triggers the `submitContactForm` interface with the current field values and enters a submitting/loading state.

When `submitContactForm` resolves successfully, the page receives `success`, `message`, and `ticketId`, exits the submitting state, and the submission-status component displays a success notification using the returned values.

When `submitContactForm` returns an error or the request fails, the page exits the submitting state, shows an error notification via the status component, and keeps the form data so the user can correct and resubmit.
When the user clicks the 'Book a Consultation' link, the specialized-forms-navigation component triggers a navigation to book_consultation.html.
When the user clicks the 'Get a Project Estimate' link, the specialized-forms-navigation component triggers a navigation to estimator.html.
When the user clicks the 'Request a Proposal' link, the specialized-forms-navigation component triggers a navigation to proposal_request.html.

When the user interacts with alternative contact channels (e.g., clicking a mailto link or copying an address), those actions are handled by the browser or environment; no `submitContactForm` call is made.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state is the optional URL parameter `topic`. On initial page load, a controller component reads window.location and parses the `topic` query parameter. If a value is present, it sets the initial topic state in the contact form component; if not, the form topic remains in its neutral default state. The URL parameter is read-only in this context; the page does not rewrite the URL when the user changes the topic field. The contact form manages internal UI states: idle (form not submitting and no recent result), submitting (form disabled while `submitContactForm` is in progress), success (a successful response has been received and the status component shows the success message and ticket identifier), and error (an error occurred and the status component shows an error message). These states are stored in memory at runtime and are not persisted in URL parameters. Navigation to specialized pages (book_consultation.html, estimator.html, proposal_request.html) is performed via standard link navigation without adding additional URL parameters from this page. The `submitContactForm` interface is the only external call, and its response directly updates the submission-status component state without modifying the URL.

components.functionality: Reads the `topic` URL parameter on page load and exposes the initial topic value to other components, primarily the contact form. It ensures that any incoming `topic` parameter from index.html or about.html is reflected in the form's topic field.
event_handlers: onLoad
onURLParamChange

components.functionality: Collects user input for a general inquiry, including name, email, company, topic, and message. It accepts an initial topic value from the topic-url-initializer, allows the user to change it, validates required fields, and submits the data using the `submitContactForm` interface.
event_handlers: onFieldChange
onSubmit
onValidationError
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitFailure

components.functionality: Displays the current submission state of the contact form. It shows loading state while the form is submitting, success messages and ticket identifiers when `submitContactForm` returns a successful response, and error messages when the submission fails.
event_handlers: onSubmitStart
onSubmitSuccess
onSubmitFailure
onDismissStatus

components.functionality: Provides navigation links from the general Contact page to specialized request flows for booking a consultation, getting a project estimate, or requesting a proposal. Clicking a link navigates the user to the corresponding page without invoking `submitContactForm`.
event_handlers: onClickBookConsultation
onClickEstimator
onClickProposalRequest

components.functionality: Shows non-form contact channels such as configured email addresses, phone numbers, or office locations sourced from runtime configuration or backend data, enabling users to reach out without using the `submitContactForm` interface.
event_handlers: onClickExternalContactLink
onCopyContactDetail

components.functionality: Coordinates the arrangement and interaction between the topic-url-initializer, contact-form, submission-status-banner, specialized-forms-navigation, and alternative-contact-info components. It ensures that the form receives the initial topic state and that status updates are displayed appropriately.
event_handlers: onLoad
onComponentStateChange

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The contact page borrows the hero-like two-column structure from the mockup. The left column becomes the primary interaction zone with a linear top-to-bottom flow: 1) submission-status-banner (when present), 2) a compact topic-url-initializer control, 3) the main contact-form. The right column acts as the counterpart to the portrait in the original hero, holding supporting but important contact options: specialized-forms-navigation at the top and alternative-contact-info below it. All of this sits within a centered max-width container beneath the existing global navigation.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The contact page borrows the hero-like two-column structure from the mockup. The left column becomes the primary interaction zone with a linear top-to-bottom flow: 1) submission-status-banner (when present), 2) a compact topic-url-initializer control, 3) the main contact-form. The right column acts as the counterpart to the portrait in the original hero, holding supporting but important contact options: specialized-forms-navigation at the top and alternative-contact-info below it. All of this sits within a centered max-width container beneath the existing global navigation.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A 12-column grid is used, with the main content container centered at ~1100–1200px max-width and padded by 24–32px on large screens. The left functional column occupies roughly 7–8 of 12 columns (~60–65% width) and the right supporting column uses 4–5 columns (~35–40%). Vertically, the main contact region roughly fills at least the first viewport height, with 64–96px of top padding below the nav and similar bottom breathing room before any footer. Each component stack follows the generous vertical spacing from the mockup, with clear gaps separating banner, controls, form, and supporting cards.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm, even though this is a form-heavy page. Form fields are grouped with 16–24px vertical spacing, and each major component block (banner, initializer, form, specialized links, alternative info) is separated by 32–48px. Text lines remain short to medium due to the column widths, and the dark background with high-contrast typography preserves the editorial, modern feel without clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mimics the mockup’s Z-pattern. After the global header, the eye lands first on the left column—specifically, the submission-status-banner if active, then the topic-url-initializer, then the contact-form fields and primary submit action. From there, attention is pulled horizontally to the right column, first to the specialized-forms-navigation (as the key secondary path), then downward to the alternative-contact-info for direct channels. On smaller screens, this flow becomes a clean vertical stack in the same logical order, maintaining the top-to-bottom narrative: status, controls, main form, specialized forms, alternate info.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the dark, slim global navigation, the contact page opens into a full-width, charcoal-toned section that feels like a focused, form-centric hero. A centered content container, about 1100–1200px wide, is split into a generous two-column layout: the left two-thirds is a calm, vertically stacked sequence of status feedback, a compact topic selector, and the main contact form, while the right third forms a tall supporting column of clean cards for specialized form links and alternative contact methods. Generous 64–96px padding at the top and bottom keeps the page airy, while 32–48px gaps between component blocks echo the editorial spaciousness of the original mockup. The eye moves naturally from the primary interaction zone on the left to the secondary options on the right, maintaining the modern, minimalist, consulting-oriented aesthetic in a way that makes the act of reaching out feel deliberate and frictionless.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The topic-url-initializer sits in the left main column, just below the submission-status-banner (if visible) and above the contact-form. It aligns flush with the left edge of the content container and uses roughly 60–65% of the total page width (spanning about 7–8 columns of the 12-column grid). Visually, it appears as a compact horizontal control bar or small card with 16–24px internal padding, possibly containing a topic dropdown and optional URL field. There is a clear 24–32px vertical gap above and below it, separating it from the status banner and the first form field. On larger screens, it does not extend full-width across the page, maintaining the same left column alignment as the form; on smaller screens (tablet and below) it becomes a full-width row within the stacked flow, still directly preceding the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that participates in the normal document flow; it expands vertically as needed to fit controls but maintains a single-row, compact appearance where possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the dominant element in the left column, positioned immediately below the topic-url-initializer with a 24–32px gap. It spans the full width of the left column (about 60–65% of the total page width, or 7–8 grid columns) and is vertically generous, easily extending beyond the initial viewport if there are multiple fields. Inside the form, fields are arranged in a clean single-column stack, with optional two-column rows for shorter fields on desktop while still respecting the 12-column grid. Vertical spacing of 16–24px between fields and 32px before the primary submit button maintains the airy, low-density feel. The form’s top is roughly one-third down from the top of the viewport once the global header and top padding are accounted for, making it feel like the main hero content but interaction-focused. On mobile, the form becomes full-width, centered with 16–24px side padding, and remains the primary block the user scrolls through.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content: the form defines the primary scroll height of the page and does not use sticky or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner appears at the very top of the main content region within the central container, directly beneath the global header and the section’s top padding. It spans the full width of the content container, cutting across both the left and right columns visually but with its text aligned to the left, mirroring the way a slim notification bar might sit above a hero. The banner is relatively shallow—roughly 48–64px tall—with 16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, maintaining the 8px grid, and leaving 24–32px of whitespace below it before the topic-url-initializer and left column content begin. When hidden, the layout simply collapses this space, pulling the left and right column content up. On mobile, the banner still spans full width and sits as the first element users see after the nav, preserving its role as immediate feedback.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in the normal flow; when visible it pushes all content below it downward. No fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The specialized-forms-navigation occupies the upper portion of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top region of the contact-form but offset to the right. It spans roughly 35–40% of the total page width (about 4–5 grid columns), beginning at the same top baseline as the topic-url-initializer or slightly below the status banner’s lower edge. It is presented as a clean card or stacked list of links with 24–32px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between individual links. The card has ample whitespace around it, with a 32–48px gap below before the alternative-contact-info card. Its visual weight balances the left column’s form: strong headings and clear link labels, but less typographic dominance than the form’s main heading and submit button. On smaller screens, this component drops below the contact-form, becoming a full-width card with preserved spacing above and below, following the same linear reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the right column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The alternative-contact-info block sits directly beneath the specialized-forms-navigation within the right column, separated by 32–48px of vertical space to keep the page airy. It shares the same right-column width (about 35–40% of the total page width) and is styled as a slightly more understated card: concise text lines describing email, social, or other channels, with small icons aligned left. Internal padding is 24px on all sides, and individual contact lines are spaced at 12–16px to maintain low density. The top of this card typically aligns roughly with the midsection of the contact-form, so as the user scrolls through the form, the eye can drift right to discover alternative channels. On mobile, this block appears below the specialized-forms-navigation, both now full-width cards stacked with 24–32px gaps between them and any content above/below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the right-column stack; no fixed or sticky behavior, but it naturally comes into view as users scroll down the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-layout-manager is responsible for creating the overall hero-like contact section beneath the global navigation. It establishes a dark, full-width background band and centers a max-width content container (~1100–1200px) with 24–32px left/right padding and 64–96px top/bottom padding. Inside this container, it divides the space into a responsive two-column grid on desktop: the left column spanning about 7–8 of 12 columns for the submission-status-banner alignment, topic-url-initializer, and contact-form, and the right column spanning about 4–5 of 12 columns for the specialized-forms-navigation and alternative-contact-info. Vertical rhythm is enforced with consistent 8px-based spacing between components (24–48px gaps), ensuring each block has clear separation. On tablet and mobile breakpoints, the layout-manager collapses these into a single-column stack, preserving the logical order—status banner (if present), topic initializer, contact form, specialized forms, alternative contact—while keeping generous whitespace so the page still feels like a spacious hero rather than a dense form page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the responsive grid and stacking behavior for all child components; remains static under the global header and above the site footer, without fixed or sticky positioning, but controls how components reflow across breakpoints.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Displays the dynamically loaded privacy policy, including data collection, usage, storage, and tracking practices, using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface with support for section navigation and deep links.

core_features: Retrieve the current privacy policy content (last updated date and sections) via getPrivacyPolicyContent when the page loads.
Render the full privacy policy as a list of sections, each with a title and body, using the sections returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Display the last updated date of the privacy policy based on the lastUpdated value from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Provide a table of contents that is generated from the dynamic section list and allows users to jump to specific sections.
Support deep linking to specific sections using a URL parameter that corresponds to a section id from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
Support an optional policy text search/filter using only the loaded sections in memory, with the current search query synchronized to a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Read the full privacy policy:
1. User opens privacy.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. Page initialization triggers a call to getPrivacyPolicyContent.
3. While loading, the page shows a loading state (e.g., a generic loading indicator managed by the frontend layer).
4. On success, the lastUpdated value and sections array are stored in page state.
5. The page header shows the last updated information.
6. The table of contents is generated from sections (using each section.id and section.title).
7. The main content area renders each section.title and section.body in order.
8. User scrolls through the content to read details about data collection, storage, usage, protection, cookies, tracking, and privacy contacts, as provided in the dynamic policy content.

Navigate to a specific section from the table of contents:
1. After the policy is loaded, the table of contents shows a list of section titles based on sections from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
2. User clicks a section entry in the table of contents.
3. The click handler updates the URL to include or replace the section URL parameter with the corresponding section.id (e.g., privacy.html?section={section.id} plus any other existing parameters preserved).
4. The page scrolls to the section whose identifier matches the clicked section.id.
5. The clicked section is marked as active in the table of contents, based on the section URL parameter.
6. If the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the page listens for URL changes and updates the active section highlight and scroll position accordingly.

Open a deep-linked section directly via URL:
1. User opens privacy.html with a section parameter in the URL (e.g., privacy.html?section={some-id}).
2. On page initialization, before or in parallel with calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, the page parses URL parameters and stores the section parameter value in transient state.
3. Once getPrivacyPolicyContent returns, the page searches the sections array for a section whose id matches the section parameter value.
4. If a matching section is found, the page scrolls to that section and marks it as active in the table of contents.
5. If no matching section is found, the page does not change scroll position and treats the view as a normal full-policy view without active section highlighting.

Search within the privacy policy text:
1. User opens privacy.html, and the page loads policy content via getPrivacyPolicyContent as usual.
2. User enters a search query into a search input field dedicated to the privacy policy.
3. On each change to the search input, the page updates a q URL parameter with the current query value.
4. The policy sections viewer filters or highlights sections locally in memory based on the query, using section.title and section.body content (no additional API calls).
5. If the user shares a URL with a q parameter (e.g., privacy.html?q={query}), another user opening that URL sees the search input prefilled with the query value and the same filtered/highlighted view applied after content is loaded.
interactions: Page load: On initial load of privacy.html, the page triggers getPrivacyPolicyContent and sets internal loading/error/success states based on the result.
URL parameter parsing: On load and on browser navigation events, the page parses section and q URL parameters and updates the active section and search query state accordingly.

Table of contents click: When a user clicks on a section in the table of contents, the page updates the section URL parameter to the clicked section.id, updates the active section highlight, and scrolls to the corresponding section block.
Section deep link click: If section titles in the content area are rendered as anchors, clicking a section title also updates the section URL parameter to that section.id and scrolls accordingly.
Search input change: When the user types in the search input, the page updates a q URL parameter and filters/highlights sections in the viewer based on the current query.

Error handling interaction: If getPrivacyPolicyContent fails, the page displays a generic error state controlled by the frontend (for example, a generic error message component) and may expose a retry action that triggers getPrivacyPolicyContent again.

Scrolling interaction: As the user scrolls the policy content, an optional scroll handler can detect which section is currently in view and update the section URL parameter and active table of contents entry to match the visible section.id.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is coordinated with the URL using query parameters to allow deep linking and preserving user context.

URL parameters:
- section: Optional string. Represents the id of a section returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent.sections[].id.
  - On page load:
    - Parse the section parameter value if present and store it as desiredActiveSectionId.
    - After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves and sections are available, search for a section whose id matches desiredActiveSectionId.
    - If found, scroll to that section, mark it as active in both the sections viewer and the table of contents, and ensure the URL keeps section={that-id}.
    - If not found, clear desiredActiveSectionId in internal state (but do not alter the URL automatically unless needed by the implementation) and present the default full-policy view.
  - On user interaction (click in table of contents or clicking a section title anchor):
    - Update the section parameter in the URL via history APIs without reloading the page.
    - Update the active section state and scroll into view the corresponding section.
  - On browser navigation (back/forward):
    - Listen for URL changes and re-apply the above logic, updating the active section and scroll position based on the new section value.

- q: Optional string. Represents the current search query for filtering/highlighting within the policy text.
  - On page load:
    - Parse the q parameter value if present and set the search component's input value to this query.
    - After getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, apply the search filter/highlighting to sections based on q.
  - On user typing in the search input:
    - Update the q parameter in the URL using history APIs (add or remove it depending on whether the query is empty).
    - Recalculate the filtered/highlighted view of sections in memory, without additional API calls.
  - On browser navigation (back/forward):
    - Re-parse the q parameter and update the search input and filtered/highlighted state accordingly.

Generic state considerations:
- The page maintains a loading state and an error state tied exclusively to the getPrivacyPolicyContent lifecycle.
- All rendered policy content, including lastUpdated and sections, is taken directly from the getPrivacyPolicyContent result; no constant or hardcoded policy text is used.
- No localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms are used to persist state; the URL is the single source of truth for sharable state (active section and search query), and all content comes from getPrivacyPolicyContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getPrivacyPolicyContent, manages loading and error states, and exposes the lastUpdated and sections data to other components via shared page state or a context mechanism.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when privacy.html is loaded; calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and sets loading state.
onSuccess: Triggered when getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves successfully; stores lastUpdated and sections in page state and clears any previous error.
onError: Triggered when getPrivacyPolicyContent fails; sets an error state that other components can use to render a generic error view.
onRetry: Optional handler triggered by a user action (e.g., clicking a retry control) to call getPrivacyPolicyContent again and reset loading/error state.

components.functionality: Displays the static page title and the dynamically loaded last updated information for the privacy policy.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: Triggered when policy-data-loader updates lastUpdated; re-renders the displayed last updated value.

components.functionality: Renders a list of links representing each policy section based on the dynamic sections array and allows users to jump to any section, while keeping the currently selected section synchronized with the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onSectionClick: Triggered when a user clicks an entry in the table of contents; updates URLParams.section to the clicked section.id and instructs the sections viewer to scroll to that section.

onUrlParamChange: Triggered when the section URL parameter changes (including via browser back/forward); sets the active table of contents entry to the matching sections[].id and, if desired, notifies the sections viewer to adjust scroll position.
onPolicyDataChange: Triggered when policy-data-loader updates sections; regenerates the table of contents entries from the new sections array.

components.functionality: Renders the full privacy policy content by iterating over the sections array, displaying each section title and body, and supports scrolling to specific sections based on the section URL parameter and user clicks in the table of contents.
event_handlers: onPolicyDataChange: Triggered when policy-data-loader updates sections; re-renders all section blocks with their current titles and bodies.
onUrlParamChange: Triggered when section or q URL parameters change; scrolls to the section whose id matches section (if any) and applies or updates search highlighting/filtering using q.

onInViewSectionChange: Triggered by scroll observers as the user scrolls; when a new section enters the primary view, optionally updates URLParams.section to that section.id and informs policy-toc to highlight it.

onSectionTitleClick: Triggered when a user clicks a section title that is rendered as a link; updates URLParams.section to that section.id and scrolls to the corresponding section without reloading the page.

components.functionality: Provides an optional search input for filtering or highlighting the privacy policy sections entirely on the client side, synchronizing the search query with the q URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit: On component initialization, reads URLParams.q and sets the initial search input value; triggers an initial filter/highlight in the sections viewer if q exists.
onInputChange: Triggered when the user types into the search input; updates URLParams.q to the current query value and notifies policy-sections-viewer to re-apply filtering/highlighting.

onClearSearch: Triggered when the user clears the search (e.g., via a clear control); removes or empties URLParams.q and instructs policy-sections-viewer to show all sections without search filtering.
onUrlParamChange: Triggered when the q URL parameter changes due to browser navigation or external updates; updates the search input value and re-applies any necessary filter/highlighting.

components.functionality: Shows a generic error state if loading the privacy policy via getPrivacyPolicyContent fails and optionally provides a way to retry the load.
event_handlers: onErrorStateChange: Triggered when policy-data-loader sets or clears an error; shows or hides the error state accordingly.
onRetryClick: Triggered when the user activates a retry control; calls policy-data-loader.onRetry to invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent again.

components.functionality: Displays a loading state while getPrivacyPolicyContent is in progress and hides it when loading completes or fails.
event_handlers: onLoadingStateChange: Triggered when policy-data-loader updates its loading state; shows the loading indicator when loading is true and hides it when loading is false.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid with a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px), using generous left/right padding for safety on large screens.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a hero-then-content pattern adapted for a legal document. At the very top of the main content, the policy-header behaves like a compact dark-mode hero: small context label, large page title, and brief explanatory copy stacked vertically and centered within the main container. Directly beneath this, a single horizontal band holds the policy-search centered across the page. Below the search, the layout resolves into a two-column structure: the policy-sections-viewer dominates the left column as the primary reading area, while the policy-toc occupies the right column as a structured, vertically stacked navigation panel. The data-loader orchestrates when content appears. The policy-loading-indicator and policy-error-state temporarily replace the sections-viewer area when applicable, keeping feedback aligned with the main reading zone.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a hero-then-content pattern adapted for a legal document. At the very top of the main content, the policy-header behaves like a compact dark-mode hero: small context label, large page title, and brief explanatory copy stacked vertically and centered within the main container. Directly beneath this, a single horizontal band holds the policy-search centered across the page. Below the search, the layout resolves into a two-column structure: the policy-sections-viewer dominates the left column as the primary reading area, while the policy-toc occupies the right column as a structured, vertically stacked navigation panel. The data-loader orchestrates when content appears. The policy-loading-indicator and policy-error-state temporarily replace the sections-viewer area when applicable, keeping feedback aligned with the main reading zone.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main container remains centered with a max-width of about 1100–1200px and generous side padding. The header band (policy-header) spans the full container width but uses ample top and bottom padding (64–80px) to feel like a hero. Beneath it, the search band spans roughly 60% of the container width and is centered horizontally, leaving generous whitespace on the sides. The lower content area is split into two columns: the left column (policy-sections-viewer) uses approximately 60–65% of the horizontal space, while the right column (policy-toc) uses 35–40%. Vertically, major groups (header, search, content columns) are separated by 32–64px gaps, maintaining the spacious, editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page uses low content density, consistent with the mockup: large, readable type, generous line-height, and clear separation between clusters. The policy-header elements (label, title, description) each have 8–24px vertical spacing. The policy-search has 16–24px of padding inside the field and 32–48px of whitespace above and below. The two-column area keeps the text column to a comfortable readable width, with 24–32px padding between the left and right columns. TOC items are spaced with 8–16px vertical gaps, and section headings within the sections-viewer have strong spacing (24–32px) above them to create clear anchors. Loading and error states sit within the same grid, with ample empty space around them, avoiding any clutter when they appear.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the bold page title in the policy-header near the top-left of the container, then quickly registers the smaller context label above it. From there, the gaze moves down to the short supporting text in the header and then continues downward to the centered policy-search bar. After interacting with search, the eye follows the vertical flow down into the main content area, initially drawn to the left column where the policy-sections-viewer begins. As users scroll, the right column (policy-toc) forms a stable vertical reference, drawing the eye rightward when users want to jump sections. The loading and error indicators appear centrally in the left content area, ensuring that state changes are immediately noticeable in the natural reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Privacy Policy page sits on the same dark, charcoal background as the rest of the site, with a centered, max-width content column and high-contrast white typography. Below the global site header, a compact hero-style band introduces the privacy policy: a subtle all-caps label, a large, confident H1, and a short explanatory sentence all stack vertically on the left half of the container, while the right half remains mostly empty, creating a calm, minimalist feel. Beneath this hero band, a single, wide search field is centered horizontally, occupying about three-fifths of the container width and surrounded by generous whitespace, making it feel like the primary interactive tool for exploring the document. Below the search, the layout shifts into a clear two-column system: on the left, a wide, scrollable reading column presents the privacy policy sections in sequence, with generous spacing before each heading; on the right, a slimmer vertical panel lists the table of contents, aligned to the top of the reading area and staying visible as users scroll. This right-hand TOC mirrors the role of the portrait in the original hero—lighter, structured, and supportive—while the left column carries the dense textual work. When the policy is loading, a centered spinner appears in the left content area; if there’s an error, a prominent message replaces the content there, keeping user feedback in the primary visual path. Overall, the layout feels modern and disciplined, with a clear, top-down flow from title to search to content, and a right-side navigation rail that keeps the long-form policy easy to navigate.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader has no direct visual footprint but conceptually wraps the entire privacy policy content region. It controls the visibility of the policy-header, policy-toc, policy-sections-viewer, policy-search, and the status indicators. Spatially, you can think of it as encompassing the area starting just below the global site header down to the bottom of the page, within the central max-width container. When in loading or error states, it ensures that the corresponding indicators occupy the same primary content area as the sections-viewer, without shifting the header or search bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logical container only; not rendered visually. It governs the conditional rendering of the content and status components, but does not occupy explicit space in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-header forms a compact hero band at the top of the page’s main content, immediately below the global navigation. It spans the full width of the central container (up to ~1100–1200px) and is horizontally centered with 24–32px padding on the left and right. Vertically, it uses generous padding—about 64–80px at the top and 48–64px at the bottom—sitting on the dark background to echo the homepage hero. Inside, elements stack in a left-aligned vertical column that occupies roughly the left 60% of the container: at the top is a small, all-caps label such as “PRIVACY POLICY” or “LEGAL,” followed 8–12px below by a large, multi-line H1 headline that may occupy up to one-third of the visible hero height. Another 16–24px below the headline, a concise supporting paragraph explains the purpose and scope of the policy in 2–3 short lines. The right 40% of this band is mostly open space, maintaining visual balance and breathing room, with no competing imagery so that the textual introduction remains the primary focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, always fixed at the top of the policy page content but scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-search sits directly below the policy-header, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–48px to create a clear but connected band. It is centered horizontally within the main container, with the search field (and optional filter controls) spanning roughly 55–65% of the container width, leaving ample empty space on both sides to maintain a minimalist feel. The search field itself is a single horizontal bar with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside, aligned to the 8px grid. There is about 24–32px of whitespace above the field (before the edge of the header) and 32–40px below it before the two-column content area begins. On narrower screens, the search stretches closer to 90–100% of the container width, but remains centered and visually separated from both header and content. It visually reads as the primary interactive tool associated with the policy, but does not dominate the page height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow, positioned between the header and the content columns. Optionally, on taller viewports, it can become sticky within a small vertical range just above the main content so that it remains accessible when the user first begins scrolling, but it does not persist over the entire page length unless explicitly desired.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-toc occupies the right column of the main content area, beginning just below the policy-search and aligned to the top of the policy-sections-viewer. The content area beneath the search is divided into two columns: the left column takes about 60–65% of the container width for the sections-viewer, and the right column takes about 35–40% for the TOC. A vertical gutter of 24–32px separates the two columns to prevent visual crowding. The TOC panel is right-aligned within its column, with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. It displays section links in a vertical stack, each item separated by 8–16px. Section group headings (if any) have more breathing room (16–24px) above them to act as clear dividers. The top of the TOC aligns horizontally with the first visible section heading in the sections-viewer, reinforcing their relationship. On long pages, the TOC remains visible as the user scrolls, providing quick navigation through deep links to each section. On smaller screens, the TOC collapses into a toggleable panel above the sections-viewer but still appears visually below the search bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky column on desktop: within the viewport, the TOC sticks to the top padding of the main content area as the user scrolls, staying visible along the right edge while the left sections-viewer scrolls. On mobile and narrow tablets, it loses the sticky behavior and flows normally above the content or within an accordion-style drawer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-sections-viewer is the primary reading area and occupies the left column of the main content zone, starting directly beneath the policy-search and aligned vertically with the policy-toc. It spans roughly 60–65% of the container width and is left-aligned with the container’s left padding, creating a clear, strong text block. There is a 24–32px gutter separating it from the TOC on the right. Inside, each policy section is presented as a vertical stack: a section heading with strong typographic hierarchy, followed by one or more paragraphs, bullet lists, or subheadings. Section headings have 32–40px of space above them and 16–24px below, while paragraphs within a section have 8–16px spacing. The overall column is tall and scrollable, extending well beyond the initial viewport height, with the hero-style header and search scrolling out of view as users move down. Deep-link anchors are positioned so that when a user clicks a TOC item, the relevant section heading appears comfortably below the top edge of the viewport, with 64–80px of offset space for readability. On smaller screens, the sections-viewer expands to nearly 100% width, sitting below the TOC (if the TOC collapses) while preserving the same generous vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, vertically scrollable with the page. It serves as the main anchor for loading and error states, which temporarily replace its content area without affecting the header or search bands above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-error-state appears in the same visual slot as the policy-sections-viewer when an error occurs, ensuring that feedback is shown in the user’s primary reading path. It occupies the full width of the left content column (the same 60–65% width normally used by the sections-viewer) and extends downward with generous padding (32–48px) on all sides. It is centered vertically within the visible portion of that column when possible: at the moment the error appears, the message, icon, and any retry action are positioned roughly at the mid-height of the left column’s viewport, with at least 64px of empty space above and below for clarity. The right-hand policy-toc can either be hidden or replaced with a brief notice indicating that sections are unavailable, but it remains in its own column, maintaining the two-column structure. The error’s headline text is noticeably larger than body text and aligns left, with a short explanation beneath and a button or link to retry prominently placed with 16–24px spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the sections-viewer’s content. It scrolls with the page but is intended to appear in the initial viewport where the user expects to see the policy text, without shifting the header or search components above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-loading-indicator is displayed while the privacy policy content is being fetched, occupying the same general area as the policy-sections-viewer. It is centered within the left content column, both horizontally and, as much as possible, vertically within the initial viewport. The indicator—such as a spinner or pulsing skeleton lines—sits within a container that matches the width of the sections-viewer (about 60–65% of the container width) and uses generous padding (32–48px) so it does not feel cramped. Above it, the policy-header and policy-search remain visible and fully spaced, so users see that loading pertains specifically to the policy content area. The right-hand policy-toc area may show a skeleton list aligned with the loading state or remain empty, but maintains its width to preserve the familiar two-column layout—even in loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered overlay within the main content area, replacing the sections-viewer content while data is loading. It scrolls with the page but is intended to be fully visible without scrolling on typical desktop viewports when it first appears.
